review:
Counter-Strike is where it all started. Simple, raw, and purely skill-based. No fancy mechanics, no distractions — just aim, movement, map knowledge, and teamwork.

Every round feels intense, and even small plays can make a huge difference. The game rewards practice and experience more than almost any other shooter.

Even after all these years, Counter-Strike is still fun and incredibly competitive. A true classic that defined FPS games and will always have a special place in gaming history.

A classic. I'm in the younger generation of players for this game, my dad introduced me to it when I was five. Almost 20 years later and I've moved out and am studying at university about 150km away from my parents. I get to hang out with my dad through this game, which I think is pretty cool :)

Additionally, the game is good fun all around with it's many different gamemode servers. You have the classics like surf, gungame, zombie, kreedz and deathrun. I recently revisited the game and aside from the nostalgia blast of all the old maps, found out theres a warcraft gamemode too!! There's a corner for basically everyone in this game.

Counter-Strike 1.6 is more than just a game - it’s a memory, a feeling, a whole era. About 15 years ago, my friends and I used to gather in small internet cafes after school, packing into tight rooms full of noisy PCs, shouting across the tables, and connecting through a local network just to battle it out in cs. No fancy matchmaking, no skins, no battle passes - just pure competition and endless fun

What makes cs truly special is that time hasn’t killed it. Even after all these years, it remains a classic that refuses to fade away. The gameplay is simple, fast, and incredibly skill-based. Every round still feels tense, every clutch still makes your heart race and every headshot still feels earned. Modern shooters may look better, but very few feel this honest and raw

Even today, the community is alive. There are tons of servers running modes like war3ft, deathrun and Surf - and they are still insanely fun. Jumping into these servers instantly brings back that old-school vibe and a wave of nostalgia that modern games just can’t replicate. cs isn’t just remembered - it’s still played, still loved and still awesome. A true timeless legend
good old classic game you can make friends easily and having fun playing different old mods

The Half-Life mod that changed the online FPS world forever.

It's actually a light tactical shooter where one team plays the terrorists with the objective to plant the bomb, and the other team the counter-terrorists with the objective to defuse the bomb, both teams having a plethora of weapons and gadgets on dispose.

A simple concept, but one that stood the test of time, as we can see in the refreshed versions of this game.

The OG C-S has a certain feel to it that is unique and not trully replicated again (although "Condition Zero" is very similar to it), I like how some weapons feel, the movement and everything.

It is still played and populated today, so you're free to check out some community servers, or make your own one and invite some friends over.

A classic "evergreen" title that also marked the gradual shift towards more realism-oriented shooters, opposed to purely fantasy/Sci-Fi arcade ones (which I also enjoy, but they're a different experience).

I genuinely cannot stand Counter-Strike (2000). I don’t care how “legendary” people say it is - being old and influential doesn’t magically make it good.

This game feels like a museum exhibit that someone forgot to retire. The movement is clunky, the gunplay feels like it was designed to punish you personally, and the recoil system is less “skill-based” and more “memorize this invisible nonsense or suffer.” It’s not intuitive. It’s not welcoming. It’s not fun.

The graphics look like they were assembled out of leftover cardboard, the animations are stiff, and the sound design is so flat it might as well be placeholder audio that accidentally shipped. Every match feels less like tactical depth and more like getting instantly deleted by someone who has been practicing the same two maps for the last 20 years.

There’s no meaningful onboarding, no quality-of-life features, and absolutely no forgiveness for new players. You don’t “learn” the game - you endure it. And if you’re not already part of the nostalgia club, you’re just cannon fodder for veterans who treat public lobbies like their personal highlight reel.

People call it hardcore. I call it outdated and painfully overrated.

Respect its historical impact if you want.
Playing it in 2026? Miserable.

CS 1.6: My Childhood. My Roots. 🕊️
Most kids grew up with toys; I grew up with a laptop. My mom gave me my first one when I was only 1 year old. By the time I was 4 or 5, I was already actively playing CS 1.6.
I wasn’t chasing headshots or trying to be a pro. I was just playing. I lived in those maps—the echoes of de_dust2, the tension of cs_mansion, and the classic radio commands. It was the simplest, purest part of my childhood.
I’m 16 now and finally here on Steam, but 1.6 will always be where it all started.
Thank you, 1.6, for the memories. A true legend never dies. ❤️

This game is publicly "dead," as the kids say. However, if you play with large groups of friends and/or are willing to find the right online communities, it's a very fun time. Compared to TF2, which I'm sure is what people are checking reviews to see, it's functionally way jankier, has an infinitely higher skill curve, and is far less customisable. It also has a community of approximately 20 people, 4 gnats, and a ball of lint. Despite this, it has a great variety of potential playstyles and game modes (both official and fan-made) going for it despite what may appear at first glance to be barely anything. A fun game for people who want to find a new niche community to bond over a dorky interest with. I'd say the gameplay itself is more serious and competitive than a TF2 match with friends while the community is more goofy and closely connected than TF2's.

Team Fortress Classic is a pivotal piece of gaming history whose influence far exceeds its modern playability. As the foundation for the class-based shooter genre, its DNA is visible everywhere today. Yet, returning to it now reveals a game that is equal parts charming, raw, and frustratingly dated.

Strengths: Pure, Archetypal Design
Long before "hero shooters," TFC established the iconic class roster—Scout, Soldier, Heavy, Medic, and others—that remains a blueprint. Each role is distinct and mechanically pure. The exhilaration of the Scout’s speed, the skill-ceiling of rocket jumping, and the mind-games of the Spy still shine. Its simplicity is a virtue: objectives are clear, combat is swift, and there’s a refreshing lack of modern clutter like unlocks or microtransactions. It’s pure, skill-based gameplay.

Weaknesses: Dated and Unforgiving
However, that simplicity feels archaic today. Movement is fast but lacks polish, weapon feedback is weak, and the GoldSrc engine shows its age in blocky visuals and sparse audio. Map design, while iconic, often relies on choke points and narrow corridors, leading to defensive stalemates—especially against stacked Engineer sentries. Balance feels harsh and inconsistent by modern standards, with certain classes (Heavy, Sniper) easily tipping matches depending on context. The game demands coordination, yet public play often devolves into chaotic or stagnant bouts.

Verdict
Team Fortress Classic is an essential historical artifact that genre fans should experience. Its core class design is brilliant and influential. However, as a modern multiplayer experience, it’s held back by dated mechanics, technical limitations, and uneven pacing. The score of 6/10 reflects this divide: immense respect for its legacy, but a clear-eyed acknowledgment that it’s more a museum piece than a living game for most players.

The original Day of Defeat is still gold.

Even after all these years, it holds up remarkably well. There’s something pure about its design — no unnecessary mechanics, no overcomplicated systems, just tight WWII multiplayer built around teamwork and positioning. It feels focused and intentional in a way that many modern shooters don’t.

As someone who enjoys historical war games, the original Day of Defeat hits that perfect balance between realism and playability. Weapons feel impactful, movement is deliberate, and each class has a clear purpose. You can’t just sprint around mindlessly; you have to think, hold angles, coordinate with teammates, and respect the lethality of the battlefield.

The maps are iconic, too. They’re compact, well-structured, and built around objective control rather than chaotic kill-chasing. That design philosophy creates tense, memorable matches where every push and defensive stand feels meaningful.

Of course, it’s an older title. Visually and technically, it reflects its era. The player base is smaller than it once was. But none of that takes away from what made it special — and still makes it fun.

Day of Defeat (DoD) is a solid old skool squad Shooter that is probably on its way out at no fault of its own. It is still semi-active and has a fairly strong community of dedicated long time players. There is also bots and they are good. I feel bad for putting this game one on the way side. Placed in exchange for more modern shooters emerging at the time. Mainly leaving for online competitive leader boards. Day of Defeat was a very competitive shooter in its time and it still holds it titles up today. You don't lose your championship belt when you retire. No different than what R6 or CS is today. We aim trained, we practiced, we farmed. It was the start of what became hardcore titles. I remember back in 2020 a group of friends and I where playing.  Many games were getting remade around this time and the hard core genre was being defined. We talked about why DoD was not getting any meaningful updates or even a full remake, like the other games of the time. The conclusion we came to was that Day of Defeat does not need a remake. "Don't fix what aint broke" There is nothing wrong with this classic shooter, not even today. So do your self a favor. Put down the competitive shooter of 2025 and have some fun instead. Todays shooter will be as dead as Day of Defeat in ten years anyway. Take a moment to enjoy what you have while its there. Because before you know it, the fun has passed while you were too busy taking things seriously and you end up missing all the actual fun to be had. Please don't make the same mistake I did. Play the games you love to play, not just what everyone else is playing. Because eventually there will be no more players and no more servers for the game you actually enjoy. Instead you wasted that chance trying to force your self to stay in the competitive zone. Grab some buds and a couple copies of the game on sale. "This is where the fun begins!" To the guys who stayed with Day of Defeat I say this: You hold Solace in knowing you made the right choice. I commend you for your dedication. "hold the line."

Deathmatch Classic is a multiplayer shooter released by Valve as a mod for Half-Life, taking strong influence from older arena shooters. The visuals are clearly dated and reflect the style of games from the late 1990s, which may not appeal to players used to modern graphics. Gameplay is very direct, with fast movement and constant action. The sound effects do their job but do not stand out in any special way.

The game is easy to start and understand, but learning advanced movement and combat techniques can take time, especially for new players. There is no story or single-player focus, as everything is built around online matches. One advantage is the low system requirement, allowing the game to run smoothly on older machines. However, the small online community can make it difficult to find active matches.

In conclusion, Deathmatch Classic remains a clear example of early online shooter design. Its old visuals and limited player base are clear weaknesses, but the core gameplay still reflects the roots of the genre.

Half-Life: Opposing Force — вышла 1 ноября 1999 года.

I have no clue what you people are on about. I've heard some crazy glaze for this game, even people saying its their favorite Half Life game, but this game is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. I was very let down with this after playing the original and hearing all the praise this expansion gets. I wasn't even expecting something of the same quality and depth, but I was still let down. 

The level design is awful, with the intended path of progression often feeling very poorly conveyed. There were several times when parts of a level threw me off since they looked to serve some importance but actually had nothing to do with the intended route. There are times when it basically copies stories beats from Half Life 1, but they're just so much worse (ex. pit worms nest is like a great value version of blast pit). Enemy placement feels all out of whack, and its just overall a huge downgrade from hl1. Enemies feel way more spongey, and fights in general feel way less balanced. I feel like I got ganked way more in this game, and it always felt unfair. Unfair is a pretty apt word to describe the difficulty in opposing force. Weapons feel way less accurate, its like cs2 weapon spray the way my ♥♥♥♥ fires. The final boss was lame as hell, and it makes the Nihilanth look good by comparison. There are less weapons with alt fires like in hl1. Also this game is too dark, and the night vision is horrendously hard to look at. 

I didn't expect the stand alone expansion to, what I would consider a masterpiece, to also be a masterpiece, but I expected it to at least be good. Half Life Opposing Force isn't even that. Its amateur, unfair, and really boring. The only compliments I can muster are that the base concept isn't half bad, interacting with the g man and seeing freeman head to xen was cool, and the alien grappling hook is pretty neat. Besides that, I got nothing.

No intenta reemplazar al juego original, sino complementarlo con una identidad propia: armas nuevas, situaciones diferentes y la perspectiva de alguien que normalmente estaría del otro lado del conflicto. Ese cambio de rol le da frescura inmediata a la experiencia. Lo que más destaca es cómo mantiene el ritmo. Alterna combates intensos con momentos de exploración y pequeñas decisiones tácticas gracias a los aliados, lo que evita que todo se reduzca a disparar sin pensar. Aun así, se notan ciertas limitaciones de la época, como comportamientos irregulares de la IA o algunos tramos menos inspirados, pero nunca lo suficiente como para romper la inmersión. Al finalizar, queda la sensación de haber vivido un capítulo paralelo que enriquece el universo general. No es solo “más contenido”, sino una expansión que entiende lo que hacía especial al original y construye sobre esa base. Para un juego de su tiempo, sigue siendo sorprendentemente sólido y memorable.

This game, much like Team Fortress and Counter Strike, began as a Half-Life mod before becoming a standalone release. The developers improved the textures, mechanics, and level design compared to the original. The graphics are simple by today’s standards, but the maps are built for fast movement and competitive matches. The game focuses entirely on multiplayer, so there is no story or single-player mode.

The gameplay is easy to pick up but takes time to master. Players need to move quickly between platforms and pay attention to opponents. Controlling the map and timing attacks are important. The basics are simple, but learning advanced movement and strategies takes practice.

Overall, this game is a good example of classic arena shooters. The graphics are outdated, and the online community is smaller now, but the gameplay still works well.

Tips for new fans:

1) Don't forget about the tutorial (Training Room). I managed to miss it my first playthrough and didn't learn about some mechanics until I played Half-Life 2.

2) Take it slow. This game (and all the other Half-Lifes) greatly benefits if you take the time to explore and take in the many details of the environment.

3) You might want to turn the music volume up/SFX volume down? For me the default settings were so quiet I could barely hear the incredibly atmospheric soundtrack this game has.

4) Get ready for some 1998 jank. If you aren't used to games from this time period and/or don't have the patience for some frustrating moments, you might want to try Black Mesa (the fan remake) instead. I highly recommend playing both either way, and believe it or not this jank does give Half-Life a unique charm (although it might be easier to appreciate once you have more experience with the franchise).

5) You can get this and Half-Life 2 for under $5 on sale. I'm not kidding, this deal is so good it will make any other game over $10 seem not worth it.

Enjoy :). This series is hands-down my favorite gaming experience of all time, even one of my favorite stories of all time, and I only first played it barely a year ago - so know that all the praise for this game isn't just nostalgia.

Even years later, Half-Life shows how powerful good gameplay and storytelling can be. If you care about gaming history or just want to experience a legendary FPS, this is a must-play.

I admit that it took me a while to start liking this game and i considered dropping it a handful of times, i felt frustrated, bored and extremely lost, but for many years i've heard how genius and masterfully crafted this game was, so i decided to push through and i definetely don't regret it now! Once i got around chapter 6 or 7 i was more used to the movement, mechanics and atmosphere, which made me really start appreciating the game more, i was actually interested in exploring Black Mesa and learning exactly what was going on. It's frankly quite hard to start because i have a lot to say, this game is beyond impressive for it's time and aged like the finest of wines, i felt so damn immersed like i never been before, the story telling here is insane and definitely my favorite part! The gameplay doesn't fall short either, it's a very welcoming shooter with an amazing variety of guns that only get better with time, and i like how scarce the resources can be, you need to manage your ammo and health pretty wisely if you want to be able to get through enemies. My playthrough lasted about 13 hours and it was such a complete package, all the 20 chapters (tutorial included, which was really good for teaching me how to play) offer a really interesting setting filled with memorable set pieces with really in depth exploration, open areas for larger waves of enemies, some neat little puzzles, and a really natural storytelling, based less on telling and more on showing, the story unwraps itself around you in a really natural way, you start getting so immersed in the story in a way that is really hard to see elsewhere, at first i was quite scared of the ambience and enemies  but as i played i started getting used to defeating them, and i guess Morgan did as well, Freeman becomes a well known name due to everything he manages to pull off, soldiers fear him and Black Mesa employees entrust their hope on him, hoping he will be able to do something about this mess, it's just ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ awesome. Like i mentioned in my introduction i didn't really like the first 1/3 of the game and was quite confused as to why it was held in such high regard, even thinking it was just an overrated game, but im sure that was just skill issue and replaying the game would make me appreciate those chapters too! I noticed people also aren't very fond of the later chapters set in Xen, but i didn't really mind them, i thought they were pretty in line with the rest of the game while still being a nice switch up, but im definitely biased to anything involving Aliens so maybe don't take my word for it. Overall, Half-Life is a masterpiece that very quickly became one of my favorite games ever, i barely finished and im already craving more of it, and if you like videogames i think you should definitely give it a chance, maybe G-Man could even hire you in the end!
Even after nearly 30 years, this game is still incredibly enjoyable. Amazing graphics tho :), my gpu struggled to maintain stable FPS (!). Overall a 8.5/10

One of the most important games ever made. It's impressive how well this aged. Some of the funny little quirks like the wide ladders and ridiculous abstract geometry add a lot of unwanted comedy nowadays where we're used to more grounded settings, but it adds a charm that doesn't detract from it for me. 

The balancing however hasn't aged super well in my eyes. Especially on hard, health and armor are so rare that you're often just constantly limping around at 20 HP unless you manage to dodge pretty much every attack. I think it's great that this mode is very punishing and pushes you towards using your whole arsenal, but in practise it just makes the guns obsolete and forces you to use explosive for pretty much every encounter. And since you can hold 10 frag grenades and 10 smg grenades, you never run out of those anyway. HL2 rebalances the explosives so you can only hold 5 frag grenades and 3 smg grenades and removes tripmines and remote charges, which makes them much more situational. As someone who played HL2 first, this was quite the change for me since I wouldn't use grenades on mid-tier enemies, but in HL1 you absolutely have to if you don't want to trade damage. Enemy AI and your guns are balanced in a way that feels like you have to trade 50HP to kill a single grunt or you have to use a well placed grenade. It's just a different type of gameplay, but I personally don't like it when taking damage is pretty much unavoidable, since I think it should feel more like a punishment for not paying attention, rather than something you just constantly get even if you play well. Add onto that the extremely rare health and energy refills compared to HL2 and this creates a really unforgiving experience that's kinda cool and unique, but I think a few more refills here and there before big fights wouldn't have hurt that experience. 

Energy in general feels like a point less. How exactly it absorbs health is kinda ambiguous and since picking up either health or energy isn't a choice you do in the game, the two just feel like the same thing. I may have been more interesting if the chargeup stations allowed you to pick if you wanted to refill either health or energy. That's something HL2 doesn't really fix either, but it at least gives you much more energy so you feel its absorption much more. Doom Eternal does this well where health and armor are created differently in fights and armor acts as a 100% health shield until it's depleted. That way in fights they're the same, but your method of obtaining them is different, creating a more interesting gameplay loop

But that's really my only gripe with the game, which shows how ahead of its time it really was

Valve did it! They made one of the all-time best single-player shooter in 1998. The core gameplay loop in Half-Life is simple: get some guns, run, shoot, jump. No cutscenes or fancy RPG elements. Game is entertaining, consistent and constantly surprising. The enemy soldiers you fight in Half-Life are tough. These guys actually yell stuff relevant to the combat, such as "Fire in the Hole!" or "Look out!" as the grenades go flying. Then they actually try to get away from your grenades. And what's more, they also throw grenades back at you. The enemies actually cooperate to try to take you down.

Half-Life does away with the concept of game levels and instead the world feels like a continuous whole, with small pauses and loads when you traverse. The environments are highly varied: there are labs and warehouses, monorail stations and gun bunkers, missile silos and parking garages. Most of the game takes place in an underground military base called the Black Mesa Facility, but it is not confined there. You get to see so much more during your journey. The ambience is excellent.

The game is not perfect though: ladders feel awkward to use for instance. But that is a minor problem. The character of Gordon Freeman also is flat and dry. He lacks personality and never says anything in the game. That leaves a little to be desired. Still, you could make a case that giving him a personality would actually detract from your ability to identify and relate with the character.

My biggest problem with Half-Life was some of the level design, particularly in the middle stages of the game. Here the tension seriously sags with tedious jumping puzzles and instant death traps. Towards the later part of the game I did however really enjoy the chapter "Surface Tension".

Anyway, those are some of my thoughts. This brilliant game reworks typical genre elements and presents you with variety, surprises, and new challenges to keep you hooked. This is classic single-player FPS at it's finest. 9/10

Counter-Strike: Condition Zero is a classic entry in the Counter-Strike series and a great experience for fans of tactical shooters. It keeps the core gameplay that made Counter-Strike famous while adding single-player content and missions that make it feel fresh and unique.

The gunplay is precise and satisfying, rewarding good aim, positioning, and teamwork. The missions in the Tour of Duty mode add an interesting twist, giving players objectives to complete while facing increasingly difficult bots.

The maps are iconic and timeless, many of which are still loved by the Counter-Strike community today. Whether you’re practicing against bots or jumping into multiplayer, the gameplay still feels solid and competitive.

Developed with contributions from Valve, this title remains an important piece of Counter-Strike history.

For fans of classic FPS games or anyone who enjoys the roots of competitive shooters, Counter-Strike: Condition Zero is definitely worth playing.

Condition Zero is an underappreciated gem that unfortunately got overshadowed by its bigger brother, CSS. like month or two later.

People often dunk on Condition Zero for being just a reskin of CS 1.6. They may be right at one point, but
people often overlook that CZ brought things that may not look like much, though given the amounts of small improvements combined together, it's quite lots.

but people either praise 1.6 for CZ's features or they never really even played CZ to experience it.

For example, maps and models in CZ have so much better visual clarity. I mean, sure, some maps look completely different, straying from the original, but they do help you differentiate between CT and T Models quite well... especially in darker corners of maps...
1.6 and CSS had tons of dark and damp maps where not even night vision did the work for you most of the time. CZ solves all that, its actually something that GO/CS2 attempted to fix also, to provide better visual clarity.

CZ brought other things to the table, though, like bot support. We all know bots, right? People often mistake 1.6 for having bot support, but not really, they came with CZ.
Later they got backported to 1.6 in *cough* pirated versions *cough*.

Also, general stability, ping, it all feels much smoother. Some 1.6 veterans may see it as a minus, but CZ does feel friendlier when it comes to actually hitting your targets. (ghost hits) I think that's a plus for a wider variety of players.

essentially CS:CZ is kind of like cs 1.7, it expanded on old with new, really harming anything from the prior

This game could have become the foundation for a story-driven Counter-Strike series, but at the time Valve chose a different path - a pure 5v5 competitive shooter with no narrative. Maybe that was the right decision. Still, if you are curious about what a completely different CS could have looked like, Condition Zero is worth trying.

There is no real story to talk about. You simply play as different special forces units around the world and clear missions in a very Rambo-like fashion.

A special mention goes to the series of challenge missions where you are given objectives for the entire game and must complete them in 5v5 matches. This mode is pure torture and slowly destroys your morale. On the highest difficulty, it feels like a direct attack on your sanity.

The bots give you no time to think, no room to breathe, not even a second to react. Condition Zero can easily be nominated as one of the hardest games ever made. If you have been looking for a truly brutal challenge, this game is for you.

★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ☆ ☆ ☆ ☆ ☆ (5/10)

So, Condition Zero in execution is: CS 1.6 "improved in aspects" with graphics, modeling, some weapon animations, some Condition Zero maps being at night (something you didn't see in CS 1.6)
The gameplay itself is the same as CS 1.6
And of course, we can't highlight the single-player campaign, those who like single-player campaigns may have a tiny chance of enjoying the Condition Zero campaign, as it's monotonous and tedious!
This Condition Zero is perfect for those who play the campaign and especially the online mode, which is what most people prefer.

In short, it's a cool CS, you can easily have fun online!
I recommend it as much as CS 1.6, although it deserved a little more attention from CS fans.

*Counter-Strike: Condition Zero* feels like classic CS with a simple single-player twist. The bots and missions are fun for practice, but it doesn’t hit the same intensity as multiplayer. Still, I liked it as a chill way to play CS without the pressure.

Counter-Strike: Condition Zero is a classic entry in the Counter-Strike series and a great experience for fans of tactical shooters. It keeps the core gameplay that made Counter-Strike famous while adding single-player content and missions that make it feel fresh and unique.

The gunplay is precise and satisfying, rewarding good aim, positioning, and teamwork. The missions in the Tour of Duty mode add an interesting twist, giving players objectives to complete while facing increasingly difficult bots.

The maps are iconic and timeless, many of which are still loved by the Counter-Strike community today. Whether you’re practicing against bots or jumping into multiplayer, the gameplay still feels solid and competitive.

Developed with contributions from Valve, this title remains an important piece of Counter-Strike history.

For fans of classic FPS games or anyone who enjoys the roots of competitive shooters, Counter-Strike: Condition Zero is definitely worth playing.

Joc seria Counter-Strike încă de la primul titlu lansat și, chiar dacă acum joc pe acest cont, experiența mea se întinde pe zeci de ani. Condition Zero este o piesă importantă din istoria CS, fiind jocul care a încercat să aducă ceva diferit printr-o campanie single-player, păstrând în același timp spiritul competitiv care ne-a făcut pe toți fani.

De ce merită jucat chiar și acum:

Nostalgie Pură: Este o călătorie în timp către vremurile în care tactica simplă și skill-ul brut erau tot ce conta.

Campania Single-player: "Deleted Scenes" oferă o perspectivă interesantă și un mod de joc diferit față de restul seriei, fiind perfect pentru momentele când vrei să joci solo.

Mecanici Clasice: Se simte familiar, rapid și precis, fiind puntea de legătură perfectă între CS 1.6 și Source.

Concluzie: Deși seria a evoluat enorm, Condition Zero rămâne un titlu pe care orice fan adevărat al francizei trebuie să-l aibă în bibliotecă. Este o dovadă a modului în care a început totul și o experiență care încă mai are farmec în 2026.

underrated as hell but seriously has the most broken ending and beginning of all the three hl trilogy titles.

it's really unfortunate that valve never updated these to fix the things hl1 received. a big example of this is it toggling mouse acceleration, but an even bigger one is the sheer amount of texture clipping that occurs. I loved the lore elements, but again, for whatever reason, it's somehow better that these games aren't canon, which sours all of the cool stuff done here. More unfortunately, when i got to the end, i instinctively hit the save hotkey, and ended up having my game crash every single time I did, resetting all my settings, and BREAKING MY ABILITY TO LAUNCH IT WITH STEAM CLOUD ENABLED.

If you like hl1, please play the gearbox games, however, i do recommend remembering the console commands:
fps_override 0 (do this when something physics based breaks)
and 
fps_override 1 (to revert)
also, if at the beginning of the game you get the scientist texture clip bug that sort of softlocks the game, set your max fps to a lower number with fps_max. (i know 24 works, but likely much higher ones will work too, i just love going from 24 to 540 to shock my eyes every couple minutes.

Anyways, great game, has equally wasted potential as opfor, and is genuinely more replayable than i thought.

I am playing every hl title back to back in anticipation of hlx being actually released soon, if that doesn't happen, i will end my life.

Half-Life: Blue Shift was released on June 12, 2001.
It's 287MB in size, but it's 5 hours of fun. There's a bit of overuse, with headcrab attacks that instantly appear from around corners, dark locations, and confusing, dark burrows. And then I saw that Gearbox did it... They know how to screw things up when you least expect it.
But I'm still glad I played it. This game features "forgotten ancient technologies," such as puzzles that rely on object physics and spatial positioning.

Half-Life: Blue Shift offers another perspective on the events of the original Half-Life, this time placing the player in the role of Barney Calhoun, one of the security guards at the Black Mesa facility. Caught in the aftermath of Gordon Freeman’s disastrous experiment, Barney must find a way to escape the complex alive.

From Barney’s point of view, players can occasionally glimpse Gordon Freeman following his own storyline from the main game, while also encountering the famous mysterious figure known as the G-man.

This entry in the series is noticeably shorter, and when compared to Half-Life: Opposing Force, which featured Adrian Shephard as its protagonist, Blue Shift does not stand out through added complexity over the base game.

Instead, this perspective acts as a narrative bridge between the cause of the disaster and its resolution, with Barney pursuing a far simpler goal: survival and avoiding a tragic fate.

and

Anyways, great game, has equally wasted potential as opfor, and is genuinely more replayable than i thought.

I am playing every hl title back to back in anticipation of hlx being actually released soon, if that doesn't happen, i will end my life.

22 years later, I finally catch up with the Half-Life franchise, and boy it was great. If you own Steam and haven't played this game and its predecessors, what are you doing? You only live once, so please play this series! It's a once in a lifetime experience you won't regret.

Half-Life 2 is still an incredible experience, even today. The atmosphere, storytelling, and world-building are on another level and pull you in from the first minutes. Everything feels connected and immersive without relying on cutscenes.

The physics, gunplay, and level design were way ahead of their time and still feel fun and creative. Every section brings something new, keeping the game fresh from start to finish.

Half-Life 2 proves that great game design doesn’t age. If you’ve never played it or want to revisit a masterpiece, this game is absolutely worth your time.
This game affected my life, Recently whenever im gonna make a decision i always remember G-Man saying "Prepare for unforeseen consequences" and it just makes my head think straight.

Half-Life 2 continues the legacy of its predecessor and takes both the story and the mechanics to another level. The game begins with the introduction of the iconic Gordon Freeman in City 17, a city shaped by the consequences of the Black Mesa incident.

His employer, the mysterious and iconic G-Man, is portrayed in Half-Life 2 as a character who introduces a new variable into the story - Gordon Freeman himself. Unlike in the first game, where he seemed more like an observer, here he appears to actively influence events.

Both the mechanics and the environment have been significantly improved, and by the end I found myself saying, “How cool is this gravity gun?” It feels like the world around you is designed specifically to highlight this weapon. Another standout tool, besides the gravity gun, is the pheromone grenade that guides the antlions to attack. You hate them at first, but later they become useful allies in the fight against the Combine.

From a narrative perspective, it was great to see familiar characters return from the first game. I also appreciated that the developers kept the same storytelling approach, avoiding traditional cutscenes and instead building a memorable and intriguing narrative flow through in-game interactions. Personally, I enjoy this style of storytelling that avoids cinematic interruptions. However, one aspect I considered a drawback is the fact that the character you play does not have any spoken lines. I believe that integrating some dialogue from the player character’s perspective could have added more emotional depth.

Half-Life 2 has a charm that has stood the test of time; it is incredible how, after so many years, it still feels like a strong and compelling product. Thank you, Valve, for the passion invested, and I hope the same standard remains high in the future, whether in this series or in other games.

[h1] Overview [/h1]
“about that beer I owed ya…”

Sometimes when I play games from my childhood, I get a little nervous. How much of my love for them is nostalgia? How much have they dated in terms of gameplay and visuals, potentially ruining my fond memories? 

I haven’t played Half Life 2 since around 2005, and I never played the episodes. It was one of my first purchases on Steam, but primarily served Garry’s Mod until I decided to play through it recently.

It has aged exquisitely. The gameplay is fluid, the weapons all feel fun to use, especially the classic shotgun, crowbar, and gravity gun. The story holds up incredibly well, with good writing, voice acting, character development, and a constant air of mystery surrounding everything. I really miss the era of silent protagonists where you could put yourself in their shoes. The immersion is only helped by the fact that there are few true cutscenes, instead you are free to look around and position yourself as you wish during most of the big story moments, making you feel like you are part of the events rather than just watching a movie. There were only a couple of sequences that I felt were a bit tedious (striders have way too much HP on hard IMO) but overall it was incredible to revisit this absolute gem of gaming history. 

The graphics also hold up astonishingly well. I was constantly surprised at how good this game looks. Instead of going “yup, this game is from 2004…” I found myself going “wait, this game is from 2004??” . The characters’ faces and expressions look absolutely amazing (compare to Halo 3 which came out years after this).

Half-Life 2 is full of amazing little details. Shooting a combine with the crossbow will pin him to the wall behind him. Alyx will wince and hold her hand up if you look directly at her with the flashlight on. Throwing a paint can at enemies will splatter them with white paint. Seagulls will ♥♥♥♥ on your car. Just like the original Half-Life, a ton of objects and NPCs are interactable despite having no practical gameplay or story function. 

If you played it back in the day – it’s 100% worth revisiting. If you’ve never played, even better. Go in blind and enjoy. 

My only real complaint would be there’s no Episode 3 or even Half-Life 3. Memes aside, Episode 2 ends on quite a cliffhanger and I got really down after realizing that’s just it. Would love to see more of this universe. 



[h1] Steam Deck [/h1]
I mostly played on PC, but played a couple levels and did some achievement cleanup on the Steam Deck. It runs flawlessly, easy 120FPS and it looks amazing. I tend to prefer shooters on mouse & keyboard but this plays surprisingly well with a controller. The HUD and menus also adjust themselves accordingly, and there’s a fully optional aim assist. A few minutes getting used to the buttons and I was good to go. Shouldn’t have expected any less from Valve, I guess.

[h1] Achievements [/h1]
The base game doesn’t have much in the way of big challenges, but does have collectibles in the form of supply caches throughout the campaign that can be annoying to hunt down if you’re missing a couple. 

The episodes have some more difficult ones. Episode 1 requires you to finish the campaign only firing one bullet (which is needed to progress at one point) so you can only use the gravity gun, crowbar, grenades, and rocket launcher. This sounds harder than it is. The Episode 1 campaign is quite short, and there were only a couple sequences that I really felt the challenge with this restriction, but generally creative and liberal use of the gravity gun can carry you through.

Episode 2 requires you to carry a gnome through almost the entirety of the campaign. More annoying than challenging, as I often found that I would put it down to fight enemies, forget, and continue on and have to either backtrack or reload saves if I had gone too far. During the driving sequences, the gnome will fly out of the car from the slightest turn or bump, forcing you to drive super slowly and cautiously as Alyx looks over and judges you for the tomfoolery you’ll put yourself through for a single achievement. 

Episode 2 also has the grub achievement, which requires you to kill all 333 Antlion grubs across the first 2 levels. Follow a guide and constantly check your numbers with the in-game tracker to make sure you aren’t missing any as you go. I didn’t do this and my first attempt at this achievement ended with 331/333 and I had to start the whole thing over. Don’t be like me.

i did enjoy half life one but had a lot of issues with it, mainly due to its age. i found it hard, unforgiving, not super clear, and while i liked its atmosphere it ended up feeling like it was getting bogged down by soldier points. the scientists were fun but the story was very background compared to the gun action...

half life 2 fixes literally all of the problems half life 1 has, and it does something super cool too... it changes the genre, scope, and scale. whats so exciting about half life 1 is the way it builds, starting from a normal day in the office to survival of aliens, fighting the military, destroying the core, and saving xen. but half life 2 opens with a huge scope, an impossibly incomprehensible amount of things has changed since half life 1 ended. the entire world has changed and been reborn, so what half life IS is changed and reborn. the story is front and centre now, and the world we navigate is vastly different. it's ♥♥♥♥♥♥' awesome!!!

the gunplay is SIGNIFICANTLY improved and the resources drops feel a lot more generous, which means you don't feel as backed into a corner as much as you could do in HL1. shooting guys feels particularly good and much more responsive. more importantly though is that valve in 2004 made an excellent physics engine that allows the puzzles in HL2 to be really engaging and fun, in a way where i did not necessarily feel they were in HL1! the bit where you get to throw a can at a guy is great, and the opening sequences are fun for the way the physics engine allows you to move around. the graphics are amazing for 2004, with characters emoting and expressing and gesticulating in a super realistic way. THEN you get the gravity gun. at first i was like "I know this is cool but i'm not super comfortable with it" and then it just ends up being the best thing ever. i LOVE the gravity gun. it ends up being super intuitive and obviously necessary for puzzles but also a great aid in combat as well. ravenholm as this tutorial for the gravity gun for puzzles and combat is really awesome, it just feels good to use and moreso than the Free Man's crowbar it becomes your bread and butter. then at the end of the game you lose all your guns, think you're gonna lose Mr Gravity... and it accidentally gets upgraded. that chapter is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ awesome, blowing up like 20 combine soldiers with your silly science gun and just feeling unstoppable as you go. it's really awesome. i will say i am in the crowd of Follow Freeman being the worst chapter, it's not bad necessarily but it is super overwhelming and fighting the striders is HARD as ♥♥♥♥ too, got me genuinely angry.

the coolest thing about the gameplay is playing it and seeing it as the DNA of all of valve's other biggest hits though. you play ravenholm and follow freeman you can see how half life becomes L4D, with this crew of people who can help you, heal you, and also fight against zombies in tight dark corridors and alleyways, awesome! then the gravity gun physics puzzles end up being the basis of portal as well, and thats crazy and awesome too! you can also see how it ends up creating CS:GO (from half life 1) with the tight, fast shooting with cover and quick changing between guns. half life is kind of really important in that way and its cool!

i really like what they do with the story in this one. the non-characters of HL1 become kleiner and eli here, and now there's an entire world at stake. we get alyx whose new, and breen and judith are also interesting. all of these characters, in the surprisingly small cast are interesting and unique! i like that there are no actual combine characters to speak to, because they are so alien and not at all like breen as a villain. the one small glimpse you get of his alien overlords is terrifying and intriguing. i'm hoping i'll find out more about who the ♥♥♥♥ or what the ♥♥♥♥ that was in the next episodes!

and the ending is nuts too, i was like WHAT when the credits rolled because it's so sudden! i love the way half life is able to leave so much unsaid about g-man, his employers, what breen knows about gordon, about what the combine want or do exactly, it's all just so intriguing and exciting. i wish we got to spend a bit more time in eli's hideout, and got to smell the roses there a bit more. i also wish we had a named vortigon character we could get to know. i love RPGs a bit too much and would almost like this to have RPG elements but that's also not what half life is and that's okay hahaha

[hr] [/hr]

[h1] Rise and shine, Mister Freeman... rise and shine. [/h1]

Half-Life 2 is the continuation of the original Half-Life made by VALVe, being an FPS game while also being a puzzle game. You are in the shoes of Dr. Gordon Freeman, a former Black Mesa scientist with a PhD in Theoretical Physics and an MIT graduation.
[b]Now, don't be alarmed![/b] The puzzles aren't what you probably think. They're actually very simple, so you shouldn't worry about them.

[hr] [/hr]

[h1] Why exactly do I recommend this game? [/h1]

VALVe made the game in 2004. For its time, the graphics were stunning, and hell, it was revolutionary. Introducing puzzles to an FPS game wasn't very normal at the time, and VALVe made sure Half-Life 2 could live up to its name for a long time. Even after 20 years (at the time of writing this review), the game is still insanely fun. And with the 20th Anniversary edition into the game, you can play the Half-Life 2 Episodes just with this game.

[hr] [/hr]

[h1] The Graphics [/h1]

Even today, the graphics of this game are still great. If you want to play the game for this reason, I'm of course going to question why you just want to see the graphics instead of everything else, but, still, you've done a good choice if you're going to play this game for that reason.

[hr] [/hr]

[h1] The Physics Engine [/h1]

Even to this day, the game is still very fun because it uses the janky Source engine. You can perform funny things such as prop flying or even prop climbing! What's not to lose? Me in particular, I don't see any problem with... of course, Accelerated Back Hopping at Mach 5 towards Nova Prospekt.

[hr] [/hr]

[h1] The Story [/h1]

The story is absolutely great. There will be unexpected moments happening, and so many things that you will have to deal with. If you're a person that likes lots of challenges, this game is for you, and if you don't like challenges, hell, this game is STILL also for you!

[hr] [/hr]

[b] I genuinely love this game. You'll probably enjoy it as many other people do (you probably won't finish the main Half-Life 2 campaign 15+ times like me but still), and you'll have lots of fun. [/b]

Title: Counter-Strike: Source

Summary: Classic multiplayer shooter game

Personal notes: Counter-Strike: Source is a source engine remake of the classic shooter we all know, bringing the formula into a more modern engine while keeping the same core gameplay. From a modern perspective, the formula is the same but feels very different to what we have nowadays in CS2. Source plays slower with movement that feels more rigid IMO. I also would like to point out how many more maps are available in this version, despite them being less balanced than what we have now. In addition to some of the drastic changes of maps that have remained such as train! It was quite an experience seeing how this game has aged by revisiting this capsule of time. I will say the visuals and animations clearly show their age when compared to more modern games but I feel like thats part of the charm of playing these games from the past. The player-base is also a lot smaller but community servers are still active from my experience. Achievement wise, this game has a lot more than its future itteration, with many relating to weapon kills or objectives. None are that difficult, but some are quite tedious especially the higher number ones. I would recommend using bots to attain these in a reasonable time but gives you the liberty of difficulty. In addition to this, there are a few that require clanmates/friends. Overall, Counter-Strike: Source feels like a snapshot of the series years ago. While CS2 is clearly more polished and active, Source still has some charm thanks to its simplicity and its time. It's not where I would normally play my CS but it's interesting to revisit for sure. I would recommend this only if you want an old state of Counter-Strike, if you want the latest, play CS2 for free instead. 

Time it took to complete: 27hrs

- SAR Czar (Rescue 500 hostages)
- Multiple Map Veterans (Win 100 rounds on every map)

The Day of Defeat: Source is one of those games that has simply stood the test of time for me.

I’ve been playing since before the Source version released, and I still come back to it. That alone says a lot. There’s something about its pacing, gunplay, and grounded design that continues to feel satisfying even years later.

As someone who’s a big fan of historical war games, this one hits a sweet spot. It doesn’t try to be overly flashy or exaggerated. The weapons feel weighty and deliberate, the maps are tight and focused, and teamwork actually matters. Capturing points with your team, holding choke positions, coordinating pushes — it creates a simple but effective loop that rewards awareness and positioning rather than chaotic run-and-gun gameplay.

I also appreciate its straightforward class design. Each role has a purpose, and matches often feel like they hinge on coordination rather than individual heroics. It captures a certain old-school multiplayer charm that’s harder to find in modern shooters.

Of course, being an older title, it shows its age in some areas. The player base isn’t what it once was, and visuals reflect its era. But for me, those aren’t deal-breakers. If anything, they add to the nostalgia and identity of the experience.

If you enjoy historical WWII shooters and appreciate classic Source-engine gameplay, I absolutely recommend it. It’s a reminder of a time when multiplayer shooters were simple, focused, and built around tight mechanics and community servers.

Even after all these years, I still love it — and that says everything.

Day of Defeat (DoD) is a solid old skool squad Shooter that is probably on its way out at no fault of its own. It is still semi-active and has a fairly strong community of dedicated long time players. There is also bots and they are good. I feel bad for putting this game one on the way side. Placed in exchange for more modern shooters emerging at the time. Mainly leaving for online competitive leader boards. Day of Defeat was a very competitive shooter in its time and it still holds it titles up today. You don't lose your championship belt when you retire. No different than what R6 or CS is today. We aim trained, we practiced, we farmed. It was the start of what became hardcore titles. I remember back in 2020 a group of friends and I where playing. Many games were getting remade around this time and the hard core genre was being defined. We talked about why DoD was not getting any meaningful updates or even a full remake, like the other games of the time. The conclusion we came to was that Day of Defeat does not need a remake. "Don't fix what aint broke" There is nothing wrong with this classic shooter, not even today. So do your self a favor. Put down the competitive shooter of 2025 and have some fun instead. Todays shooter will be as dead as Day of Defeat in ten years anyway. Take a moment to enjoy what you have while its there. Because before you know it, the fun has passed while you were too busy taking things seriously and you end up missing all the actual fun to be had. Please don't make the same mistake I did. Play the games you love to play, not just what everyone else is playing. Because eventually there will be no more players and no more servers for the game you actually enjoy. Instead you wasted that chance trying to force your self to stay in the competitive zone. Grab some buds and a couple copies of the game on sale. "This is where the fun begins!" To the guys who stayed with Day of Defeat I say this: You hold Solace in knowing you made the right choice. I commend you for your dedication. "hold the line."

Day Of Defeat is I assume Valve's answer to early Call Of Duty. I think it would have sufficed back in 2005, but 20 years later, the game's systems are quite tedious, and I don't think this game really benefits from a port to the Source engine (although I will admit I never played the original)

I think Stamina and class restrictions are my main issue, this game wants to be a military simulator so bad, but can't shake it's arcade shooter roots of Half-Life. Some guns have really forgiving recoil, and some guns have to be mounted because their recoil is the most unforgiving. This mimics the weapons of then, sure, but it doesn't help that movement is super slippery, and that I don't think most people are going to playing this today seeking realism.
The maps feel like they force teams into a stalemate, which is great for the 24/7 servers of today, but not the best if you actually want a goal to achieve when playing.

The game still has that Valve touch though, the sound design is on point, soundtrack is great and the game does reap the rewards of still looking serviceable today due to being on Source. This is also one of the only multiplayer games I have played as well with this much of an active voice chat. Sure, it's probably fathers who have been playing for 20 years, but I sure do get a laugh when I play this game.

I do recommend, if you get the Valve Complete Pack, but unless that's the case, you would probably find your money better spent elsewhere, but it's still a very chill shooter to just zone out in. 7/10.

The Day of Defeat: Source is one of those games that has simply stood the test of time for me.

I’ve been playing since before the Source version released, and I still come back to it. That alone says a lot. There’s something about its pacing, gunplay, and grounded design that continues to feel satisfying even years later.

As someone who’s a big fan of historical war games, this one hits a sweet spot. It doesn’t try to be overly flashy or exaggerated. The weapons feel weighty and deliberate, the maps are tight and focused, and teamwork actually matters. Capturing points with your team, holding choke positions, coordinating pushes — it creates a simple but effective loop that rewards awareness and positioning rather than chaotic run-and-gun gameplay.

I also appreciate its straightforward class design. Each role has a purpose, and matches often feel like they hinge on coordination rather than individual heroics. It captures a certain old-school multiplayer charm that’s harder to find in modern shooters.

Of course, being an older title, it shows its age in some areas. The player base isn’t what it once was, and visuals reflect its era. But for me, those aren’t deal-breakers. If anything, they add to the nostalgia and identity of the experience.

If you enjoy historical WWII shooters and appreciate classic Source-engine gameplay, I absolutely recommend it. It’s a reminder of a time when multiplayer shooters were simple, focused, and built around tight mechanics and community servers.

Even after all these years, I still love it — and that says everything.

Half-Life 2: Deathmatch wasn't just a game for me. It was my introduction to the "online" world. It was the game that taught me how "just one more round" could accidentally turn into 5:00 AM, realizing the sun was coming up and I should have been in bed hours ago.

The memories I have of this game are pure, high-speed insanity. It was non-stop run-and-gun fun where the physics engine was the star of the show. One minute you’re launched across the room by a stray explosion, the next you’re successfully "decapitating" someone with a wooden chair launched from your Gravity Gun. And nothing, absolutely nothing hit quite like the sound of a steaming hot crossbow bolt pinning you to a wall.

I tried booting it up again recently, but it just didn’t hit the same. It’s not that the game is bad. It’s that as an adult, the "time tax" feels heavier. Looking at my massive backlog of unplayed titles, I found myself thinking, "I should probably be playing something else." My time is limited now, and the frantic, twitchy energy required for HL2DM feels like a young man's game. To me at least. 

I’ve decided that I likely won't ever boot this up again. Not just to save time or brain cells, but because I want to preserve the version of the game that lives in my head. I want to remember it as the peak of online chaos, not as a ghost town of empty servers or a reminder that my reflexes aren't what they used to be.

Primary Attack - LMB
Secondary Attack - RMB
R - reload
E - interact
G - gravity gun
F - flashlight
Z - suit zoom
1-6 - change weapon
Mouse wheel - change weapon
Q - last weapon

Мне 58 лет, я отец, наверное, один из самых старших игроков в эту игру. Я отец-одиночка, воспитываю сына, которому сейчас 14 лет. Сын получил эту игру на Рождество 2021 года от своего дяди, мы установили её на его компьютер, и он начал играть. К концу недели он наиграл 40 часов. Для меня это было ужасно, так как мне и так было трудно найти время, чтобы провести его с сыном, ведь он постоянно гуляет с друзьями или смотрит на YouTube, как украинские мужчины целуются по-французски. Поэтому я решил создать аккаунт в Steam и купить эту игру, чтобы посмотреть, смогу ли я играть вместе с ним. Я зашёл в игру, выбрал персонажа и мир и начал играть, но застрял на каком-то моменте. Я попросил сына о помощи, и он создал для меня игру. Мне очень понравилось, это было лучшее время, которое я провёл с сыном после смерти моей жены. С тех пор эта игра снова сблизила меня и моего сына, и теперь мы действительно проводим время вместе вне дома. Эта игра напомнила мне, что во всем можно найти что-то интересное, и она подарила мне и моему сыну много счастливых воспоминаний.

Perfect puzzle platformer from Valve using the revolutionary portal gun. As expected, I had no performance issues on my 4080 at 4K. This game is ancient now so easily hit my monitors refresh cap of 240.

Unlike what people on Steam, such as Ko-Duke would say, who is on a mission to defame Valve... on Steam(?), Portal is actually a really cool game.

Portal 1 at times feels like a tech demo to show what the Source Engine is capable of. However, once you get behind the concept of Portals, you get to notice how fun it is to speedrun it a bit. And that tech demo feeling, goes away very quickly, when you realize that there's an entire story to Portal.
If you want more, there's Portal 2 and other Portal mods. Aside from the obvious mods available on Steam if you own Portal and Portal 2, you can also get Portal sourcemods, ranging from just more puzzles, to outright horror (such as the ERROR mod)

all in all, 10/10 would recommend.

'Twas a fun, short, simple, yet still occasionally challenging game. Honestly, I believe that the majority of what made this game so great was the excellent pairing of story and gameplay progression, where you could learn the story through important locations brimming with lore after having mastered the game mechanics that were carefully taught to you, one level at a time. Challenge builds as you progress, but so do the rewards in the form of important story progression. The entire game's progression, thus, feels quite natural and near perfect for leading the player to overcoming all the challenges within. Also, for being a short game, it has quite a bit of character and some awesome puzzles. Please consider playing this game and its sequel! (I actually haven't finished the sequel, yet, but already know that I like it a lot from the amount that I've already played.)

The oldest of the two still keeps its shine after nearly 20 years.

Portal 1 is great in gameplay, especially for a 2007 game, with interesting gameplay and a fairly nice twist at the end. The puzzles, while simple, maximise every aspect for the limited features. 

Portal 1 also gives a nice lab feeling (but also kinda looks like an insane asylum) with good graphics and clear ambiguity to "Hey! This here, is a lab."

It also does well for a sort-of alternate reality Half-Life (or whatever you'd call it), where it plays off the "whoop whoop, everyone is dead lol" from the original, but sets a different, yet connected story.

Conclusion: Portal 2 MIGHT be better but this does the trick.

It feels kinda dumb reviewing a game that’s been out for 19 years, but honestly, this game is a breath of fresh air compared to the absolute mess that modern PvP shooters have become. Most shooters these days are sweaty 5v5 or 6v6 grindfests filled with the most chronically online people you’ve ever seen.

TF2? Nah. TF2 throws 10–16 idiots on one team against 10–16 morons on the other. Four of them are playing the same class, but somehow they’re all doing completely different nonsense.

In modern shooters, you die because someone’s crouch-spamming like their life depends on it, or because they picked the hard counter to your character. In TF2, you die because some guy rocket-jumped across the entire map in five seconds — using a launcher that barely even does damage — just to smack you once with a shovel before immediately getting headshot by a completely out-of-position sniper holding what is essentially a jar-powered rifle. And that sniper? He’s currently about to get backstabbed by someone who’s been staring at him for 30 seconds.

More shooters need this level of chaos. This beautiful, unfiltered stupidity is what makes the game so good.
The game is, of course, free. There is no excuse for it to not be in your library already. It is great fun, but nobody cares about my opinion, so find out for yourself.

Team Fortress 2, developed and published by Valve, stands as one of the most iconic and enduring multiplayer shooters in gaming history. Originally released in 2007 as part of The Orange Box, it quickly distinguished itself from other first-person shooters of its era through its bold art direction, sharply defined class roles, and a tone that blended competitive intensity with irreverent humor. Nearly two decades later, it remains a staple on Steam, a testament to the strength of its core design and the loyalty of its community.

At the center of Team Fortress 2’s identity are its nine distinct classes, each carefully crafted to fulfill a specific battlefield role. From the nimble Scout darting through enemy lines to the resilient Heavy anchoring defensive positions, every class feels mechanically unique and strategically important. The synergy between these roles defines the game’s flow. Success depends less on individual reflexes alone and more on how well a team coordinates offense, defense, and support. Medics enable pushes through timely healing and ÜberCharges, Engineers fortify objectives with sentry guns and teleporters, and Spies disrupt enemy formations with stealth and deception. This dynamic interplay ensures that matches remain varied and tactically rich.

The visual style plays a crucial role in the game’s longevity. Rather than pursuing photorealism, Team Fortress 2 embraces a stylized, cartoon-inspired aesthetic influenced by mid-century illustration. Exaggerated silhouettes, expressive animations, and vibrant color palettes not only give the game personality but also enhance readability during chaotic firefights. Even years after release, its art direction feels timeless. Complementing the visuals are memorable voice lines and character personalities that give each class distinct charm. The game’s humor—both subtle and overt—infuses every match with a sense of playfulness that sets it apart from more serious military shooters.

Gameplay modes further contribute to its depth. Classic objectives such as Control Point, Payload, King of the Hill, and Capture the Flag provide varied strategic scenarios. Payload maps, in particular, showcase the importance of coordinated team pushes and defensive holds, creating dramatic momentum swings within a single round. The diversity of map design ensures that no two matches feel identical, as terrain layout and objective structure significantly influence team composition and tactics.

Customization and progression add another layer of engagement. Over time, Valve introduced an extensive system of unlockable weapons, cosmetic items, and taunts. While most of these items are cosmetic, they have fostered a thriving in-game economy and trading community. The culture surrounding rare hats and unusual effects became a defining aspect of the game’s identity. Players often spend as much time curating their loadouts and participating in trades as they do competing in matches. This personalization element has contributed to the game’s sustained appeal.

Community involvement has been vital to Team Fortress 2’s longevity. The Steam Workshop enabled creators to submit maps, cosmetics, and other content, many of which were officially integrated into updates. Seasonal events, community map rotations, and holiday-themed modes keep the experience fresh. Even as official update frequency has slowed in later years, the player base continues to generate custom servers, mods, and unique gameplay variations that extend the life of the core mechanics.

However, the game has not been without challenges. In recent years, issues with bots and cheating have impacted the casual matchmaking experience, leading to frustration among portions of the community. The aging Source engine also reveals technical limitations compared to modern shooters. Performance inconsistencies and dated infrastructure occasionally remind players of the game’s age. Despite these hurdles, the foundational gameplay remains compelling enough that many continue to return.

Beyond its own player base, Team Fortress 2’s influence on the industry is profound. Its class-based design and emphasis on hero identity laid groundwork for later hero shooters and team-based competitive games. The concept of combining distinct character personalities with gameplay roles became a blueprint that many titles would adopt in the following decade. Its impact extends beyond mechanics into community culture, streaming, and even internet meme history.

Ultimately, Team Fortress 2 endures because it excels at what matters most: delivering satisfying, team-oriented multiplayer combat with personality. Its blend of strategic depth, humor, and stylized presentation creates a uniquely balanced experience that appeals to both casual players and competitive enthusiasts. While its infrastructure shows signs of age, its core design remains as engaging as ever. Few multiplayer games have achieved such lasting relevance, and Team Fortress 2 continues to stand as a landmark in the evolution of online shooters.

Team Fortress 2 is less a game and more a full-blown existential crisis wrapped in an absurdly colorful, hat-obsessed fever dream. It's free, which is great, because you'll need all your spare cash for the virtual haberdashery. Seriously, I've spent more on tiny digital fedoras than I have on actual groceries this month. My doctor thinks I have a hat-related gambling addiction. He's probably right.

You'll join a server, ready to defend a briefcase, and instead find yourself in a chaotic ballet of rocket-jumping maniacs, perpetually drunk Scotsmen, and invisible Frenchmen trying to spoon-feed you a knife. You'll laugh, you'll cry (mostly from random crits), and you'll scream at your screen as a robot with perfect aim snipes you from across the map, because apparently, the bot crisis is just part of the charm now. It's like inviting a few friends over for a party, and half of them turn out to be highly aggressive, slurring AI.

Despite Valve treating it like their forgotten middle child who occasionally gets a birthday card two years late, TF2 still shines. It's a glorious, hilarious mess that will consume your evenings, your weekends, and eventually, your very soul, all for the fleeting joy of a perfectly executed Medic uber or a satisfying backstab. Is it perfect? No. Is it utterly captivating and infinitely re-playable? Absolutely. Come for the free fun, stay because your inventory is worth more than your car, and you're too emotionally invested to leave.

This game doesn't waste your time with complicated systems. No skill trees. No crafting. No 200-hour grind. Just you, three survivors, and pure chaos. And somehow.. that simplicity is exactly why it works.

The core gameplay loop is brutally effective: move from save room to safe room, survive panic events, cover your teammates, and pray the AI Director doesn't decide you're having too easy of a time. Every run feels slightly different. Sometimes you barely make it with 5 HP. Sometimes a Tank spawns at the worst possible moment and your whole team collapses in 30 seconds.

What makes this game legendary is team dependency. You cannot be the "main character". If a Hunter pins you, you're useless without help. If a Smoker drags you away, you're dead alone. The game forces cooperation naturally - not through rewards, but through survival.

The atmosphere is another strong point. The sound design alone builds tension better than many horror games. Hearing the Witch cry in the distance instantly changes the team's behavior. Heavy footsteps of a Tank? Panic. Pure panic.

Weapons feel simple but satisfying. Shotguns are powerful at close range. The rifle is reliable. Molotovs and pipe bombs can turn disaster into hope. It's not about realism - it's about flow and pressure.

Yes, the game shows its age visually. Animations and textures are dated. Campaigns are relatively short. Solo play with bots is decent but nowhere near the experience of playing with real people.

But honestly? This game proves that strong design never gets old.

Even in 2026, it still delivers: Intense co-op gameplay; High replayability thanks to the AI Director; Memorable special infected; Fast pacing with zero filter.

It's one of those rare games where mechanics matter more than graphics. And that's why it survives.

Recommended? Absolutely. It would be very stupid not to recommend such a legendary game!
Play it with friends. Turn off the lights. Use voice chat. And try not to scream when the Tank shows up xd

Left 4 Dead is a classic — and a strong reflection of the quality Valve has historically been known for.

At its core, Left 4 Dead perfected the co-op zombie shooter formula. Four players, tight map design, dynamic enemy spawns through the AI Director system, and a constant sense of tension that keeps every run feeling slightly different. It’s simple in concept but incredibly polished in execution.

What makes it stand out is pacing. The game knows when to apply pressure and when to give players a brief moment to breathe. The special infected are designed to punish disorganized teams, reinforcing the importance of communication and coordination. When your team is working together — covering corners, calling out threats, sticking close — the experience feels smooth and satisfying.

The campaign structure is also memorable. The maps are concise, atmospheric, and replayable without feeling bloated. There’s no unnecessary filler — just focused, high-quality co-op gameplay.

Like many older titles, it shows its age visually. But mechanically, it still holds up extremely well. That says a lot.

If you enjoy cooperative shooters or want to experience one of Valve’s most iconic multiplayer titles, Left 4 Dead is an easy recommendation. It’s a reminder of a time when tight design and strong fundamentals carried a game — and it still works.

Left 4 Dead 2 is an outstanding product. Modern titles should take it as a benchmark in many respects, from lore design to technical execution. All of this forms a distinctive vision of a game that remains relevant despite having been released as far back as 2009.

The decision to host a pool of Chinese servers for many years still looks novel and fresh. I often feel the need to remind myself of the game’s current state and of the publisher’s and developers’ overall attitude toward the community-in such moments I join online sessions with 24 out of 24 players, an insane mess of on-screen effects, and 400 ms ping. Occasionally, vanilla sessions appear-and that looks strange.

The most unusual part is that from time to time I encounter recommendations to use the in-game server browser to find sessions in order to play without the classic Chinese mod soup. But why? The point is that the “Internet” list produces the same 400-ping sessions anyway, while “Custom” contains private and friends-only lobbies where you are definitely not expected and not welcome. There is no way to filter this out-L4D2 SRCDS does not provide any information about the lobby type or even the difficulty (although, seemingly, the developer would only require setting the “FCVAR_SERVER” flag for the “z_difficulty” cvar… no, too difficult). Abandon these attempts.

To be honest, I still cannot understand why large updates like “The Last Stand,” which bring a lot of new and interesting content into the game, are needed at all. This looks outdated; I am confident that very few people were waiting for this. I am also confident that the community is eagerly awaiting the day when the developers reach an agreement with the publisher and implement traditional in-game gambling in the form of cases, as well as competitive matchmaking, which would undoubtedly breathe a second life into the game. They all certainly have some experience in this area.

Therefore, I look forward to new smart and well-considered decisions from the developers regarding this title. Many thanks to them for their care and for shaping the current state of the product, which allows me to enjoy the game… on LAN, or in a solo mutations like “Last Man on Earth.”

Left 4 Dead 2: The immortal king of co-op shooters.

It’s been over a decade, and nothing has managed to kill this game. Not even the apocalypse. If you want pure, unadulterated chaotic fun, look no further.

✅Infinite Replayability: No two runs are the same. The "AI Director" changes the spawns, the items, and the weather every time you play to keep you on your toes.

✅Co-op Perfection: This is the ultimate "play with friends" game. Whether you are saving a teammate from a Smoker or screaming as a Tank smashes through a wall, the memories are priceless.

✅The Workshop (The Real MVP): Steam Workshop support is insane. You can turn the zombies into Shrek, the weapons into laser guns, or play thousands of custom maps created by the community.

✅Content Galore: It includes all the campaigns from the original Left 4 Dead, making it the definitive zombie experience.

Pros:
✅ Runs on a potato (perfect optimization).
✅ Infinite content thanks to the community.
✅ Satisfying gunplay and gore system.
✅ Pure, chaotic joy with friends.

Cons:
❌ Your friends might "accidentally" shoot you with a shotgun.
❌ You will never hear a car alarm the same way again.

Verdict: 10/10. Even in 2026, it is still the GOAT.

❌The Legacy Continues...❌
While many "spiritual successors" have tried to take the crown, Left 4 Dead 2 remains undefeated. The community is still alive, the servers are full, and the mods keep getting crazier every day. Whether we ever see a "3" in the title or not, this game has already achieved immortality. Grab a frying pan, get your friends, and start the engine. The bridge isn't going to lower itself!

If this review helped you find a gas can, a reward would be greatly appreciated. Stay away from the Witches!

The short answer is yes, you should buy and play it because it's totally worth it, and the price nowadays is equal to a few packs of chips.

Now the long part: Left 4 Dead 2, a game made and launched in 2009, which is almost 17 years old as of 2026, which most of the people might consider to be some old-school game, which no doubt it is, but then you see the "DEVELOPED BY" part, where we come across a familiar name, which is VALVE. And as we all know, Valve is known for making timeless classics, mainly focusing on gameplay while also balancing other core elements, which is also one of the reasons this game is considered old but not boring. Because the gameplay is so good that no matter how old it gets, it's always fun to play, and even more fun if you play with your friends (which, no doubt, I did).

Everything in this game is so nice and well made that you end up getting stuck in the world of L4F2, from the atmosphere to the sound effects, from the music to the characters and their dialogues (fire, btw). And one of the most important and crucial selling points of this game is 'Fun Gameplay,' which sounds stupid, but trust me, you won't find something like this anywhere else. That's why new people get hooked on this game and old players keep coming back for it.

Now if all reasons have convinced you to buy this game, then go on and convince your friends to buy it too, because the game is no doubt fun to play in single-player, but it is a co-op game, so having someone to play with is obviously more fun and entertaining (people/friends > bots).
