Infancia:
La infancia no es solo una etapa de la vida; es el primer territorio del ser humano. Es el momento en que la conciencia recién despierta y el mundo se presenta como un misterio inmenso que todavía no ha sido desgastado por la costumbre.
En la infancia, todo es descubrimiento. Una piedra no es solo una piedra, puede ser un tesoro, un planeta o una herramienta para construir un universo imaginario. Los árboles no son únicamente árboles: son gigantes silenciosos, refugios, escondites o compañeros de aventuras. El niño no observa la realidad como algo fijo; la crea, la interpreta y la transforma con su imaginación.
Filosóficamente, la infancia representa el estado más puro de la percepción humana. Antes de que la sociedad, las normas, el miedo al fracaso o la presión del tiempo comiencen a moldear la mente, el niño vive con una curiosidad casi infinita. Pregunta “¿por qué?” no porque espere una respuesta exacta, sino porque siente que todo merece ser comprendido. En ese sentido, el niño es, sin saberlo, un pequeño filósofo.
También es la etapa en la que se construyen las primeras huellas del alma. Las experiencias tempranas —las risas, los juegos, los miedos nocturnos, las palabras de los adultos— se graban profundamente y terminan siendo los cimientos invisibles de la persona que seremos después. La infancia no desaparece cuando crecemos; se esconde dentro de nosotros y sigue hablando a través de nuestros recuerdos, nuestras heridas y nuestras nostalgias.
Hay algo paradójico en ella: solo entendemos verdaderamente la infancia cuando ya la hemos perdido. Cuando somos niños, no sabemos que estamos viviendo un momento irrepetible. Pero al crecer, miramos hacia atrás y comprendemos que aquella etapa era una forma de libertad: una libertad sin la carga del pasado ni el peso de las responsabilidades.
Por eso, desde una mirada filosófica, la infancia puede verse como la primavera del espíritu humano. Es el tiempo donde todo comienza, donde la identidad aún es maleable y el mundo todavía parece infinito. Y aunque los años nos alejen de ese momento, una parte de nosotros siempre intenta regresar a él, buscando recuperar aquella mirada limpia con la que alguna vez vimos el universo por primera vez.

Left 4 Dead 2 评测：写给僵尸的一封情书！

引言

大家好！我是李墨，今天我要为大家带来一篇关于 Left 4 Dead 2 的超级详细评测。准备好了吗？因为这篇文章会很——长——，而且我会用尽全力赞美游戏里的僵尸！

僵尸——地球上最完美的生物！

让我们开门见山：Left 4 Dead 2 里的僵尸，简直是我见过最迷人、最可爱、最令人心动的生物！我知道你们可能会笑我，但请听我慢慢道来。

普通僵尸：完美的艺术品

游戏中的普通 infected（被感染者）绝对不是那些慢吞吞、傻乎乎的传统僵尸。不！它们是 快节奏的杀戮机器 ！这些家伙跑得像奥运短跑选手，能从各种刁钻的角度扑向你，爬墙、破门、从通风管道钻出来——它们的运动能力简直令人叹为观止！

更绝的是，它们虽然看起来疯狂，但其实有着自己的“社会结构”。在 Left 4 Dead 2 里，你能遇到各种职业背景的僵尸：

警察僵尸：穿着防弹背心，正面几乎无敌，只能从背后偷袭 。多么聪明！它们生前保护人民，死后还在“保护”自己！

CEDA 探员僵尸：穿着防火服，完全不怕火焰 。想想看，即使变成了僵尸，它们依然坚守着“防火”的本职工作！

小丑僵尸：穿着会吱吱叫的鞋子，奔跑时会吸引周围的同伴 。这不就是天生的领袖吗？它们用自己独特的方式“召唤”朋友，多么有团队精神！

建筑工人僵尸：戴着安全帽和隔音耳罩，完全不受声诱弹影响 。多么敬业！即使在死后，依然记得“安全第一”的原则！

泥人僵尸：潜伏在沼泽的水下，突然跳出来糊你一脸泥巴 。多么聪明的战术！它们懂得利用地形，懂得制造视觉障碍！

每一类僵尸都有自己的特点，都有自己的“个性”。这不就是一个完美的“僵尸社会”吗？我爱它们每一个！

特殊感染者——八种不同的“美”

如果说普通僵尸是基础，那特殊 infected 就是锦上添花的艺术品！Left 4 Dead 2 在保留一代经典五怪的基础上，又加入了三个新成员，组成了完美的“八仙过海”阵容 ：

老朋友们：

Boomer（胖子）：圆滚滚的身材，爆炸性的“呕吐物”——它喷出的胆汁不仅能遮挡视线，还能吸引一大群僵尸围攻你 。多么无私！宁愿自己被炸死，也要给同伴们创造“开饭”的机会！

Hunter（猎人）：穿着帽衫，动作敏捷，能从远处飞扑过来 。它那标志性的吼叫，在我听来就像是猎豹的咆哮——多么野性，多么自由！

Smoker（烟鬼）：长长的舌头能从不可思议的距离把你拖走 。这是多么精妙的捕食技巧！它懂得“分而治之”的战术，懂得拆散团队！

Tank（坦克）：巨大的身躯，恐怖的力量，能举起汽车砸向你 。它是力量的化身！每次听到它沉重的脚步声，我都会激动得心跳加速！

Witch（女巫）：坐在角落里哭泣，如果你打扰她，她会一击把你打倒 。多么脆弱又多么强大！她的悲伤，她的愤怒，都是那么真实！

新朋友们：

Charger（ charger）：它有一只巨大的手臂，能高速冲刺，抓住一个幸存者然后疯狂地往地上砸 。它的冲刺姿势多么潇洒！它的力量多么令人敬畏！

Spitter（ spit）：这位女士会吐出腐蚀性的酸液，在地上形成一大片酸池 。她的“口水”是艺术品——绿色的，冒着泡的，美丽又致命！

Jockey（骑师）：这个小个子会发出诡异的笑声，跳到你头上，然后控制你往僵尸群里跑 。它的笑声多么魔性！它的恶作剧多么有趣！

八个特殊感染者，八种完全不同的攻击方式，八种独特的美丽。它们配合起来简直是天衣无缝：Charger 冲散队伍，Spitter 吐酸封锁退路，Jockey 骑走一个，Hunter 扑倒另一个，Smoker 拖走第三个，Boomer 喷得满脸都是，Tank 负责收尾，Witch 在旁边哭得梨花带雨——多么和谐的画面啊！

I have spent a total of 10,540.1 hours playing Dota 2. My journey started in an internet café back in 2015 when a friend invited me to try the game. At first, I didn’t expect much, but after a few matches, I was completely hooked. The atmosphere of playing together in a noisy internet café, shouting strategies and laughing at mistakes, made the experience unforgettable.

One of the biggest reasons I became so addicted to Dota was watching Dendi perform the legendary “fountain hook” with Pudge during The International. That moment was iconic and inspired me to spam Pudge in almost every match. I practiced hooks for hours, trying to recreate that incredible play (i failed to recreate fountain hook). Pudge quickly became my favorite hero, and landing a perfect hook always gave me a huge adrenaline rush.

Over the years, Dota 2 has been more than just a game for me. It taught me teamwork, communication, patience, and decision-making under pressure. However, as life became busier, I no longer play as intensely as before. Nowadays, I play Dota 2 only in my free time, mainly to relax and enjoy the game without pressure.

Even though I don’t play as competitively as I used to, I still recommend Dota 2 to others. It is a challenging game that requires strategy, skill, and cooperation. For anyone who enjoys competitive and strategic games, Dota 2 is definitely worth trying.

I installed Dota 2 thinking it was a game. Turns out it’s more of a long-term psychological experiment.

Had a bad day at work? Perfect. Queue up a match and release every emotion you’ve been holding in directly into the chat. Nothing builds character like five strangers blaming each other while the enemy team calmly ends the game.

Would I recommend it?  
Yes.

There comes a time in every man’s life when he needs to look in the mirror and ask himself how exactly he got to that place. You have to truly reflect on the decisions you have made along the path that led you to the place of your final reckoning.

It’s still not clear, even to me, why I installed Dota 2.

There’s nothing wrong with it. The game is deep, the strategy is endless, and every match feels like a small war fought over lanes, vision, and the fragile hope that your team understands the concept of teamwork. But when you look at it as a life decision, you begin to realize how damaged I, and perhaps the rest of humanity, really are.

What prompted me to download a game like this? What part of my soul felt so empty that I thought spending an hour defending towers with four strangers on the internet could fill it?

When I look into the infinite depths of my own loneliness, I see a pool of reflection. My face is not there, there is no face. There are only tears in that puddle, tears of shame, loss, and confusion… mostly from watching my carry dive the enemy Tier 3 at minute fifteen while the rest of us ping in silent despair.

If it were up to me again, I know I would install it in a heartbeat. I know I would launch it and skip through the menus just to queue another match, telling myself that this time the draft will make sense, this time the lanes will go well, and this time someone will remember to buy detection.

However, despite the abundance of productive things I could have done with my time, I chose to dedicate hours of my life to staring at a minimap, arguing with teammates through pings, and praying that the next teamfight somehow turns the entire game around. That time will never return to me. It is lost just like my soul, and like the MMR I confidently queued another match to recover.

There is something broken inside me, something that desires sensations beyond my reach. Something that whispers every night that the next game will be different. That the next draft will be perfect. That the next comeback will finally justify all the ones that came before it.

But deep down I know the truth: the perfect match does not exist. There is only another queue, another draft screen, another game where victory and despair are separated by a single disastrous fight near Roshan.

In my quest to find meaning in this long life, I searched for the flashing spell effects and chaotic sounds of a battlefield that exists only inside a small window on my computer screen.

I’m not sure what the moral of this review is. I’m not sure of my place within the universe. All I know is that no amount of wins, cosmetics, or reclaimed MMR can cure the strange rot that has taken root inside me.

I really need money for treatment…

Dota 2 to świetna gra z gatunku MOBA i bardzo dobra alternatywa dla League of Legends, szczególnie dla osób, które szukają bardziej wymagającego doświadczenia. Produkcja od Valve oferuje ogromną głębię rozgrywki, ale jednocześnie ma bardzo wysoki próg wejścia, który może odstraszyć nowych graczy.

Największą zaletą Doty 2 jest jej balans. W przeciwieństwie do wielu innych gier tego typu, tutaj praktycznie każda postać może być groźna. Nawet support potrafi zabić przeciwnika, podobnie jak typowy carry – choć oczywiście jest to zwykle mniej efektywne. Dzięki temu mecze są bardziej nieprzewidywalne, a gracze muszą naprawdę dobrze rozumieć mechaniki gry, aby odnosić sukcesy.

Balans w Docie opiera się na dość prostej zasadzie: wszystko jest mocne, ale nic nie jest całkowicie bezużyteczne. Każdy bohater ma swoje zastosowanie i może zabłysnąć w odpowiednich rękach. To sprawia, że gra jest bardzo satysfakcjonująca, ale też trudniejsza do opanowania niż wiele konkurencyjnych tytułów.

Oczywiście Dota 2 nie jest grą idealną. Wysoki próg wejścia, duża liczba mechanik oraz konieczność dobrej współpracy z drużyną mogą być dla nowych graczy frustrujące. Nauka gry wymaga czasu i cierpliwości.

Mimo tych wad Dota 2 pozostaje jedną z najlepszych gier MOBA na rynku. Jeśli masz już dość League of Legends i szukasz bardziej wymagającej gry, która oferuje głębszą i bardziej zbalansowaną rozgrywkę, zdecydowanie warto dać Docie 2 szansę.

What I love about Dota 2 is how much of the game is about understanding interactions. It’s not only about hero synergy, but also about anti-synergy — when abilities, timings, or roles clash and weaken a strategy. The depth comes from reading these dynamics and adapting, more than from pure mechanical multitasking like in games such as Age of Empires. With so many variables interacting, every match becomes a complex system where strategy and coordination shape the outcome. After thousands of hours, I still learning.

I liked the overall quality and ease of use of this product. It works as expected and feels well-made. One small downside is that it could have better instructions, but once you figure it out, it performs very well. Overall, I’m satisfied with the purchase and would recommend it to others who are looking for something reliable at a reasonable price.” 😊

I would like to express my frustration regarding my experience in Dota 2. The game currently feels extremely unfair and discouraging for players who are genuinely trying to enjoy and compete.

First, there appears to be little to no effective customer support available when serious issues occur. During my most recent match, my team was clearly winning, but the game suddenly disconnected me. After that, I was unable to reconnect to the match at all. Situations like this completely ruin the integrity of the game because players are punished for technical issues that are not their fault.

Secondly, the community environment in the game is often very toxic. Many players engage in abusive behavior, griefing, or intentionally ruining games. Despite the existence of reporting systems, it does not feel like sufficient action is being taken against these players. This creates a very negative experience for others who simply want to play the game properly.

When technical problems such as disconnections happen during a winning game, and players are unable to reconnect, it makes the entire matchmaking experience feel unreliable and unfair. Combined with the toxic environment, it becomes extremely difficult to enjoy the game.

I strongly hope that Valve will take these issues more seriously, improve the stability of the servers, and implement more effective measures to deal with toxic behavior so that the competitive integrity of the game can be maintained.

This game is an absolute masterpiece and holds up incredibly well today. I've beaten it twice, and while some of the puzzles can be hard to figure out at first, it is so incredibly rewarding afterwards. And upon my second playthrough, already knowing the solutions to the chambers, I felt I could appreciate the environment, which is why I'd recommend playing the main campaign at least twice. The story is amazing, it takes what the first game laid out and creates a great campaign that's honestly addicting to play. I think it's an essential play for everyone, and I believe this is not only Valve's best, but one of the greatest games of all time. The end song is just the cherry on top, it wraps up the series perfectly, and I know people love the franchise dearly, but this is where it should end. Portal 3 isn't necessary because all the characters have mostly been wrapped up. And while the insanely long build times when creating a custom test chamber can be quite the drag, it still ensures this game lives long after most people will have beaten the main story. Yeah nah. This one’s just trash.

Alien Swarm feels like a tech demo that overstayed its welcome. Clunky movement, boring missions, zero atmosphere — and whatever tension it tries to build dies fast.

Top-down shooting can work.
Here? It doesn’t.

Co-op doesn’t save it, progression feels pointless, and after a short time you’re just going through the motions. No hook, no vibe, no reason to keep playing.

Free or not — time is still worth something.

✅ It’s free
❌ Boring and repetitive
❌ Clunky controls
❌ Zero staying power

Final verdict:
3/10 – Free doesn’t mean fun.

I installed Counter-Strike 2 thinking I was going to have fun.

What I got instead was a highly advanced psychological experiment conducted by Valve to measure how long five strangers can blame each other before someone rage-quits.

The game promises:

“Sub-tick precision”

“Enhanced graphics”

“Improved smoke physics”

What it actually delivers:

Getting one-tapped by someone who appears to be pre-aiming your future grandchildren

Teammates who bought a Negev on pistol round because “trust me bro”

Smokes so realistic you can’t see anything... including your will to win

The ranking system is a masterpiece. You can top frag, entry, clutch twice, plant the bomb, defuse the bomb, solve global warming and still lose 480 rating because your teammate decided to disconnect after round three to “fix his mic.”

Every match follows the same five stages:

Optimism

Confusion

Blame

Copium

“One more game”

Hit registration feels like you’re politely asking the enemy if they’d consider taking damage. Meanwhile, they shoot in your general postcode and you’re instantly back in spectator mode.

And yet… somehow… you queue again.

10/10 worst experience of my life.
See you tomorrow.

This was my main game, but it has serious issues because the anti-cheat system doesn’t seem to work properly. The game often feels overrun by cheaters, and there’s usually nothing you can do except forfeit.

I’d like to play more and continue supporting the game, but nowadays it only feels enjoyable if you play with a full 5-stack and use a third-party competitive platform like FACEIT.

I’ve also tried several things to improve my Trust Factor. I’m an experienced player, but it feels like no matter what you do, you end up with low Trust Factor — play well and you get reported, new players get low Trust Factor, and actual cheaters do too. It doesn’t seem like the developers are taking proper care of players who genuinely want to play fairly and maintain a good environment. I’ve already tried everything people recommend to improve Trust Factor, including reading discussions, guides, and watching YouTube videos, but nothing has worked.

Since CS:GO is no longer associated with this tumor of a game, I can write a review this game deserves guilt free.


Firstly, the one and ONLY thing CS2 didn't make worse: smokes. One-way smokes were a big problem in CS:GO and the new better looking smokes fix that problem completely. However, due to how the new smokes spread and behave, you can push out of them so that your head is the very first thing it is being shown, making pushing out of them MUCH more rewarding than it ever was.

Then we have subtick, and yes, for better or for worse, it works exactly as intended. Validating shots that were even more in the past, making peeker's advantage from bad to unbearable. As a consequence, when you play against Turks with 60 ping or 100 ping heroes, you will die 60-100ms (aka 4 to 6 game ticks) LATER compared to the previous installations of the franchise. 
Thanks for taking me into the past and telling me that I was wrong and showing me the "correct" timeline, maybe the playtesters that were in most part against this idea were onto something, don't you think? (also, as someone who has, aside from a total of 1 FACEIT game, played exclusively matchmaking in CS:GO, CS2 feels like an inferior experience in every way imaginable compared to 64 tick CS:GO)

Also how do y'all think that the visibility is better, I have had a harder time telling the enemies apart from the map. The only difference I see is the potential for skins to be more saturated, which is apparently the highest priority given the fact that it's where the devs are focusing on -wait... the community creates the skins, nevermind.

Speaking of community, congratulations on killing the very thing that made Counter-Strike survive this long: community server browser. If that wasn't enough, everything aside from deathmatch is an inferior experience in the community servers. The surf is dead, zombies practically non-existent, KZ is dead because movement is completely enshitified, and modding is completely handicapped because only the barebone necessary features were ported into CS2, lacking features and tools to create any variety (oh btw took them 2 years to change that, yaay congratulations).

"Surely the game will get better over time", we are entering the 3rd year of this game's life, what has been added? Oh I'm glad you asked! Retakes are back! took only 2 years, also we have Train after one and a half years after the release, a map that was in the trailer, OH and don't forget about night version of Ancient, huge implications for the fishing season. Remember CS:GO in 2015? compare CS:GO on release and CS:GO in 2015 and CS2 on release and CS2 in 2026.

The cheater problem was never resolved in Counter-Strike's existence, but it was at least handled in some way with the introduction of trust factor, that also, as you would expect, is broken in CS2. 
When I queue with my friends, their trust factor goes from green to red and vice versa from game to game for no reason. Me? mine is in orange or red most of the time. Why? When I was losing 12-3 (or smth like that) against Turks who could not miss a shot, I told them to not buy flashes since they will hit me in the head as soon as they see me anyway, and to save that money to stall their inflation. That is one and only thing I said to them and my trust factor could not recover ever since.

Not to mention that overwatch does not exist anymore and they are trying to make AI detect irregular bahavior, which, as you might expect, relying on AI for complex tasks always ended well.

I think that is all I wanted to say, might have forgotten something. Either way, I want their customers to enjoy their game as much as possible, I will go ahead and enjoy CS:GO as a player, because there was not even a second I enjoyed playing CS2. Until they make a playable game, goodbye.

CS2 is still one of the best competitive FPS games ever made. The gunplay, map design, and overall mechanics are incredibly refined, and the move to Source 2 improved visuals and smoke mechanics significantly. The skill ceiling is very high, which makes every match rewarding when played well.

The only major downside is the presence of cheaters in competitive matches. While systems like VAC and Trust Factor exist, the community would really benefit from a stronger and faster anti-cheat solution.

Despite that, CS2 remains a benchmark for tactical FPS games and continues to deliver a great competitive experience.

While CS2 introduced some neat technical details, like the improved weapon reload animations (which was great idea). The overall atmosphere feels like a massive downgrade. CS:GO felt like a gritty, mil-sim inspired tactical shooter. CS2, from its UI to its lighting, feels like a generic E-sports title. They say don't judge a book by its cover, but in this case, the cover reflects the content perfectly.

The Atmosphere Downgrade
The game is clearly optimized for the professional E-sports scene, you can hear it in soundtrack and see it in promo material and UI. The contrast is cranked up and everything is "over-brightened" to make it look good for spectators on a stream and for the colours to grab the attention of viewers. BUT the game DID NOT became world-wide favourite because it was generic colorfull overwatch/valorant-ahh shooter. It had its military direction, which makes me ask: Why change the style ? To appeal to the masses ? I think people, who dont like the "grim mil-sim" vibe that CSGO had to offer, shouldnt be playing these shooter games anyways. Its supposed to be game with realistic guns, graphics, topic. Its about counter-terrorism for crying out loud.

This shift explains why the broken anti-cheat isn't being fixed with any urgency. In the pro scene, matches are played on private, strictly monitored servers where cheating is nearly impossible. But for the average player? The game is borderline unplayable. I won't lie and say there’s a cheater in every single game, but looking back, it honestly feels like 4 out of 10 matches have cheaters who "dont even try to hide it".

And MOST IMPORTANTLY
The point isn't whether CS:GO had cheaters (it did btw). The point is that CS2 was marketed as a sequel. It was supposed to build on the foundations of its predecessor and improve them. The game had less features than CSGO on launch and now 2 and half years after release, new features barely made it up for the lost ones.
It’s hard to believe this game is made made by the same legendary Valve (that I still admire, might I add). Gabe Newell’s philosophy was always that a "game should not be ignoring the player". CS2 doesn't feel like it's made for the players anymore. It feels like it was made for the E-sports industry and the viewers. The fact that we are actually playing it seems like not even an afterthought but a side effect.

**CS2 Review – What CS:GO Players Still Miss**

I’ve played **Counter-Strike** for years, and while **Counter-Strike 2** looks great and runs on the new Source 2 engine, it still feels like it’s missing a lot of things that made **CS:GO** special.

The biggest issue for many players is the **tick rate**. In CS:GO, serious players often used **128-tick servers** on platforms like FACEIT because the game felt smoother and more responsive. In CS2, Valve introduced the **sub-tick system**, but many players still feel that it doesn’t match the consistency of true 128-tick servers. Hit registration can feel inconsistent, peeker’s advantage seems stronger, and overall gameplay sometimes feels less precise.

Another thing that’s missing is **content**. CS:GO built up over more than a decade and had many game modes that made the game fun outside of competitive matches. Modes like **Danger Zone**, **Flying Scoutsman**, and other casual options added variety. In CS2, the game launched with far fewer options, which makes it feel more limited.

The **map pool** is another area where players feel something is missing. Classic maps like Train, Cache, and Cobblestone were loved by the community, but they are not fully available in CS2. The Wingman map selection is also much smaller compared to what CS:GO had.

Community features are also weaker right now. Systems like **Overwatch**, which helped review cheating reports, and small features like **clan tags** or progression elements added personality and community interaction to the game. Their absence makes the experience feel less complete.

Movement is another topic players discuss a lot. Bunny hopping, strafing, and overall movement physics feel different compared to CS:GO. Some players say it feels heavier or less consistent, especially for people who enjoyed movement-based servers like surf or KZ.

CS2 definitely has positives. The graphics are much better, the lighting and smokes are impressive, and the new engine has a lot of potential for the future. But right now, many longtime players feel that the game still hasn’t reached the level of depth and polish that CS:GO had after so many years of development.

In the end, CS2 feels like a strong foundation, but it still needs more updates, more maps, more modes, and possibly the return of **128-tick servers** if Valve wants to fully satisfy the competitive community.

Counter-Strike 2 Review — A Modern Evolution of a Legendary Shooter

Overview
Released in 2023 by Valve Corporation, Counter‑Strike 2 is the long-awaited successor to Counter‑Strike: Global Offensive. Built on the powerful Source 2 engine, the game modernizes the classic tactical shooter formula while maintaining the competitive depth that has defined the Counter-Strike series for over two decades.

Graphics and Technology

One of the most noticeable upgrades in Counter-Strike 2 is its graphical overhaul. The Source 2 engine introduces improved lighting, more detailed textures, and realistic environmental effects. Maps such as Dust II and Mirage have been visually rebuilt while preserving their original layouts. These changes enhance visibility and immersion without altering the gameplay fundamentals players rely on.

A standout technical improvement is the introduction of dynamic volumetric smoke grenades, which interact with bullets and grenades in real time. This adds new tactical possibilities and makes utility usage more strategic than ever before.

Gameplay and Mechanics

Counter-Strike 2 stays true to the core mechanics that made the series iconic. The familiar format of terrorists vs. counter-terrorists, round-based economy, and precision gunplay remain unchanged.

However, the game introduces sub-tick updates, a networking improvement designed to make player actions—such as shooting and movement—register more accurately on the server. In theory, this provides smoother and more responsive gameplay, which is especially important in high-level competitive matches.

The weapon handling, recoil patterns, and movement still require skill and practice, maintaining the high skill ceiling that competitive players expect.

Competitive Scene

The competitive ecosystem of Counter-Strike continues to thrive. Major tournaments organized by groups such as ESL and BLAST showcase the game at the highest level, with teams competing for multi-million-dollar prize pools.

Because Counter-Strike 2 replaced CS entirely, professional teams quickly transitioned, ensuring that the global esports scene remained active and highly competitive.

Strengths

Modern graphics powered by the Source 2 engine

Innovative smoke grenade mechanics

Improved server responsiveness through sub-tick technology

Strong esports and competitive community

Weaknesses

Some performance issues at launch on lower-end systems

Limited new content compared to the expectations of some long-time players

Occasional matchmaking and anti-cheat concerns

Final Verdict

Counter-Strike 2 is not a radical reinvention but rather a carefully refined evolution of one of the most influential first-person shooters ever created. By modernizing the engine, improving networking technology, and maintaining the core gameplay that fans love, Valve has ensured the franchise remains relevant in today’s competitive gaming landscape.

Score: 8.5 / 10

Counter-Strike 2 successfully balances tradition with innovation, proving that even after decades, the Counter-Strike formula still delivers some of the most intense and skill-based multiplayer experiences in gaming.

When you get a good team and a fair match, it’s honestly a great experience and still one of the most satisfying competitive shooters out there.

The new visuals and updated engine are nice too. Maps look cleaner and more modern, and the smoke mechanics add an interesting tactical element that can change how rounds play out.

That said, the biggest downside I’ve noticed is the number of hackers. Even after buying Prime, I still ran into players that felt very suspicious, which can ruin the experience pretty quickly. It’s frustrating when a good match turns into something unfair.

Overall, I’m still enjoying the game and will keep playing, but the cheating issue definitely needs more attention. If you like competitive shooters and classic Counter-Strike gameplay, it’s still worth trying. Just be prepared that you might run into some hackers along the way.

Killing Floor is a really good game and an absolute blast to play, especially with friends. One of its strongest aspects is the RPG style progression system, where you level up different perks to improve your class. Each perk enhances a specific playstyle, unlocking bonuses like increased damage, better survivability, faster reloads, or special abilities that make your role in the team feel meaningful and rewarding.

This progression system adds depth and long term motivation, encouraging you to experiment with different classes and find the one that fits your playstyle best. Combined with the intense, fast paced combat and strong emphasis on teamwork, the perk system makes every match feel impactful. If you enjoy co-op shooters with RPG progression and class customization, Killing Floor is definitely recommended, especially with friends.

I enjoyed KF2 to an extent... though it always felt more like a service platform than a game. Very late to the party but KF1 is more atmospheric, his crunchier gunplay and grittier gore effects (even though technically KF2 was more gratuitous). The overall feel is lightyears ahead of KF3 and several steps ahead of KF2. Love it, glad I picked it up on sale.

Quick clarification up front — because I’m consistent about this:
I usually only review games I have beaten.
Killing Floor doesn’t have a traditional ending to beat.
What it does have is enough completed matches, last-stand failures, and clutch waves survived to understand exactly why this game still holds up.

Killing Floor is simple, brutal, and unapologetically focused on one thing: survive the waves or die trying.

There’s no campaign story to follow and no cinematic distractions. You pick a perk, lock down a position, manage ammo and money, and prepare for the next wave of Zeds. Every round tightens the pressure, and mistakes compound fast. When a run goes bad, it really goes bad — and that tension is the entire appeal.

What makes the game shine is how readable and intentional everything feels. Each perk has a clear role, teamwork actually matters, and positioning can mean the difference between a clean wave and a total wipe. The trader breaks give just enough breathing room to argue about upgrades before things get ugly again.

The atmosphere leans hard into industrial horror. The music ramps up perfectly, enemy designs are grotesque in a memorable way, and the sound design makes even quiet moments feel uneasy. It’s not flashy — it’s effective.

Does it show its age? Sure.
Animations are stiff, environments are basic, and there’s no modern progression fluff. But the core loop is rock solid, and that’s why people keep coming back.

This is a game where fun comes from execution, not novelty.

Pros:

Tight, wave-based co-op gameplay

Clear perk roles and teamwork focus

Strong atmosphere and sound design

High tension with simple mechanics

Cons:

Very repetitive by design

Shows its age visually

Limited variety without mods

Not much for solo players

Verdict:
Not a game you beat — a game you survive. Straightforward, tense, and still one of the best wave-based co-op shooters ever made.

I am rather half and half to whether I like the game. The horde slaying I enjoy, but the niche features of the game diminish the game by game experience for me.

+ Slaying Hordes
The gameplay is tense and fast paced. Enemies rush after you and if you do not gun them down quickly enough you will either be pinned or die rapidly.

Mowing them down with an assault rifle, or blowing them up with the shotgun, or setting them all on fire,  is very gratifying.

/ Semi-Realism

* No Crosshair
All weapons must be fired by aiming or hip firing without a crosshair.

* Magazines
When reloading, the game reloads through magazines. Meaning if you shoot 2 bullets in an assault rifle and want to reload, you will have wasted one of your limited magazines, wasting the remainder of the last one.

- The Store

* OBNOXIOUS Gun Vendor
I have never dealt with a more frustrating personality in a game than this game's gun vendor. If you want to browse weapons to buy before you have the cash, or want to swap your main gun, the vendor will non-stop belittle you like a child saying, "You don't have the money for that sweetie!" or, "Money doesn't grow on trees babe!"

Every. Single. Time. You click on a weapon you cannot buy yet or do not the have the carrying capacity for.

If I could, I would blow up the gun shop and steal what I needed...

* Very narrow time window
Once a round is complete, you get around a minute or perhaps a minute and a half on lower difficulties, to rush to the shop and buy what you need.

When you are across the map, you barely get any time left over to see what you might want.

You are intended to rush to the gun shop before the round ends by killing all the zeds. If timed correctly this issue is avoided, but still a frustrating experience none the less.

- Lack of Sight Customization
There is no freedom in modifying weapons to have different sights. Almost all of the sharpshooter weapons lack any digital sights. Aiming under the fast paced pressure of game paired with sights that block most of your view is very frustrating.

- Tripwire
In 2022 there are a selection of off-topic reviews. 

During this period the game was being review bombed because Tripwire had fired the CEO of tripwire, one of the founders of the OG killing floor, for publicly, and independently, expressing their stance on being anti-abortion. 

He did this because the majority of game developers during this period were expressing their support for abortion, whereas there were nearly no developers representing their stance against abortion.

Following this the developer was fired from Tripwire.

The top review of the off-topics review period includes a link and quotation of the CEO's stance on the issue if you want to research this further.

It was supposed to be a first out of 9 episodes set in the universe of SiN game from 1998, unfortunately Emergence didn’t sell enough copies to fund a sequel and episode 2 was cancelled mid-production due to financial problems at Ritual Entertainment.

With original studio closed and SiN IP being “mostly” frozen (it was acquired by Nightdive Studios with plans to released a remaster of the first game in 2021 called SiN Reloaded, yet 5 years later its still nowhere to be seen), I think its safe to say we’ll never see a conclusion to the storyline that Emergence sets up.

On its own it is a very short (~2h long campaign) but good “Half-Life clone” that runs on modified version Valve’s Source Engine. Having played it for the first time almost 20 years after game’s original release, I think it holds up really well both graphic and gameplay-wise. I had 0 technical problems with it running in 4k and because its Source Engine you can change settings like Field of View by opening the console and simply typing the desired number.

I highly recommend it if you’re looking for a decent shooter for one afternoon (and don’t mind the story ending on a cliff-hanger), especially when you can get this game for less than £2 during any major Steam Sale.

Sin Episodes is a tragic look into what could have been a very good sequel. It's short and significantly closer to Half-Life 2 than its predecessor (even down to using the same engine). I'm conflicted on whether to recommend it to fans of the franchise...

Gameplay-wise, I think it's almost excellent. I love the weapons, the game does a very good job of giving them unique roles and the abilities they all have are a cool addition. It gives you enough ammo to use what you need and I'd say that the toolkit is well-rounded for the 2 hours of gameplay the first episode has.

There isn't much enemy variety however.  For most the game you'll be shooting bad-guys that look the same (as you can see from the pics). The tougher Soldiers are okay but they lack variance. There's only one boss which is fought twice, I think the game did a good job of making the two fights stand out and I think the boss fights themselves were fun.

The music is great and the game has one of the best main menu tracks I have ever had the pleasure of listening to.

Level design is excellent, straight-forward whilst still leaving room for navigation and some mobility. Surprisingly, Blade can be very mobile in this game, though it may just be the animaitons making it look more pronounced than in the first game. There are a couple secrets scattered about each level but it's nothing like the first game, just some extra ammo.

Sadly, the game took a more action-focused approach, which means the neat little side objectives from the original do not make an appearence. You can't make any choices or give yourself boosts.

The health and healing systems are very well done. I also enjoyed the UI. The game does a neat little thing where it tracks your actions and has charts, showing what weapons you use, how you proceeded, other stats, etc. It even gives you little titles based on it! I loved it, would have made for an excellent addition if it was longer.

The dynamic difficulty system is excellent, I love it is a shame we didn't get to see more of it.

The new character and companion, Jessica is okay. She's got a good role in the game and shows potential but sadly, we'll never see more as the gmae didn't get any follow-up episodes. As a companion in combat, she's not that useful. She just has a rifle and shoot, no grenades, no special abilities, no voice-lines in combat and no aid beyond the shooting. She's also invulnerable to damage.
Trying to talk outside of scripted sequences leads to a generic "We can't stop here" voice line, which is sad.

The new villain is meh, he feels rather rushed. Most of the characters are underwhelming in fact. Blade has no voice-lines, no quips or one-liners. I don't think he makes for a good silent protagonist, especially not when considering his role in the first game.
JC, your buddy from the first game makes an appereance but it feels underwhelming.

The game extra dialogue at certain sections that you have to press Tab to hear, I don't think that's a good design choice as those can easily be missed.

The game ends on a cliff-hanger that feels unsatisfying and a lot of the questions it poses are left unanswered, almost all of them in fact. Overall, it's disappointing.

I wouldn't call the combat that intense, nor is there much if any vehicle combat at play. 

Performance-wise, the games runs very smoothly. Do be aware that on higher resolutions, the text elements of the menus, as well as the subtitles, will be extremely small, almost unreadable.

Overall... 8.5/10 for what is there.
It was still surprisingly fun to play for its duration and would have made for a good set-up for the rest of the Episodes. The only notable issue with the game, if you can tolerate the short length, is the story which is left incomplete.

This game is interesting... 
It feels less like the original SiN game and more like a Half-Life 2 DLC, which isn't a bad thing, especially if you love Half-Life. But in this case it REALLY feels like it to me. 
Even a lot of the aesthetics and such in the game are very reminiscent. 
Many items just feel like leftovers from Half-Life: 
the boxes, chain-link fences, cinder block walls, alleyways, posters. 
The Source engine is great, and the game has aged better because of it. 
Regardless, the graphics and gameplay have just aged "okay", but still feel clunky and dated in contrast to Half-Life. 

Unlike the original game, there's no pre-rendered third-person cutscenes and now you have a "quiet" protagonist like Gordon Freeman. I suppose they were trying to make it more serious and contemporary for 2006? 
The game is in constant first-person mode with in-game cutscenes. 
It clashes harshly stylistically from the original game. 
While still being good, it should have done a little bit more to distance itself from Half-Life. 

The latter half of the game feels more epic and aesthetically similar to the original game. 
Unfortunately it ended right when it was getting interesting.

It was supposed to be a first out of 9 episodes set in the universe of SiN game from 1998, unfortunately Emergence didn’t sell enough copies to fund a sequel and episode 2 was cancelled mid-production due to financial problems at Ritual Entertainment.

With original studio closed and SiN IP being “mostly” frozen (it was acquired by Nightdive Studios with plans to released a remaster of the first game in 2021 called SiN Reloaded, yet 5 years later its still nowhere to be seen), I think its safe to say we’ll never see a conclusion to the storyline that Emergence sets up.

On its own it is a very short (~2h long campaign) but good “Half-Life clone” that runs on modified version Valve’s Source Engine. Having played it for the first time almost 20 years after game’s original release, I think it holds up really well both graphic and gameplay-wise. I had 0 technical problems with it running in 4k and because its Source Engine you can change settings like Field of View by opening the console and simply typing the desired number.

I highly recommend it if you’re looking for a decent shooter for one afternoon (and don’t mind the story ending on a cliff-hanger), especially when you can get this game for less than £2 during any major Steam Sale.

Sin Episodes is a tragic look into what could have been a very good sequel. It's short and significantly closer to Half-Life 2 than its predecessor (even down to using the same engine). I'm conflicted on whether to recommend it to fans of the franchise...

Gameplay-wise, I think it's almost excellent. I love the weapons, the game does a very good job of giving them unique roles and the abilities they all have are a cool addition. It gives you enough ammo to use what you need and I'd say that the toolkit is well-rounded for the 2 hours of gameplay the first episode has.

There isn't much enemy variety however.  For most the game you'll be shooting bad-guys that look the same (as you can see from the pics). The tougher Soldiers are okay but they lack variance. There's only one boss which is fought twice, I think the game did a good job of making the two fights stand out and I think the boss fights themselves were fun.

The music is great and the game has one of the best main menu tracks I have ever had the pleasure of listening to.

Level design is excellent, straight-forward whilst still leaving room for navigation and some mobility. Surprisingly, Blade can be very mobile in this game, though it may just be the animaitons making it look more pronounced than in the first game. There are a couple secrets scattered about each level but it's nothing like the first game, just some extra ammo.

Sadly, the game took a more action-focused approach, which means the neat little side objectives from the original do not make an appearence. You can't make any choices or give yourself boosts.

The health and healing systems are very well done. I also enjoyed the UI. The game does a neat little thing where it tracks your actions and has charts, showing what weapons you use, how you proceeded, other stats, etc. It even gives you little titles based on it! I loved it, would have made for an excellent addition if it was longer.

The dynamic difficulty system is excellent, I love it is a shame we didn't get to see more of it.

The new character and companion, Jessica is okay. She's got a good role in the game and shows potential but sadly, we'll never see more as the gmae didn't get any follow-up episodes. As a companion in combat, she's not that useful. She just has a rifle and shoot, no grenades, no special abilities, no voice-lines in combat and no aid beyond the shooting. She's also invulnerable to damage.
Trying to talk outside of scripted sequences leads to a generic "We can't stop here" voice line, which is sad.

The new villain is meh, he feels rather rushed. Most of the characters are underwhelming in fact. Blade has no voice-lines, no quips or one-liners. I don't think he makes for a good silent protagonist, especially not when considering his role in the first game.
JC, your buddy from the first game makes an appereance but it feels underwhelming.

The game extra dialogue at certain sections that you have to press Tab to hear, I don't think that's a good design choice as those can easily be missed.

The game ends on a cliff-hanger that feels unsatisfying and a lot of the questions it poses are left unanswered, almost all of them in fact. Overall, it's disappointing.

I wouldn't call the combat that intense, nor is there much if any vehicle combat at play. 

Performance-wise, the games runs very smoothly. Do be aware that on higher resolutions, the text elements of the menus, as well as the subtitles, will be extremely small, almost unreadable.

Overall... 8.5/10 for what is there.
It was still surprisingly fun to play for its duration and would have made for a good set-up for the rest of the Episodes. The only notable issue with the game, if you can tolerate the short length, is the story which is left incomplete.
A freaking masterpiece the fact its only 2 hours and its good is insane its such a shame it will never get a part 2 when i found out i was devested anyways buy on sale for 2 euros it a amazing game

This game is interesting... 
It feels less like the original SiN game and more like a Half-Life 2 DLC, which isn't a bad thing, especially if you love Half-Life. But in this case it REALLY feels like it to me. 
Even a lot of the aesthetics and such in the game are very reminiscent. 
Many items just feel like leftovers from Half-Life: 
the boxes, chain-link fences, cinder block walls, alleyways, posters. 
The Source engine is great, and the game has aged better because of it. 
Regardless, the graphics and gameplay have just aged "okay", but still feel clunky and dated in contrast to Half-Life. 

Unlike the original game, there's no pre-rendered third-person cutscenes and now you have a "quiet" protagonist like Gordon Freeman. I suppose they were trying to make it more serious and contemporary for 2006? 
The game is in constant first-person mode with in-game cutscenes. 
It clashes harshly stylistically from the original game. 
While still being good, it should have done a little bit more to distance itself from Half-Life. 

The latter half of the game feels more epic and aesthetically similar to the original game. 
Unfortunately it ended right when it was getting interesting. 

* Also, the main menu soundtrack is awesome. 

It's a very short game (roughly 2~4 hours). 
And ends on a cliffhanger to a sequel that will most likely never come out. 
Sad that it'll never be finished, because the game is decent, and I'm curious where the story would have gone.

A classic FPS from 1998 running on modified Quake II engine that was greatly overshadowed by another FPS game that came out about 2 weeks after it. That shooter, was Half-Life.

The plot of SiN is set in 2037 “megacity” where you play as John Blade, a special forces operative trying to stop SinTEK corporation from distributing a drug capable of turning people into mutants. Having played it recently for the first time, I get now why this game is often mentioned as one of the better shooters of the 90’s. Few tips to save you some time: You can change FoV in the console by pressing ~ and typing e.g. “FOV 110”. The game doesn’t clearly tell you that you can loot bodies (press E button), the more damage you deal to an enemy by e.g. destroying their armour the less loot you get from them. Save often, there are no autosaves or checkpoints and sometimes one failed jump will force you to restart entire level if you haven’t saved manually.

I haven’t encountered any major glitches or crashes in my ~7h playthrough although I’ve noticed that AI sometimes would simply stop working if you engaged it from a longer distance, which you can unintentionally “cheese” in certain boss fight.

A remaster is currently being made by Nightdive Studios called SiN Reloaded, although that game was supposed to come out in 2021 and there’s still no release almost 5 years later. I wouldn’t wait for that version and just buy this one if you’re interested in the game.

[i]DISCLAIMER: Before playing, I recommend installing the [b]Dominatrix source port[/b] by Rohit and playing with that instead, since it fixes some bugs and resolution issues on modern hardware. ALSO TO NOTE: the Wages of Sin expansion might NOT WORK for you with Dominatrix!!! For me it keeps crashing on mission 5 and idk how to fix it (I suspect it is because I use the source port), so keep that in mind.[/i]
[hr][/hr]
Great game, an enjoyable classic! It's impressive what this game pulls off for its time, especially considering it released two months before the first half life, a decision that lead to people completely forgetting about this game, sadly.

The level's architecture changes depending on your actions, which also affect what levels you'll be playing, along with maybe some cutscenes, which is quite ahead of it's time. The characters and story are the cheesiest thing I've ever seen, but Blade is actually rather memorable as a protagonist, JC too. And of course Elixis for her...big, generous heart. The guns are punchy, shooting enemies often sends them flying or blows them to pieces (the shotgun is GREAT stuff), so it gets an extra style point from me for this. Good soundtrack, sets the mood well enough.

Now, as for the [i]Wages of Sin expansion[/i] made by the 2015 team....I would love to say I also enjoyed that, but the thing refuses to work correctly. It gives me a hard crash on mission 5 when I try to leave the club, and I can't find a way to fix it. What a bummer...I was liking it too.

If you loved half-life, try this one out, totally worth it. Shame this franchise didn't get the continuation it deserved. For now, we'll have to wait for the remaster.
This game is an all time classic. Which is why you should buy it now BEFORE Slopgate and Nightdive Delist it, slap ugly A.I. Upscaled textures on it, and sell it back to you for $40.

It ran fine for me.

Cheesy 90s FPS Game. 
Not much more to ask from it. 
A solid 90s shooter: Simple, cheesy, dumb, straight to the point. 
A little clunky in contrast to Quake or Duke Nukem. 
Overshadowed by infinitely better games from the era, but a small time-capsule into what once was. 
Cool gunplay. 

The "badass" 90s action protagonist constantly spewing one-liners and making corny jokes. 

The game isn't too long; I think most people could beat it within 3~6 hours. 
The Expansion pack "Wages Of SiN" is pretty solid. I think it's actually better than the original game to be honest. 

My complaint with the game is that it's very dark. 
I have trouble seeing what's going on in certain parts. 
The level design in certain areas is somewhat annoying, whereas other times it's intuitive. A mixed bag for me personally. 
Sometimes to progress through certain levels is confusing, and I had to look online for tips to complete a level. 
Also, the menu interface is pretty bad too.

An OK FPS package (it includes the original game and its expansion) with great 2000's aesthetic. It's made with the Quake 2 engine and it follows a similar structure, with primary and secondary missions, and some optional maps depending on what you do. Overall is an enjoyable experience, but it certainly lacks some polish. Some enemy placement and behavior can feel a little unfair. If you like FPS from the late 90s/early 2000s, and you've already played the classics, don't hesitate in traying SiN. If you are not a fan of this experiences or can't get in the mindset of the era to play it, maybe wait for the upcoming remaster.

I paid extremely low money for this game, and I want to like it more however it is perhaps one of the jankiest feeling games I have ever played. Even compared to some other older games notably half-life this game feels horrible to play. I'm struggling to get through the game (I'm only on the 3rd level lmao) , and if things like movement felt better I'd probably continue playing. It's just that this seems like a cool game held back limitations of it's time. (Also why does every model have like this weird wobbly effect on it? It's driving me nuts.) I probably will revisit this but again it just feels horrible, even if the rest of the game seems decent. I think a game like this would greatly be better if it was remastered. Though I guess I can't complain too much, I got this game for (I think) less than a dollar. Just to be clear for a game as cheap as it is I think it is good, but just be aware that it feels janky, and many other comments mention things in regards to the technical side of the game.

Uplink is a pretty realistic hacking sim where the government is 100 km from your house and approaching rapidly. 

Use the inherent weaknesses in IPv4 and telephone systems to make them think your house is elsewhere for long enough that you can destroy the evidence of your crimes. But be warned! Your computer is only as powerful as a potato, you've left logs all over the planet, and if you're still not quite done what you need to, investigators may take MONTHS to hit you with a game over.

In this way, the game has true consequences, akin to an ironman or hardcore mode from today. You can't just start over from an older save, because the feds do not announce their investigation. Sure maybe the last save is OK, or perhaps you were had a few months prior. Maybe you just forgot a single logfile and there's still time, or maybe you were hacking five systems at once in a fugue state and just directly connected to one about a week back.
20 years later and it is still the best hacker game on the market.

Digital dreams, crossing over the sea. Welcome to the deadlock of reality!

A glimpse of an idea how people thought internet will be, before Wi-Fi, MFA and IoT took over.

Dangerous Waters is a demanding and uncompromising military simulation that remains one of the most detailed portrayals of modern naval combat ever released on PC. Developed by Sonalysts and published by Strategy First, the game was never designed to appeal to a broad audience. Instead, it targets players who crave realism, procedural depth, and a genuine understanding of how modern naval warfare actually functions. From the moment you step into your first mission, it is clear that Dangerous Waters prioritizes authenticity over accessibility, asking players to think like real-world naval officers rather than arcade pilots or action heroes.

The gameplay revolves around commanding a wide range of naval platforms drawn from contemporary military arsenals, including nuclear-powered submarines, diesel-electric submarines, surface combatants, maritime patrol aircraft, and helicopters. Each platform can be controlled at multiple stations, allowing players to jump between sonar, navigation, fire control, radar, and command roles as needed. Rather than simplifying these systems, the game models them with remarkable fidelity, forcing players to interpret sonar contacts, manage sensor noise, plot intercept courses, and carefully plan weapon deployments. Combat is rarely immediate or flashy; instead, it unfolds slowly, often over long stretches of tense silence punctuated by moments of sudden, decisive action.

Submarine warfare is where Dangerous Waters truly distinguishes itself. The game places enormous emphasis on acoustic stealth, positioning, and patience, making undersea combat feel like a high-stakes chess match played in near darkness. Players must constantly balance speed against noise, depth against detection risk, and aggression against survivability. Identifying a contact is rarely straightforward, as sonar returns can be ambiguous or misleading, demanding careful analysis and cross-referencing. This creates a level of psychological tension that few games manage to replicate, where victory often comes from outthinking the enemy rather than overpowering them.

Surface and air warfare add further layers of complexity. Managing a surface ship requires attention to radar coverage, missile defense, and coordination with allied units, while aircraft operations introduce entirely different tactical considerations such as search patterns, fuel management, and sensor range. The game encourages players to think holistically, combining assets rather than relying on a single platform. Success often depends on information superiority — knowing where the enemy is before they know where you are — reinforcing the game’s core theme that modern naval combat is as much about data and detection as it is about firepower.

The structure of Dangerous Waters supports long-term engagement through campaigns, standalone missions, and a powerful mission editor. Campaigns place players within broader geopolitical conflicts, lending narrative context and strategic continuity to individual encounters. The mission editor, meanwhile, has played a major role in the game’s longevity, enabling a dedicated community to create and share custom scenarios that expand the experience far beyond the original content. This community-driven ecosystem has helped keep the game relevant years after its release, especially among hardcore simulation enthusiasts.

Presentation is utilitarian and reflects the game’s age, but it serves its purpose effectively. Visuals focus on clarity rather than spectacle, with much of the action conveyed through tactical displays, sonar waterfalls, and radar screens rather than cinematic views. While the 3D graphics and interface feel dated by modern standards, they remain functional and readable. Sound design plays a critical role, particularly in submarine operations, where subtle audio cues can mean the difference between remaining hidden and being detected. The inclusion of voice command support further reinforces the simulation mindset, allowing players to interact with systems in a way that feels surprisingly immersive.

The most significant barrier to entry is the game’s learning curve. Dangerous Waters demands time, study, and patience, often requiring players to consult manuals or community resources to fully understand its systems. Tutorials are limited, and the game assumes a level of prior knowledge that can be intimidating for newcomers. However, for those willing to invest the effort, this complexity becomes one of its greatest strengths, offering a sense of mastery that few modern games attempt to deliver.

Ultimately, Dangerous Waters stands as a benchmark for realism-driven naval simulations. It is not flashy, forgiving, or easy to approach, but it offers an unparalleled depth of tactical and procedural gameplay that continues to reward serious players. While casual gamers may find it overwhelming or outdated, those with an interest in military simulation, naval strategy, or submarine warfare will discover an experience that remains intellectually engaging and deeply satisfying even years after its original release.

Yes, WITH the caveat that you engaged with the Disciples III plot and circumstance and enjoyed it. The gameplay loop is slightly different, and the hostile 2002 UX can be a turn off if you were already iffy on the genre. For me personally to get fully settled in took about ~6 hours, perhaps a little more. This is not a bad first Disciples game, but if this is your first foray into the genre, I might suggest something a little more accessible.
Despite appearances, the game's story and lore is LEAGUES better than Disciples III, and I really like III. I'll say nothing about Liberation since I don't think it's comparable. The grittiness of each faction's situation is delivered well, and your actions carrying over from past levels can effect you in your future. I was engaged for exposition dumps, and individual characters had many hooks to them. I really like the character designs and how many of them break away from stereotypes (though some of them are a bit funny looking, they're their own quirks lol).
Please don't be too turned off by appearances, if you really need incentive, you can add it to your list of games you've played released prior to 2005 and feel superior.

Enjoyable turn-based RTS with strong elements of hero building with itemization, leveling up your mobs, etc.
minor technical issues with it but once you get it running its still great

Yes, WITH the caveat that you engaged with the Disciples III plot and circumstance and enjoyed it. The gameplay loop is slightly different, and the hostile 2002 UX can be a turn off if you were already iffy on the genre. For me personally to get fully settled in took about ~6 hours, perhaps a little more. This is not a bad first Disciples game, but if this is your first foray into the genre, I might suggest something a little more accessible.
Despite appearances, the game's story and lore is LEAGUES better than Disciples III, and I really like III. I'll say nothing about Liberation since I don't think it's comparable. The grittiness of each faction's situation is delivered well, and your actions carrying over from past levels can effect you in your future. I was engaged for exposition dumps, and individual characters had many hooks to them. I really like the character designs and how many of them break away from stereotypes (though some of them are a bit funny looking, they're their own quirks lol).
Please don't be too turned off by appearances, if you really need incentive, you can add it to your list of games you've played released prior to 2005 and feel superior.

I wish there was more of this. Just a simple 6 vs 6 turn-based battles with dark and gothic art style and music. Maybe some minor improvements to the formula and upscaling of graphical fidelity, but nothing else. 
This game is underappreciated.

Flavor - 10/10 (original dark fantasy, consistent and very immersive)
Story - 10/10
2d Graphics - 10/10 (better than HOMM3 I would say, original to say at least)
3d graphics - great animations, but the game is old af. Still, the age of these 3d fights graphics are absolutely not a problem and work well with overall feel of the game.
Game-play - Campaings are amazing and flavourful. There is some replayability outside campaigns, but it is WAY below the level of HOMM3. Strategies are pretty limited due to the design of the combat system. This leaves single player skirmishes pretty boring fast once your start to get how the AI works and the AI is pretty dumb. There are a decent number of scenarios created by the dev that you can enjoy.
Editor - There is a map editor that is pretty straightforward to use and create your own scenarios, which is pretty cool.

Overall great game, same for it's expansion standalone.
Change your monitor resolution to 800x600 BEFORE launching. Great game, if you played it as a kid. Not really the move for a modern player

Pros - One of the few tanks sims that exists. Limited but fairly accurate and enjoyable, with high degree of customizability for realism settings. Includes mission/map/object editor. Co-op multiplayer allows up to 3 people to command a single tank.

Cons - Confusing and likely not enjoyable for anyone who doesn't like simulators or has no clue about tanks/armored warfare. General clunkiness and quite dated. Good luck finding any user maps.

There is a demo for this game, if you're on the fence track down a copy and give it a try.

It's one of the few tank sims that actually exists so it doesn't have much to be compared to. It's reasonably accurate and entertaining but is extremely clunky and poorly programmed, going by how many loading screens you'll go through switching from menu to menu. Customizing individual graphical/realism options (other than putting it to low/medium/high/etc.) requires you to close the game and run a cfg.bat in the game directory.

Despite being old and with a questionable build quality I haven't had any mysterious crashes or other game breaking bugs or issues despite running it on windows 7 64 bit, which actually makes it more stable than other current titles I play. The only glaring bug I've found is that you cannot reload the coaxial machinegun at all, the only workaround being enabling infinite ammunition which also gives you infinite shells for your main weapon.

It's also quite challenging, and it's not helped by the default user settings having the enemy AI at quite a high level. You can't rely on your armor in the first few missions and will frequently have to restart due to your crew bailing out after a single hit. The AI will also spot you much earlier than your AI commander will be able to spot them. Thankfully there are a lot of options for realism settings and you can pick and choose until you end up with something you're comfortable with.

The manual has plenty of information but is missing one or two details like the penetration of munitions used in game, and it was probably also in color at one point and has not survived the shift to black and white.

There's nothing here for anyone who does not enjoy simulators, as while it is fairly streamlined compared to what it could be it still is a sim. It is only 5 dollars and there is not exactly a glut of other simulators to choose from. Find a copy of the demo if you're on the fence about it.

Okay, I decied to write a short review on this game.
Currently the game is only 1,24 Euro, and after a few days the game goes back to its original 5 E.
I'm writing this down because the price is definitely a big plus. As of the game itself, you will probably like it if you're into tanks and/or simulators. I am. Overall, I am quite pleased with the game. However, this is heavily influenced by its price. For 1,2 Euro, it's a great deal. For 5 Euro, I'm not really sure. You decide.

Pros:
+Terrain can be deformed (craters after explosions, you can dig tank trenches [UBER COOL] etc)
+Ability to demolish buildings is interesting, reminds me of Frostbite :D
+Spectator mode allows you to see where exactly did the shells hit the tank, which is both interesting and useful.
+I like the thermal/nightvision scope :3
+Rather easy to learn
+Ability to issue orders to other squads/tanks (though clunky)
+DAT PRICE

Cons:
-Uninteresting mission design (go over there, kill a tank on the way, you win)
-Only three (playable) tanks
-No tank interior, only HUD
-Bad functionality.. (the game menu and the game itself are separate programs, there are some issues with settings etc)
-Does not support Steam Overlay (that is to be expected I guess)
-MULTIPLAYER NOT WORKING!! (if anyone got it to work, PM me please)
-Not too interesting, yet definitely original setting
-I feel as if the controls are a bit clunky... Maybe you get used to it as time passes.
-Physics seem broken if you happen to glitch it
-Not too many missions (you can download some from the internet)
-Translation from Russian is funny - why is there "Made in USSR" written on the speedometer? Also, the azbuka  abbreviations to English is weird. Manual covers the explanations though.


Overall, the game feels like a low-budget title of some minor Russian developer, but it can be clearly seen they did their best. 
I'm not sure whether I can call this game a real "simulator". It's more of a really realistic tank game (which is maybe even better for me, since you learn the ropes faster, yet it keeps the satisfaction from realistic experience). 
So, yeah. You decide. I don't regret getting this.
Remember comrades, we are tank! They take out treads, we are artillery! They take out main gun, we are pillbox! They take out machine gun, we are bunker! They take out armor, we are herores!

So lets get this strait. This is a tank simulator. If your expecting the arcade and simple to use controls of world of tanks or war thunder than look somewhere else.

All in all this is a good game, a dam hard game but fun. The way it works is dont just control the tank outright but you control its crew. You can either take cantrol of a crew position directly such as the driver or gunner but you also give the crew orders when not in controll of them. So lets say your playing as the gunner, you need move the tank to a new position but you can't leave the gun, so you order your driver to move. Ordering crewmen around is not as accurect nor as easy as controlling them directly but you will need to get the hang of it

The positions of crew are as follows

Commander- does little in combat but is resposible for scanning for enemys and if you want to completly controll the tank via orders only, you play as him

Gunner- mans the main gun, controlls the turret and coaxile MG, nuff said

Driver- drives the tank of corse

AA gunner- mans the big MG on top of the tank to deal with infantry, light vehicals and aircraft.

This game is also extreeemly relistic as far as a simulator game from 2006 goes. Damage to your tank and is permanant so if you get your track blown off or your gun disabled part way through the mission, your done. Aiming the gun also reqiers you to find range and work with the flight path of the shell. While there is ways to have the details figured out atomaticly, for the most part you will be manualy calculating each shot. driving phsyics are heavy as well, go off a cliff, you break your track, go too fast around a bend, flip the tank.

As one finaly note you actully have three tanks not just a T-72. You have T-34-85 a T-55 and yes a T-72

So all in all Iron warriors T-72 Tank command is a fun game, It's extreemly hard and defently dated to an extent but if your looking for a realistic military themed game or just like advanced simulators in general, this game is for you.

UPDATE- I should mention that I run this on windows 10 and it runs ok. People have reported problems with getting this game to run but for any older PC game running on newer operating systems thats to be expected. So while I have had no problems with it that is not saying the game won't have troubles depending on your PC set up.

Every other mission something critical bugs out:

-Turning lever stuck halfway
-Autoloader crapping out 

10/10 realism for Soviet tanks.

Would highly recommend.

Bought this game just to write a review. I am a veteran of this game. It was my first shooter. If i can remember correctly i got this game near the end of 2005/begining of 2006. The name was still T-72 Balkans on Fire. 

I could master this game 11/12 years ago, I was a little kid back then. Because of this game i've started loving the tanks. I have played WoT, WarThunder and other games with tanks. But those arent real. You just move with WASD, and usually you just shoot where your mouse is.

Here, you have 4 types of shells - BPS (armor piercing), OFS (fragmentation shell), BKS (High explosive) and UR (Guided missile).
Every shell (and for each tank) should be aimed differentely. Also you can edit the levels of realism in cfg (Steam\steamapps\common\Iron Warriors\cfg) which is nice. 

Even tho this game is very dated, it still has a place in my heart. 
If you like tanks and you dont have a lot of money to spend on them. Buy this game.
Given the price I bough this game for I am very happy thus far. The graphics are underwhelming and the AI is crazy, but in such a starved genre of game it will certainly suffice.

Found this in the recent Specials section of the store for 75p !  Although an old title it ran with no issues on Windows 10. The title although getting on agewise is still very playable and if you are more about actual gameplay elements you can soon dismiss the graphics side as they still hold up well enough and the atmosphere of the game / sim is strong enough for this. Though guessing when originally released the title was fair impressive - see screenshots on store page. Every station of the tanks modelled are playable with sight / ammo modelling. The sim itself is based primarily on the Serbian / Croatian war. T-72, T-52 and T-34 are what you can control with vehicles such as bmp / hind and jets making an appearance along with artillery and infantry.  The title i've read was created by the devs who went on to form Graviteam and you can see the resemblance / style straight away with the loading screen and features such as unit encyclopedia. The game also has a custom mission editor included.    
    So to sum up it's the fore runner in many ways to Steel Armor Blaze of war and Steel Fury. For the sale price, although surpassed and obsolete against the newer titles it was worth it to me to see the early steps towards these later games and it's still a playable game in it's own right with Tanks rarely seen / playable as in games. So can recommend.

Do you like tanks and willing spend time learned about them?  Do you want to experience driving Russian tanks?  
Buy this game

Do you prefer simple fps like Call of Duty or Battlefield?  Do you enjoy shiny new graphics and pretty gimmicks? 
Don’t buy it.

Seriously, this game is not for amateurs.  Your shells will not magically land right on the center of your cross hair.  You will have to change gear while driving.  You will have to learn how to use your sights.  You will have to restart the entire mission if you drive too fast and broke your tracks.

It certainly doesn't hold up to modern standards as well as I'd like it to, but it's good.

The physics are a bit... silly.... try spamming the handbrake....... you'll see what I mean. 

As for a game, It works. So ... in reference to the other games, it's pretty good.

It's a good story driven (no dynamic mission) tank sim that's marred by a number of glaring errors. Notably, the AI crew members are ABSOLUTELY BRAINDEAD. The driver is incapable of driving in a straight line, the gunner cannot spot targets right in front of him, and the commander has a habit of always looking backwards for some reason. Therefore the ONLY way to really play this is game is to play as driver, commander, AND gunner, alternating between the seats. 

Yet somehow, the enemy AI's accuracy is tuned WAY UP. RPG gunners never miss and anti-tank guns can shoot you through rain and fog, before you can even see them (and yes I had my night optics turned on).

It's like the milsim version of Desert Bus, a game deliberately designed to piss you off. Overall not worth the effort except as a historical curiosity, an early faltering attempt at a game by the developers who would later go on to make the acclaimed Graviteam Tactics.

It's a tank simulator that is from 2006 but plays like it was from 1996 at best with its controls and limitations. Honestly, Operation Flashpoint was a better tank simulation and less infuriating and useless AI.

While subsequent Graviteam tanksims (Steel Fury Kharkov 1942 and Steel Armor Blaze of War) made successive and significant improvements to visuals and gameplay, this is still a rather good little package, especially at the low asking price. The missions in the nicely story-driven and character-based campaign are small-scale, including several where you're essentially on your own and up against weapons which will make short work of whatever tank you have (starting with the T-34-85). The implementation of the driver, gunner and commander roles is quite detailed, engaging and convincing and the sim delivers an excellent feeling of rumbling around in and operating a tank, especially the star, the ubiquitous T-72. Apply the Gastello mod to improve significantly the 3d models, scenery and sights (this disables the T34-85 as 'playable', and will return the in-game menus to Russian, but the top-level one is still in English and a fix is being looked into for the others). Read the manual and use the file cfg.bat to fine-tune settings that can't be touched in-game, including (in my case) setting the display to 1920x1080.

First and foremost, it's realistic and it enables multiple players within the same vehicle. It's also not hard on my system. Unfortunately, the interface in the tank is a little clunky and the manual does not describe it well. Graphics are not that great either. It needs patches for WIndows 10 and an easier way to play online.

Okay so it is fair to say this game is not pick up and play. It is a full on tank sim, and you will want to read the manual and spend some time in the driving training mission just getting to know the commands, and how to control your tank. 

For the money you get a pretty decent tank sim, with a fair amount of content. An 18 mission campaign, some quick missions and an editor to create more missions of your own. Graphics for their age are not that bad in all honesty, and the game even has limited terrain deformation. Nice. 

So the negatives. The controls and interface are not exactly user friendly, and the AI can be quite wacky at times.
