One of the classics, truly a favorite. Big cult following. Absolutely worth the money - but be prepared for a little bit of tinkering. The magic system will feel impossible to get right at first without mods - this is not due to a learning curve or intended difficulty but rather is a side-effect of the age of the game. Basically what happens is you cast spells by actually drawing the symbols using your mouse (which I think is extremely cool) and this worked perfectly on older hardware but on modern hardware something about the higher framerates confuses the character recognition system in the game and causes it to not recognize what spell you are trying to cast and basically fails each time. Instead of letting this turn you away: buy the game and then google "Arx Libertatis". This is a modification to the game that makes it fully functional with modern hardware and enables you to port your purchased copy over to any system you want - Apple Silicon Macs, Potato PCs, Nintendo Switch, even the PS Vita! This game is an important piece of history, by far one of the best purchases you could possibly get for just $5.

This might be one of the coolest RPGs (maybe even games in general) I've ever played. 

There are so many unqiue systems here and they all work really well together. You cast spells by drawing them, you use your inventory by opening an interface and physically dragging stuff from the game world into it, you can use physics-based objects (so basically anything) as distractions, you can combine items to make new ones and so on. The game is also not purely just combat and quests, but it's also a lot of puzzle-solving.

I think the best way to describe Arx Fatalis is a combination of RPG and point'n'click. And in true point'n'click fashion, the game can be confusing as hell at times. Nonsensical clues and no idea where to go. Which is one of the game's weakest aspects (in my opinion) alongside the not-so-great melee combat. I was able to beat most of the game without a walkthrough, but nearing the end of the game there were certain parts I really couldn't figure out. Also, looking at the walkthrough, it seems like I missed out on a *bunch* of sidequests. 

The story and world are very interesting but narratively I think this game falls a bit short. Certainly a product of its time. Despite the occasional jank and my slight complaints and skill issues, this still holds up really well and it's definitely worth a playthrough. And at 5€ it's basically a steal.

When people say this game is underrated they mean it! Arkane was really ahead of their time back in the early days when they were still starting off and Arx really shows it, Arx is a first person dungeon crawler and immersive game with a heavy influence and inspiration from Ultima underworld. The plot is pretty simple and interesting enough to follow along with the lore building though the game feels short and by the end I am left wanting for more of this world so its a shame that we won't be revisiting it or getting a sequel.

Of course Arx isn't perfect and being one of Arkane's early games it has jank here and there, the controls take a bit to get a hang of but once you do its pretty smooth, melee combat is simple and has no flair, and the magic system while being its strong point can be unwieldy since you have to draw runes to cast magic. But if you can get past the jank, the controls and how old this game is then getting into arx isn't difficult.

One last thing though, I recommend downloading arx libertatis, a open source, improved, cross platform and open source engine for the game, it will help make your experience more smoother as it allows the game to be more compatible and supported for modern systems as well as eliminating bugs and limitations. If you download it your computer will say its a virus but having downloaded and used it myself I can confidently say its not a virus and there is a steam discussion page about this issue which you can easily search for. That's all and hopefully this review helped you in trying out this game.

Criminally underrated and timeless hidden gem. It’s been in my library, probably from a Humble Bundle, for who knows how long, yet it unexpectedly became one of my favourite games ever. It is what I would consider a masterclass in contemporary atmospheric game design. The soundtracks, aesthetics and ambience across the levels are, quite literally, on another level. Creepy, hollow droning noises in the dungeons make you keep your weapon drawn; the ratmen made me jump more than once. Hitting the teleport back to the main area feels relieving, because that oppressive atmosphere gives way to hopeful, heavenly synthwork and sparse but lively NPC activity.

Sure, some of it may seem outdated, janky or even unfinished compared to what we play today. But take a chance and see the game for what it is. Something so densely packed with nuanced, interlocking mechanics and gameplay (especially for a closed-loop game that’s only about 40+ hours long) that a modern publisher would probably start sweating about “risk” and “market viability” lol

Super-clean, crisp, adequate combat system. NPC dialogue is very charming and the AI still holds up in places. The inventory system is more sophisticated and sometimes requires more deliberation than Elder Scrolls games. The mix of free-look and stationary point-and-click is blissful.

A classic imsim rpg that was obviously supposed to be Ultima Underworld 3. Though UU was more straightforward with the quest goals and progression, being overally much easier. If you want to play then Arx Libertatis is a must, because the runes you draw for spells can be much easier recognized with its fix and you can scale up the UI. If you want to have a comfortable run, then don't make an archer because the tube of arrows is an item that needs to be fixed, it vanishes after it has fully degraded and the game has only a few of them. Pure melee build will be like fighting with a pillow by the endgame. Whereas the harm and fireball spells can be so good they feel busted. Whatever your playstyle is, then mix it with magic for a good measure. Don't forget to keep a bow with you or in your chests because you need it for a couple of puzzles in the lower floors. Always keep the 2 meteorite fragments you get (3 if you're strong enough that early). Levitate, reveal and dispel field spells are needed for game progression, so keep the scrolls if you can't cast them. Also keep an enchant item scroll if your magic is not really high, because higher level items need a higher magic level. As you can see the game design with its potential softlocks is patchy, but the sound design and atmosphere are still superbly tied to an intriguing game world.

I was able to beat most of the game without a walkthrough, but nearing the end of the game there were certain parts I really couldn't figure out. Also, looking at the walkthrough, it seems like I missed out on a *bunch* of sidequests. 

The story and world are very interesting but narratively I think this game falls a bit short. Certainly a product of its time. Despite the occasional jank and my slight complaints and skill issues, this still holds up really well and it's definitely worth a playthrough. And at 5€ it's basically a steal.

When people say this game is underrated they mean it! Arkane was really ahead of their time back in the early days when they were still starting off and Arx really shows it, Arx is a first person dungeon crawler and immersive game with a heavy influence and inspiration from Ultima underworld. The plot is pretty simple and interesting enough to follow along with the lore building though the game feels short and by the end I am left wanting for more of this world so its a shame that we won't be revisiting it or getting a sequel.

Of course Arx isn't perfect and being one of Arkane's early games it has jank here and there, the controls take a bit to get a hang of but once you do its pretty smooth, melee combat is simple and has no flair, and the magic system while being its strong point can be unwieldy since you have to draw runes to cast magic. But if you can get past the jank, the controls and how old this game is then getting into arx isn't difficult.

One last thing though, I recommend downloading arx libertatis, a open source, improved, cross platform and open source engine for the game, it will help make your experience more smoother as it allows the game to be more compatible and supported for modern systems as well as eliminating bugs and limitations. If you download it your computer will say its a virus but having downloaded and used it myself I can confidently say its not a virus and there is a steam discussion page about this issue which you can easily search for. That's all and hopefully this review helped you in trying out this game.

Arx Fatalis is a great early immersive RPG. In 2002 it was WAAAAAAY ahead of it's time and I still remember being stunned about what you could do like cooking, blacksmithing, casting spells by tracing runes in the air and so on.
Is it a bit janky? Absolutely!
Is the sound mixing totally off? What?! I couldn't hear you because the background music was drowning out the whole dialogue.
Are the controls reminescent of the first System Shock, so totally horrible? Yes, and they even kept the switching between interacting and fighting.
Is it still fun? Hell yeah!
But please save yourself the headache and install Arx Libertatis from the get go and get a HD texture pack from Nexus Mods. Apart from that the game still works great!

It's a game that has some extremely genius design buried under terrible quality of life and general gameplay issues. Apart from the obnoxious registration upon first startup, there is a lot of good and a lot of bad here.

I've never seen faction design this inspired, especially in terms of base building/space management. Vehicles are modular, based on what faction you play you might need different armor or weapons, and it's all just really interesting to play. Factions are radically different in terms of focus, you get tanks and vehicles, futuristic hovercraft, biological nightmares or hordes or battle mechs.

- The Eurasian Dynasty builds large, interconnected complexes that all have to be connected via tubes

- The Lunar Corp can call down buildings from orbit anywhere on the map, but only ever 5 buildings can be queued at the same time, their man buildings are stackable from several modules

- The UCS builds large energy networks across the map, and is based on decentralization and modularity

- Aliens don't have any buildings except defense, they literally just clone or mutate from the same basic alien, literally no game up to this day has done anything more innovative in terms of faction design

Each faction gets a campaign, and missions are interestingly challenging, but do not vary much. The story and dialogue are sub-par through and through, and the biggest issue of this game is the terrible unit AI and pathfinding. The maps are generally small and units do not retain formation or cohesion, greatly detracting from the gameplay.

With some more polish this game could have easily been a gem, because it did bring the genre forward, but nobody noticed it because its innovations and ingenuity were buried under a layer of terrible UX.

Earth 2160 is a dense and ambitious real-time strategy experience that reflects a period when RTS games were unafraid to challenge players with complexity, scale, and long-form strategic thinking. Developed by Reality Pump Studios and published by Topware Interactive in partnership with ACE, the game represents the culmination of the Earth series’ ideas, delivering a sprawling sci-fi conflict that prioritizes depth and player agency over accessibility. Even years after its release, it remains a distinctive entry in the genre, particularly appealing to players who enjoy methodical planning, layered systems, and campaign-driven RTS design.

The setting of Earth 2160 is unapologetically grand in scope. Humanity has already lost its homeworld, and the surviving factions are locked in a struggle for dominance across hostile environments such as Mars, the Moon, and alien-infested territories. The narrative unfolds across multiple interconnected campaigns, each told from the perspective of a different faction, allowing players to experience the conflict from opposing ideological and strategic viewpoints. Political maneuvering, betrayals, and the looming threat of the alien Morphidian race give the story a sense of progression and consequence, even if the storytelling itself is delivered in a utilitarian, RTS-style presentation rather than cinematic spectacle.

Gameplay is where Earth 2160 truly distinguishes itself. The resource system is more intricate than the genre norm, requiring players to manage water, metal, and crystals, each serving different purposes in construction, research, and upkeep. Bases are modular and highly customizable, with structures and units built from interchangeable components that directly affect their battlefield performance. This design philosophy encourages experimentation and long-term planning, as early research choices can lock players into certain technological paths while closing off others. Rather than simply producing stronger versions of existing units, technological advancement fundamentally reshapes how an army operates, rewarding foresight and strategic commitment.

Combat emphasizes combined arms tactics and careful unit composition. Each faction has its own mechanical identity, with distinct movement styles, weapon systems, and technological advantages that demand different approaches to battle. Terrain, line of sight, and positioning matter greatly, and reckless mass production is rarely enough to secure victory. Battles often unfold at a deliberate pace, giving players time to react, reposition, and adapt rather than relying on split-second micromanagement alone. This measured approach reinforces the game’s strategic tone, making victories feel earned through planning rather than reflex.

The campaign structure reinforces this depth by frequently introducing new mechanics, objectives, and constraints that force players to rethink their strategies. Missions can be lengthy and demanding, sometimes requiring careful resource balancing and defensive planning before any meaningful offensive push can begin. While this can occasionally lead to slower pacing, it also enhances the sense of scale and investment, making each successful mission feel like a significant accomplishment rather than a quick skirmish.

Visually, Earth 2160 shows its age, but it remains impressive within the context of its era. The fully 3D engine was ambitious at the time, allowing for detailed unit models, large-scale battles, and varied planetary environments. Explosions, weapon effects, and environmental lighting still convey a sense of weight and power, even if modern players may notice dated textures and animations. The audio design complements this well, with a moody soundtrack and functional sound effects that reinforce the militaristic tone without overwhelming the player.

Multiplayer and skirmish modes extend the game’s lifespan beyond the campaign, offering opportunities for competitive and cooperative play across a variety of maps. While the online community is far smaller today than it was at launch, these modes remain a solid way to explore the game’s systems without narrative constraints. AI skirmishes, in particular, allow players to test experimental builds and strategies, further highlighting the flexibility of the game’s mechanics.

That said, Earth 2160 demands patience. Its interface, tutorials, and overall user experience reflect older RTS design sensibilities, and new players may find the learning curve steep. Pathfinding quirks, occasionally opaque mechanics, and a lack of modern quality-of-life features can create friction, especially for those accustomed to streamlined contemporary strategy games. However, for players willing to invest the time to understand its systems, these rough edges become manageable obstacles rather than deal-breaking flaws.

In the end, Earth 2160 stands as a deeply rewarding RTS for players who value strategic depth and long-term planning over instant gratification. It is not a game designed to ease players in gently, but one that challenges them to think several steps ahead, commit to meaningful decisions, and adapt to complex battlefield conditions. For fans of classic real-time strategy and those seeking a demanding, systems-heavy sci-fi experience, Earth 2160 remains a compelling and memorable journey into a future defined by survival, technology, and relentless conflict.

Two Worlds: Epic Edition is a gloriously flawed gem that I can't help but recommend, even while acknowledging its many shortcomings. The equipment stacking system is genuinely addictive — dragging a weaker sword onto a stronger one to combine them scratches an itch I didn't know I had — and the sprawling world of Antaloor has enough variety, from glacial peaks to volcanic deserts, to keep you exploring for hours. Yes, the voice acting is hilariously bad, the horse controls are a nightmare, and the main story is about as cliché as fantasy gets, but there's a scrappy charm to this game that modern RPGs simply can't replicate. Just be warned: save constantly, because crashes will come for you eventually. If you can grab it for a few dollars and you have a soft spot for janky 2007 RPGs, absolutely pick it up.

Overhated, I'll die on that hill. Part nostalgia I'll admit it, I liked this as a kid more than Oblivion and still do in many ways. No loading screens for buildings/cities, better loot/inventory/itemization, better or rather no level scaling(think more like Gothic/Morrowind), and an arguably better magic system. Everything else is objectively worse for the most part but I'm ok with that. Try it out, perchance....

Two Worlds is one of those old RPGs that sounds way more exciting than it actually is. The open world is huge and you can go anywhere right from the start, but a lot of that space feels empty and pointless, with many areas having nothing worth doing. After a while, exploring just stopped being interesting.

The visuals are mixed too. The water looks surprisingly nice for its age, but most environments are bland and overly similar, and cities very horizontal and are packed with NPCs that all look and sound the same. Most of them are completely useless, and having so many in one place caused noticeable stuttering for me.

Gameplay didn’t really save it either. The inventory limit is frustrating, combat feels clunky, and while unlimited merchant gold is convenient and combining armor together for better was interesting, it wasn't enough to make the game fun. Overall, it just wasn’t that enjoyable for me, and it definitely shows its age in ways that make it hard to recommend.

Forget about the NPCs in the reviews. This is an exciting and fun open world RPG highly inspired by the Gothic games. The strength doesn't lie in a perfect combat system or polished graphics, but rather a fullfilling experience of freedom and adventure, of exploration and exploits. Somebody doesn't let you into the village until you solved some quests or whatever - find a way to jump over the wall. The game wants you to follow this path...go into the opposite direction and see what you can find there. I made it to the very endboss in 10 hours but can't beat him so now I'm exploring the giant map, killing glacier orcs and whatnot, collecting interesting gear, growing. 

Big Bonus (I love it!): You start out as a pathetic little worm but will eventually destroy everything; no level scaling in your way (how it should be). I'm excited for the sequel.

Two Worlds: Epic Edition is a gloriously flawed gem that I can't help but recommend, even while acknowledging its many shortcomings. The equipment stacking system is genuinely addictive — dragging a weaker sword onto a stronger one to combine them scratches an itch I didn't know I had — and the sprawling world of Antaloor has enough variety, from glacial peaks to volcanic deserts, to keep you exploring for hours. Yes, the voice acting is hilariously bad, the horse controls are a nightmare, and the main story is about as cliché as fantasy gets, but there's a scrappy charm to this game that modern RPGs simply can't replicate. Just be warned: save constantly, because crashes will come for you eventually. If you can grab it for a few dollars and you have a soft spot for janky 2007 RPGs, absolutely pick it up.

If I need to describe the game in two words - an offline MMO. It's boring, repetitive, uninspired, without unique and distinctive features. It isn't worth your time even if you buy it on sale.

Witcher came out in the same year, and its combat system are probably slightly better, just because it works. However, in Two Worlds the dodge is the most broken mechanic I've ever seen. It might work properly in 1 vs 1 encounters, but it really "shines" when you fight 2+ foes, because your character starts dodging into enemies by doing this weird turn-around and the camera moves with him screwing you over even more.

Dialogues are pretty linear, and there are no way to affect a conversation in any meaningful way. What I mean is you can't bluff, threaten, lie, choose good or evil options, because you character does all the heavy lifting and he's intentionally? cocky as hell. Your role is to pick a line and watch a five-minute exchange of replies. On the bright side, they dubbed all the dialogues, so if you don't like reading, you might like it. 

If you see a 60-degree hill in front of you, don't even dare clamber over it. The character will literally move 1 foot per hour, and the best part is that enemies and animals aren't affected by the same rules at all. They can run on those slops as if they were completely flat.

Horses are the only way to open new areas fast unless you really like traversing through the map on foot, but you can't summon them unless they're really close, and you can't take them with you when you teleport. On top of that, horses do love getting stuck in objects. All those things together make them virtually useless. 

Fast travel is half-baked. Teleport stones are finite, and you have to place them on the ground to use, which means you have to retrieve them after that. So if you're in the middle of nowhere, you'd better keep moving to the nearest way point and avoid using the stones altogether. It leads to a lot of unnecessary and quite tedious backtracking.

Side quests are classic MMO garbage: kill 10 wolves, fetch moonshine, deliver a package, etc... There is nothing good about them, except a few funny lines once in a while. The main quest... idk. I didn't manage to finish it, but it didn't look promising either.

As you level up your defense and hit chance are going down, therefore you are forced to spend points on Dexterity or craft potions with permanent buffs. For example, on 71 lvl you have 63% Attack/Defense if you put 200 points into Dexterity (5 points per level). It doesn't seem to affect melee much, but it still baffles me why it exists.

Every hobo in every settlement has a unique name and three lines, but somehow they didn't add icons for merchants and questgivers. Good luck searching for them without a guide.

STRONGLY RECOMMENDED TO TRY FOR RPG AND FANTASY RPG LOVERS
Graphics & World For an old RPG, the visuals are surprisingly decent. With HDR on, lighting, water, flora, fauna, and environments look good. The world feels varied, with forests, broken forts, inns, villages, huts, hills, and dungeons. Not modern, but definitely respectable for its time.
Gameplay & Combat This is where the age shows. Combat feels janky and outdated. Shields can’t be actively used—blocking is stat-based. There’s no proper dodge button, only awkward jumping to avoid attacks, which can feel clumsy.
Traveling The map is large, and while transporters exist, there’s still a lot of traveling. Horses help, but you can’t summon or teleport them, and it’s easy to lose them when using transporters, making long journeys annoying.
RPG Systems & Content Weapon and armor variety is solid weapon enchantments are also good, magic (fire, air, earth), alchemy, and skills add depth. The world isn’t empty—NPCs give quests, teach skills, share lore, and make the world feel alive and interactive. The story so far is decent.
Conclusion
If you enjoy old-school RPGs and don’t mind outdated mechanics, Two Worlds: Epic Edition is worth trying.
Rating: 7.5 / 10

Two Worlds is one of those old RPGs that sounds way more exciting than it actually is. The open world is huge and you can go anywhere right from the start, but a lot of that space feels empty and pointless, with many areas having nothing worth doing. After a while, exploring just stopped being interesting.

The visuals are mixed too. The water looks surprisingly nice for its age, but most environments are bland and overly similar, and cities very horizontal and are packed with NPCs that all look and sound the same. Most of them are completely useless, and having so many in one place caused noticeable stuttering for me.

Gameplay didn’t really save it either. The inventory limit is frustrating, combat feels clunky, and while unlimited merchant gold is convenient and combining armor together for better was interesting, it wasn't enough to make the game fun. Overall, it just wasn’t that enjoyable for me, and it definitely shows its age in ways that make it hard to recommend.

At its best, Dark Messiah is an incredibly fun combat sandbox where your strongest weapons are guile and underhanded utilisation of the environment. Kicking enemies off cliffs, into spikes, or tripping them for an easy K.O is an absolute blast every time. If you think you'd enjoy a fantasy themed Home Alone game, I could see this being a favourite. Maps and exploration in Dark Messiah are reminiscent of Deus Ex (the original), though slightly less involved. Some areas drag, but you're mostly onto new scenery after fairly short intervals.

My biggest problem with Dark Messiah is it spends far too much of its runtime nowhere near its best. Fights against human-sized enemies are fun, but you often have to go through large stretches of fighting spiders or other such beasts, which are dull and uninteresting at best and irritating at all other times. Some sections (such as with the [spoiler]giant worm[/spoiler] ) are extremely frustrating, and even walkthroughs online repeatedly have to retry at these points.

Dark Messiah has some high highs, and some low lows. I'd say it's a game well worth playing, though finishing is a harder sell.

Criminally forgotten and deeply underrated, a real hidden gem.
Being completely honest, it does have its share of jank, but all things considered, for a 20-year-old game it’s still highly enjoyable.
I’ve often seen it described as an Action RPG, but that gives the wrong idea of what kind of game it actually is, in my opinion. This is a melee-focused FPS. The closest comparison I can make is that it’s basically a “medieval fantasy Half-Life 2.”

What I enjoyed about the game:

- The gameplay is brilliant. The varied moveset: kicking enemies into spikes or down pits, throwing barrels in their faces, or cutting their heads off with a slow-motion critical hit; is adrenaline-pumping and extremely satisfying.
- The level design is inspired. Most levels are well crafted both structurally and aesthetically. The game is linear, but it includes just enough side exploration (like secret rooms) to avoid turning into a boring corridor.
- Progression is spot on. The streamlined progression system encourages replayability without hurting balance or pacing.
- The plot is simple but creative. For an action game, even a straightforward good-vs-evil story would have been fine, but there’s something more interesting here.
[spoiler]You are the son of a demon and can choose to reject your lineage and save the world, or embrace it and conquer the world instead. Meanwhile, the antagonists (the necromancers) see death as a natural part of life and are willing to kill countless people “for the greater good”, in order to save the world from you and your father. The line between good and evil ends up being blurred in an interesting way.[/spoiler]
- Even after 20 years, the game is still unique. How many single-player, melee-focused, fantasy FPS games are there? You usually get multiplayer brawlers, traditional FPS games, or first-person RPGs. This game fills a niche that has been mostly ignored for years.

What could have been better:

- The balance on normal difficulty is all over the place. I would strongly suggest new players start on easy. In my opinion, the game shines the most when it lets you enjoy a power fantasy (being a mighty warrior mowing down enemies) while fully engaging with the combat system. On normal difficulty, however, my experience was often frustrating and relied heavily on trial and error. Environmental kills (spikes, pits) are guaranteed one-hit KOs, while standard melee combat often requires four or five fully charged hits to kill most enemies. Some enemies deal absurd damage, sometimes killing you in two hits even with a defense-focused build. On top of that, it’s often unclear when you’re supposed to block attacks, and sometimes blocking doesn’t seem to work at all. The result is enemies that feel both extremely spongy and extremely lethal. Combat ends up being overly dependent on environmental kills just to avoid taking damage. Positioning and environmental awareness are important, but the game is at its best when you can reliably use its entire moveset.
- Bugs and glitches can hurt the experience. I got permanently stuck a few times while using the rope. I also encountered a game-breaking bug during the final fight that forced me to replay it from the beginning, along with a couple of crashes.
- Running the game on modern systems is a hassle. You’ll need to follow guides to get it working, and even then performance can be rough, with frequent FPS drops.
- Various minor annoyances. Things like the shield being unequipped every time you switch to the bow, elemental weapons having annoying lighting and sound effects and occupying too much screen space, the lack of dedicated potion shortcuts, the annoying stealth reticle in the center of the screen, night vision being irritating in multiple ways and not lasting indefinitely despite the game often being very dark, and other small issues all add up.

A revised version of this game, with quality-of-life improvements, better balance, and proper bug fixes, could easily restore it to the masterpiece status it deserves. Even as it is, though, it remains a unique and genuinely enjoyable experience.

Download the mod manager, the advanced SDK and the restoration mod and you'll get it to work on modern systems. Took me 20 minutes, worth it for this one of a kind game, though it can be a bit difficult.

At its best, Dark Messiah is an incredibly fun combat sandbox where your strongest weapons are guile and underhanded utilisation of the environment. Kicking enemies off cliffs, into spikes, or tripping them for an easy K.O is an absolute blast every time. If you think you'd enjoy a fantasy themed Home Alone game, I could see this being a favourite. Maps and exploration in Dark Messiah are reminiscent of Deus Ex (the original), though slightly less involved. Some areas drag, but you're mostly onto new scenery after fairly short intervals.

My biggest problem with Dark Messiah is it spends far too much of its runtime nowhere near its best. Fights against human-sized enemies are fun, but you often have to go through large stretches of fighting spiders or other such beasts, which are dull and uninteresting at best and irritating at all other times. Some sections (such as with the [spoiler]giant worm[/spoiler] ) are extremely frustrating, and even walkthroughs online repeatedly have to retry at these points.

Dark Messiah has some high highs, and some low lows. I'd say it's a game well worth playing, though finishing is a harder sell.

Mixed feelings on this one, think I'd enjoy it as a kid when it was new but playing it for the first time now I have way too many issues with it.  

First the combat.  The game has a massive focus on getting environmental kills primarily through kicking enemies, for example kicking an enemy off a cliff, into convenient wall spikes, or campfires, all will instantly kill an enemy.  There is a lesser focus on trying to knock enemies down (kicking them down stairs, blasting with fireball, slipping with ice) so you can then "execute" them (not guaranteed to kill).  There's other environmental effects that some kill, some don't, like dropping barrels on them or a swinging rock.  While I appreciate the creativity with the environmental kills, the game has wayyy too heavy a focus on them, which causes a problem where your regular attacks/spells do trash damage and when you're being swarmed by dudes it's better to lure them to areas you can get instant kills.  So the combat becomes you constantly trying to kite enemies to death zones, which gets exhausting after several hours of this because it never changes.  The game also has some weird hitboxes/animations: goblins will swing and hit you when their club was three club lengths away from your body while you can't hit them from the same spot, someone will swing and miss and then you walk in to take advantage and you somehow take damage (they didn't swing again, idk if it's an invisible animation or if their first swing just lingers), the kick is usually reliable but sometimes will pass through the enemies or send them in the wrong direction.

Game crashed on me maybe 5 times, so not horrendous but still annoying.

Story was super forgettable (maybe better if you're really into Might and Magic).

Other random issues: Your weapon and especially your spells takes up way too much of your screen, if you're trying to aim off an edge at someone with a fireball it's difficult to see anything.  The visibility issue gets way worse with the lighting because a lot of the levels are super dark, so you activate your free night vision spell but because there are some torches in parts of the room those become way too bright so again it's hard to see (not to mention your magic weapons/spells glow like the goddang sun in night vision).  The levels drag on, I think this game would've been much better if it was half the length, you will be fighting the same enemies in the same environments for 1-2 hours a level, it gets tiring.  Like the spider temple, instead of making me gather 3 gems fighting a bunch of zombies the entire time in a dark crypt how about I just get one gem and kick 30 zombies into spikes instead of 90?  There's too many crypt style levels also, the highlights of the game were definitely the outside areas because they actually were interesting looking (the boat, the desert, the city).

Dark Messiah of Might and Magic is a first-person action game with both magic and melee combat that was programmed in the source engine. So needless to say it was extremely messy and unpolished. But in spite of that, I had a fun time and the game had enough unique elements that I kinda wish there was more stuff like it that just had a bit more refinement. It’s not something I would outright recommend to people, but I would say it's worth giving a shot for the novelty if nothing else.

—-------------------------------------------------------

A lot of the gameplay foundations seem like what you would expect for a fantasy RPG. You have a few melee weapons to fight enemies close up, a bow for ranged, and magic spells with a variety of effects. None of these options are great in retrospect with each weapon doing relatively little damage and enemies who can tank a lot of hits and stuns while dealing a lot of damage to you in the meantime. And the fact that everything is in first person can make combat quite chaotic as enemies will maneuver around you in groups while the reach of your weapons is deceptively short.  If you play this like a conventional first person action game then you're not gonna have a great time.

And yet despite that, the gameplay ends up being the best part thanks to the focus on physics. As a source engine game, Dark Messiah uses the physics effects as essentially the main method of combat. Rocks, boxes, and other inanimate objects scattered around the map can be picked up and thrown at enemies, kicked into their chest, knocked down stairs, and so much more. You’ll quickly learn that a sword might be necessary in a pinch, but if you can surprise your enemy by lobbing a rock at their face then they’ll be stunned far more than a few sword swings can ever hope for. These items have an immediate effect in terms of damage and stuns, but they really excel when used in conjunction with environmental hazards. Knocking an enemy off a ledge so they fall to death, shoving them into a spiked wall or fireplace, luring them into a staircase or alleyway where you have a trap prepared, and plenty of other possibilities are all a fun time.

While fun on its own, you won't always have what you want to pull off the kinds of tricks and traps you want. And that's where magic comes into play. You have some generic spells that are more damage focused, but the best are those with additional physics effects. Fire will ignite oil, electricity will shock anything in water, and the best of all is ice which will cause enemies running along its surface to slip and fall. The former two are useful when the situation allows it and you can make some fun combos (like lighting an enemy on fire, then kicking them into a group of their comrades to burn them as well), but the ice is going to be your best friend in any physics related shenanigans. I can't tell you how many times I lured an enemy through a doorway, spread some ice around the corner, and watched them slip off a cliff or into a fire. Rather than a typical fantasy hero, Dark Messiah makes you feel more like Kevin McCallister has gone on the offensive.

—---------------------------------------------------------

The effect would be much better if the physics were actually consistent though. As to be expected given the game’s age and engine, objects can be very unwieldy at times. The physics of each object can just feel wrong at times with the arcs they go through the sky or how quickly they reach their target, rendering precise setups a non-starter without a huge amount of trial and error. Collisions can feel weird with objects often clipping through each other, the ground, or the characters in a way that can have unpredictable effects (including a few deaths thanks to weird collision physics, both enemies and myself). And finally there’s just a great deal of inconsistency with a lot of the environmental effects. Sometimes fire just won’t light oil and enemies will run over ice without missing a step. It all leaves a system that feels very chaotic, sometimes in a good way when the chaos of the battlefield is a result of your own hijinks and enemy ai and other times in a frustrating way when a plan falls apart because the engine decided not to cooperate with your plans.

—---------------------------------------------------

Beyond the gameplay itself, a large part of the experience is driven by the level design. They’re all littered with objects, traps, and plenty of opportunities for your chaotic plans to take effect, though their success at doing so varies quite significantly. The early portions of the game were the most fun with open maps and dumb enemies that offered lots of opportunities for messing around. But as the game progresses, levels start feeling more restrictive either with very clearly telegraphed opportunities for you to mess around or with few enough options that some amount of normal combat becomes necessary. Levels become more enclosed as you move on and enemies start having ranged attacks that they can use instead of chasing you. I dont know whether its their ai being smarter or just everything else being unconducive to the tricks that used to be staples of my playstyle, but the last few levels felt like a real bore that required far more fighting and far fewer tricks than the great levels of the early and mid-game.

—----------------------------------------------------

And there isn’t much else worth mentioning besides that. The story is fine, not particularly engaging but enough to provide context for what you’re doing in each level. The visuals are pretty good, especially at highlighting what items you can interact with and what's just for show, and just being a solid dark-ish fantasy setting all around. And the sound, music, voices and other elements are just fine. There’s nothing bad enough to take you out of the experience but I don't think anything besides the gameplay will stick around in my mind for very long.

—-------------------------------------------------------

Dark Messiah was a pretty fun game overall. It's one of those experiences that's deeply flawed and undoubtedly suffers from some age-related issues but still has plenty going for it. I can safely say that I’ve never played anything else like it, but really wish that I could. A lot of the core ideas are good and a game that is fully committed to them would probably be a blast if some of the rough edges of the system here could be patched up. But as it stands right now, I have to admit that for all the fun I had while playing this game the flaws are way too hard to ignore and really bring the experience down. This is really a case of a game which does one thing particularly well and everything else is serviceable enough not to cause an issue most of the time. And that may be fine in much of the moment-to-moment experience but isn’t a great foundation when judging the game as a whole. Overall though, I would give Dark Messiah a soft recommendation for anybody interested in chaotic and physics-based gameplay and can tolerate a lot of rough edges from the engine and age.

It's painful to play, this old, polygonal pc clunker -- if you're not already so [i]steeped[/i] in it that the rose-tinted goggles do all the heavy lifting.  Released at a time when physics were a new and wondrous thing in games, you'll spend most of your Battle Time kicking guys off cliffs and throwing omnipresent barrels and crates (and not very well at that) . . . because what, after all, is the most powerful weapon in Ashan?  Is it the [b]Sword of the Dragonclaw[/b]?  Try again!  Is it the [b]Skull of Shadows[/b] perhaps?  No, no, no!  It's a heavy chunk of wood, lobbed by a maniac with apparent [i]gorilla strength[/i]!  Watch 'em rag-doll, Sareth, then it's your time to strike, because you sure can't hit them very well with your sword if they're moving at all!  Sure, it's fun to play around with the kicking and the Crates of Death in the first level or so, but that only goes so far in the face of all that's wrong.  And the game (if you can even get it to run in the current year) is held together by bubble gum and Dollar General duct tape, so it [i]will[/i] fly apart under the pressure of a strong look. Ah, dammit!  The door won't open again!  Guess I've got to load . . . again.  (I've heard a lot of folks actually have to uninstall and reinstall the thing to get past these points, and it's [i]certainly[/i] not worth that kind of trouble.)  

I suppose if you want to relive some of your past gaming glories, it might be okay.  I never played it back in 2006, but freely admit that I might have gone through it several times for the sheer novelty, if I had.  In 2026, however, I just can't get over the long, tangled dungeon levels where you keep getting stuck on the geometry (or the ropes), or what's even [i]worse[/i]:  the [b]darkness[/b] of absolutely everything.  Yes, if you're not standing in the bright sunlight, things are just too dark to see . . . [i]unless[/i] you turn on your Paris Hilton nightvision, which means you [i]have[/i] to use it.  A lot.  So I hope you like your games in really ugly, washed-out monochrome, because that's how you'll see so very much of this one.

I [i]do[/i] understand what they were trying to accomplish here, and wIth limited processing power, but it's just not much fun to play now, if it even works.  Autistically awkward swordplay (let's see you make a thrust connect with [i]anything[/i]!), nastily necessary night vision, glitchy landscapes, various door-locking bugs, and a sub-generic fantasy story vastly outweigh the fun you'll have from making guys go [i]floppy-flop[/i] with the power of Donkey Kong.

Do I like anything about it?  I certainly like the [b]idea[/b] of [i]Dark Messiah[/i]; there should be games made every month that do what this one [I]set out[/I] to, but there aren't, admittedly.  I also like Sareth's voice!  He reminds me of David Naughton, star of the short-lived 1970s sitcom [i]Makin' It[/i], a Doctor Pepper commercial or two, and my [b]favorite[/b] B-horror film, [i]The Sleeping Car[/i]!

Extremely fast-paced yet readable; excellent level design that rewards proper map/powerup control; good variety of maps; expertly-designed weapons that are properly balanced for multiplayer this time (well, for the most part - the grenade launcher is still pretty useless); superb bot AI with different behaviours, aggression, weapon preferences, and taunts in chat; rocket jumping is still possible; the same perfect movement and shooting this franchise is known for; and easy to learn with a seemingly infinite skill ceiling. It may not have as much "content" as modern titles – there aren't many weapons, the singleplayer campaign is short and repetitive, there are too few gamemodes (especially without the Team Arena expansion pack), and these few gamemodes may seem “primitive” next to the objective/vehicle/class/ability/unlock-based modes in today’s games – but very little can replicate this game’s brutal, blazingly-fast, pure skill-based, and always-action-packed combat. Still the best arena shooter ever made (along with UT99); almost every arena shooter afterwards is just a rehash of these 2 legendary titles.

8/10

Countless of hours rocket jumping and railgunning, having fun and building a community... Such fun times. Although he passed away a couple years ago, I will forever cherish these memories with him and will always remember what fun times we had. Thanks to id Software for making such a wonderful game.

It aged poorly. Quake 4 belongs to the darkest decade of ID software: the 2000s. Both this and Doom 3 share the same cringe sci-fi horror stuck in very linear dark spaceship/spacebase corridors filled with attempted jumpscares.
Especially at higher difficulties, the combat is boring and unfair as since the first encounters you realize that it's going to be all about bulletsponge enemies that have almost no reaction while you empty magazines on them until their health eventually depletes. In the meantime, they just need 2 or 3 shots to send your health deeply below zero.

Graphics-wise, it's stunning. The details of the machines and interfaces (ie touchscreens to control elevators) are the same of Doom 3 and this means that you can actually move a proper mouse cursor in them. And this is something amazing.

Weapons are dull and some of them are useless (the top tier electric gun takes a dozen of seconds of continuos fire to cull any of the lesser enemies. Again, each strogg kill is a boring continuos fire of an insane quantity of ammunition.

The story is also a carbon copy of Doom 3 with a constant "telling off" via radio like "hey corporal, stop wasting time and  go there / do this to save the World". You're an interplanetary hero who gets half stroggfied and manages to singlehandedly defeat the whole strogg civilization and still you're one of the lowest rank in the force you fight for. All officers do is simply sending you around and telling you off cause you're late... again and again.

The vehicle sections (tank and mech) are an appreciated change when you're about to abandon the game fed up with that infinite sequence of dark rooms, corridors, tiny crates managing to prevent you from going further.

But the greatest sin of Quake 4 is having zero references to Quake 2. Nothing at all. 

Buy it just to complete your collection.

Quake 4 released barely a year after Doom 3, utilizing what was then the same cutting-edge game engine to deliver unprecedented visual fidelity to the franchise that had arguably eclipsed Doom in popularity by the time Doom 3 had come out. With the shift in focus Doom 3 took the series towards darker, horror-themed gameplay in dimly-lit claustrophobic corridors, it's no surprise that Quake 4 borrows much of the same feel, except not strictly sticking to the dark, nor tight spaces. This game continues literally right after where Quake 2 left off in terms of story, so right after the Macron was defeated by Bitterman. a larger invasion force of Stroggos begins.

If you're not familiar with Quake 2 or the Strogg, stop what you're doing right now and go play one of the great seminal first person shooters of our lifetime. Once you've done that, this game will make a whole lot more sense to you, even if you don't necessarily need to have previously played Quake 2 in order to enjoy it. Quake 4 was developed by Raven Software in what feels like record time, as it released so close after Doom 3, and perhaps it suffers its identity somewhat because of that, again having many of the same aesthetics as Doom 3, including the complex Sci-fi metalworks level design, dark shadowy corners lit dynamically in real time, plus a lot of jump-scare style enemies.

Where Quake 4 feels more like a proper Quake game is in the combat loop. which is very split-second just like Quake ought to be, with an assortment of tight weapons that cover automatic weaponry, explosives, the railgun and the shotgun. A neat crossover with the first Quake in the form of the nailgun returns in Quake 4, although it doesn't behave like the original ones found n that game. Portions of the game do take place in bigger, more open rooms and outdoor environments, so this is by no means a horror game, it just dips its toe in the horror aspects of Doom 3 since all that stuff is basically baked into the engine. In essence, this feels like Quake 2 and Doom 3 had a baby, with a few extras tacked on.

One of those extras is the way the story is told through real time interaction with other characters, much like Half-Life 2, and one of the other extras is the somewhat arbitrary inclusion of vehicular segments and on-rails shooting segments where you are piloting a hover tank, a mech, taking a gunner position on a convoy, or some such thing that feels really like Raven Software was looking at Halo: Combat Evolved and Halo 2 for inspiration. You either like these segments or you don't, but they don't take away from the overall quality of the campaign or its storytelling, so it's fine. In reality, this was 2005 and for at least the next five years, everyone would copy either Halo or Call of Duty anyway.

My only gripes with Quake 4 twenty years on are all technical stuff really, as I rather enjoyed the game when I first played it on Xbox 360, and later when I got it on PC. My first major issue is how Id Software and Activison - then later Bethesda - completely abandoned support of the PC port very early on, yet still allow the game to be bought as it is. Doom 3 got a BFG Edition which added quality of life enhancements and controller support for PC users, even if I didn't like the artistic revisions to the lighting or horror elements. Quake 4 has seen none of that, and like the original PC release of Doom 3, you'll need to pour hours over the game's config files so it looks right, and use emulation for gamepads.

Using config file edits or fan-made scripts is nothing new to people playing old abandonware PC games on modern systems, but considering Id Software and Bethesda still sell Quake 4, it feels a little insulting that they couldn't at least patch in the controller support from the Xbox 360 port, and add some updated graphical settings within the game itself so you're not mucking around in the game's guts just to make it play the way it should out of the box. If you're a staunch gamepad user. you're going to have one hell of a time with the game's very unforgiving and tiny hitboxes, optimized for mouse users with no autoaim whatsoever, so either you're a sweaty pro gamer or expect to save scum a lot. Again, doesn't deter my view of the game's overall quality, but with all the updates and remasters the older Quake games receive, this one is overdue for some love.

This feels like Michael Bay made a Hollywood blockbuster loosely based on the 1996 hit video game "Quake", and then someone proceeded to make a video game based on said movie.

In Quake 4 the human race mounts a second assault wave on the Strog homeworld following the success of Operation Alien Overlord (Quake 2). You play as Kane, a marine from the Rhino squad who became known for their accomplishments during the Earth Invasion (Enemy Territory Quake Wars).

The game was built on the cutting edge Id Tech 4 engine (Doom 3) leveraging a lot of its strong points with moody dynamic light and shadows, incredible levels of geometric detail and textures, as well as an impressive amount of ambience and atmosphere with sounds going on everywhere.

Raven also enhanced several key areas of the engine’s capabilities, and one such area was the push for a much more complex friendly AI system. Quake 2 had you be a part of an invasion but act out the entire game in solo. In Quake 4 you actually feel like you are part of an invasion and Raven approached this in an excellent way that combined the grounded dark and methodical combat of Doom 3 with friendly units that you bump into which enhances the immersion and believability that you aren’t alone during the invasion.

You’ll run into friendly units during the entire campaign and help them sort different objectives ranging from demolition jobs to escorting technicians. The AI is also capable of actually killing enemies, so it’s not just set dressing even if they go down easily. At the time cod really had established the “no one fights alone” concept but with an extremely passive friendly AI ,so it was still refreshing to have friendly units who can actually kill enemies and help you outside of scripted events.

The tone of the game is quite dark and gritty and I think the direction they took with Q4 SP was great. MP retained the sort of Q3 speed and feel which honor that while SP is very well paced for the most part. You don’t run too fast either making it possible to really immerse yourself and take in the atmosphere just like in Doom 3, and you even having a dedicated walk-key! Had it had a few less cheesy one-liners and a little more emphasis on darkness and horror it could’ve been really eerie. However Strauss is a refreshing icebreaker that is well done in it’s humor, but beyond that it just undermines the horrific Strog when AI jokes about it like nothing while everything else just shows all the horrors they are capable off were friendlies lay dead, dismembered or even kept alive half mutilated to serve some horrific purpose.

The quality of the levels can be extremely impressive in terms of detail and mood but also quite uneven in certain areas, especially the outdoors which seems like a push to just satisfy drastically different gameplay that might have upset butthurt people in Doom 3, but at that point in time it was the weak points of both the visuals and gameplay, and more time could’ve been spent on the very impressive indoor missions instead.

Combat is quite well balanced, both in pacing and difficulty but at times it feels like more could've been done to pressure the player in interesting ways, especially given the quite diverse range of enemies. The need for supplies is actually something you need to keep in mind, looking for medpacks, ammo or friendly support units, because while not extremely rare or anything, you ain’t flooded with ammo either, especially on the hardest difficulty “GENERAL”. Unfortunately the checkpoints can be quite weird in their placement at times which in combination with General difficulty makes that quite annoying for someone who doesn’t want to save scum, so playing on LT if you want a challenge and a decent level of progress without saving scumming is to recommend.

The game seem to run just fine on modern hardware, all you should do is create an autoexec.cfg file in the q4base directory. Then add these lines which ensure you get a good texture hud scale plus a proper resolution. Adjust resolution to yours, and if your aspect ratio isnt 16:9 you also need to switch that parameter to the proper value.

seta gui_mediumFontLimit "0"
seta gui_smallFontLimit "0"
seta r_customWidth "2560"
seta r_customHeight "1440"
seta r_mode "-1"
seta r_aspectRatio "1"
seta r_renderer "best"

If anything I think they would transform it into something way more similar to the modern Dooms than the D3,Q4 era which is sad, in that case they’re way better of bringing that sort of gameplay to a Quake 1 reboot.

Being a Quake fan for a while, I had the impression that Quake 4 was not just a bad Quake game but also a bad game outright. After playing it I would say that there are several complaints I would levy towards it but it is not a bad shooter or even a poor addition to the Quake franchise. 

The gameplay in the campaign towards the start is rather un-Quake like: you have slow movement speed and acceleration, you take a lot of damage and rely more on cover than on movement to avoid attacks. However after the mid point (In which you gain access to new powers) the gameplay and array of weaponry becomes far more familiar to Quake and overall the game becomes more entertaining. I would agree that even by this point your base run speed is too slow but the strafe jumping in this game is enjoyable: it's slower to build momentum and requires more skill compared to previous titles but in those ways I somewhat prefer it to Quake II's. The sliding is also a fun mechanic which helps further differentiate this game from its predecessors and in a way that still meshes with Quake's identity.

I have played multiplayer deathmatch using the SABOT mod. It preserves the familiar movement and weapon design of earlier titles while introducing a few unique weapons, a lower time to kill, and the aforementioned sliding, all of which help the multiplayer stand on its own compared to say Quake Live.

The designs of weapons and the Strogg are reasonably faithful to the prior games but also with enough of their own identity. Most of the Strogg also behave and look cooler than their earlier counterparts with only a few exceptions. I also enjoyed the increased amount of clearly alien Strogg as in Quake II it's pretty difficult to discern which Strogg are assimilated humans vs another alien race. 

Though onto the issues this game suffers from: a dead multiplayer scene and a lack of bots to make up for it, poor optimisation on modern systems. As far as the campaign is concerned the worst offending elements are the vehicle and turret sections which are so distant from the core of Quake's gameplay and really quite jarring. 

I think Quake 4 has core gameplay with allot of potential but unfortunately the campaign doesn't really utilise all that potential and there are also very few mods to compensate for that.

Overall I recommend Quake 4 to Quake fans especially fans of the Strogg-Human war.

I’ve been hearing about this game for 20 years, and I don’t even remember when I bought it. But only now I finished it. It is quite a long game. Either that, or maybe the game feels so repetitive that it feels long. I will be perfectly honest about it. I don’t have any nostalgia goggles. This game is not on the level of other classics like Doom 3 and Prey (2006). Those games use the same engine, but are way more fun to play than this game. What I really don’t like about this game is that everything is just a dark corridor after a dark corridor. And in Doom 3’s case, that’s the game. It’s a survival horror game. It’s supposed to be dark, small, and claustrophobic. You’re supposed to be in a maze where your goal is to escape. This is the genre. In Quake 4’s case, you are on the offensive. You attack the Strogg on their own planet. 

So it really sucks that almost all the levels are just metallic dark corridors. And why dark? You constantly need to switch to the submachine gun because the flashlight is tied to this gun. I don’t understand it. Why the flashlight can’t be separate? Why you can use it only with the weakest gun? It makes no sense. It doesn’t make the game more of a challenge in any way. It’s just annoying. All those shadows are not fitting for the game, because the game is a fast-paced shooter. It’s not survival horror. It has some horror elements, but eventually you run fast and shoot. So the level design makes no sense in that context. It should have been more open levels and not just corridors all the time. This is what made me not like this game. The repetitive levels are so annoying in this game. Almost all of them look the same. And the game really lacks in the story.

I mean, yeah, you are at war with those aliens, but other than that? Just go and activate the tower, just go and protect this guy. Like, where is the story? Aside from something that happens in the middle, where is it? Nothing is going on in this game. It’s just Call of Duty in space, but with dark corridors everywhere. Why? Doom 3 has logs, videos, tapes. It really feels like a world you want to get sucked into. This game has nothing. It’s just Call of Duty in space from beginning to end. And what’s the point even? The game didn’t get a sequel. The Strogg are just a ripoff of the Borg, but more scary. The game has a few bosses, but they are all boring and forgettable. And like I said, it feels too long. It’s 30 levels. This is more than enough for this style of game. I guess the only replay value is just to play on higher difficulty, and that’s it. There is nothing more than that, really. There is not much to see in this game. Your character isn’t even speaking. It’s not as if there is a good story in there. There isn’t. It’s just a boring shooter that can’t decide if it wants to be horror or fast-paced action. Half-Life 2 came the year before, and that game has more level variety. Even Half-Life 1 has more level variety than this.

Yeah, It's pretty fun.

Wolfenstein is one series that has quite the rich history as far as its overall impact on the gaming industry, from its humble beginnings as an action-adventure game with the historical setting taking place within a Nazi fortress on the Apple II back in 1981 under the hands of Muse Software to then setting history itself 11 years later in 1992 by the revered video game developers behind id Software. This seemingly ordinary company would go on to revolutionize the perspective of combat from a traditional eagle-eye view of the room down to the ground floor from the lens of the heroic character themselves in what would become known as the 'First-Person Shooter' genre and the legacy it has left for many future developers in the 90s as far as revolutionary game design goes thanks to the programming legends at id Software that are John Carmack and John Romero.

As far as the premise of Wolfenstein 3D itself goes, taking inspiration from the first two games released by Muse Software, you play as the iconic B.J. Blazkowicz who made his debut in the original trilogy of episodes alongside the additional 'Nocturnal Episodes' by exploring each of the fifty-four levels spread across each of the six chapters to reach the elevator towards the next stage, during which you can expect to face off against dozens of armed Nazi troops who are quick to shoot on sight and, depending on the difficulty, even more deadly if caught off-guard!

To achieve success in this endeavor, starting off with just a meager knife and a pistol with only eight bullets to count, one must carefully clear out each of the rooms as both enemies and treasures of varying kinds await you. No doubt the regular guards are slow and weak but easily outnumber you as the difficulty is raised and progress further into the many levels of Wolfenstein 3D. Meanwhile the Schutzstaffel troops pack more of a punch with their machine guns and, although usually the gateway to higher firepower yourself early on, are always a threat to watch out for, though the officers and their quick reactions to the player alongside the deadly mutants who are renowned for their deadly silent approach when targeting the player are also dangers in their own right. This just leaves the 'killer dogs' whose main claim to being a problem is how fast they are with moving, albeit one bullet is guaranteed to do the job!

However these enemies are often accompanied by an equally-devious level design, in which their layouts often compliment their defences as far as an intruder looking to prowl through their bases are concerned. This isn't to say that we are diving into Tomb Raider levels of booby-traps, however it is that persistent issue from the expectations of a modern gamer looking into what was truly advanced and game-changing back in 1992, that idea of seeing things from the playable character's own eyes in real time sounds great and obviously left a strong impression for the time, yet it does have its obvious flaws over three decades later.

No doubt one of the biggest complaints when it comes down to Wolfenstein 3D is its labyrinth-like design as, although the first and third chapters are largely immune to this due to being based on a Third Reich castle and bunker respectively, the remaining four each take liberty with having less structured levels that often devolve into tight corridors with nothing noteworthy to aid the player in remembering where they are in the map and even can become intentional mazes seeking to confuse players, of which the 5th level of Chapter 4 'A Dark Secret' and the 7th level of Chapter 6 'Confrontation' are notoriously bad for this specific issue!

It should be noted though, however, that Wolfenstein 3D was developed during a time where many developers were brought up from the glorious arcade days of the 80s and those in id Software found themselves in this unique transitional period whereby the concept of games were starting to be seen as less of a digital 'game of skill' where the primary goal is to rack up as high of a score as possible and gradually shifting into new genres where new, different goals could be reached and through a whole host of methods distinctive to the video game medium as a whole, hence why I don't take this issue to heart and instead as a nitpick for anyone not used to how retro games used to operate in the past.

On the topic of scores, it is tracked mainly by three statistics as one can kill foes for points (guard = 100 points, SS = 500 points, mutant = 700 points etc) as well as collecting treasures that range from a simple gilded cross worth 100 points all the way to a regal crown granting 5000 points to the player when collected. Though it is the third statistic that gives Wolfenstein 3D somewhat of a notorious reputation as secrets is something that is present in every single level and often contains extra treasures to collect alongside food/medkits to heal yourself with and a surplus of ammo for your weapons, possibly even a secret exit that gives access to the elusive 'tenth' level that each chapter has on top of the standard 54!

I should preface this with another issue I had though, not that some secrets seem to lead to empty rooms (these often have another secret entrance that leads to the actual treasure) or even a select few that have Nazi troops hidden away, nicely tucked into a corner or pocket within the wall, as a shameless attempt to ambush the unsuspecting player who have their eyes on the goods! No, a sticking point is how these pushing blocks can be a little buggy at times, not pushing as far as they should and sometimes not revealing the hidden goods of a room which it still hides behind it, as the 7th level of Chapter 4 remains the only one of its kind where I had to look up a guide purely by virtue of a mandatory secret not working as it should which locked me out of a key needed to progress further into the level and the chapter as a whole.

Now based on the direction this review has been headed thus far, one might get the impression that I must have misclicked the recommendation for this game as it seems as though I am going hard on a DOS classic that has outdated gameplay and a 'relic of the past' as it were. Yet I understand all too well that such games as these have their place in gaming history, seeing as it differs from even its successors in the wake of the legendary Doom series whose DNA can be found in this very game as the setting being grounded in reality alongside a bigger focus on scores and a reduced sense of power fantasy lends itself quite nicely to you being one soldier taking on the impossible task of infiltrating and clearing out the fascist forces that lay within, deeply-entrenched in their fortified facilities.

After all, having played through all six chapters in increasing difficulty to get an idea of how the baby mode varies greatly from the stuff that makes boys into men, I can say with confidence that I get what makes Wolfenstein 3D such a joy to play despite all of the tedium and outdated game design from here in 2026. That thrill of carefully clearing out each room, not knowing what lays ahead, whilst tactfully conserving your ammo (especially at the start, where the knife's 'silent' attacks does wonders in preventing nearby enemies from being alarmed) is something that takes skill to learn the quick reflexes and precision needed to survive these stages.

Yet for what it's worth, I'd say it remains a game that is worth revisiting for veterans of the gaming scene as well as newcomers curious to see how the old retros felt like to play during the DOS days, especially with it often being on sale for as little as £0.59. And despite my issues with the overall confusing level design that was mimicking how levels looked back then with new ground-breaking technology of the era as well as specific bugs hampering the experience, you can't go wrong with cleaning out Third Reich scum in such vintage delights as seen with Wolfenstein 3D!

Wolfenstein 3D laid the groundworks for the FPS genre as a whole, with it's impressive 3D levels for the time, sound design, and gunplay. Doom didn't even come out until a year later in 1993! So yeah, you could say Wolfenstein 3D was the grandfather of the fps games! Even if the Wolfenstein franchise has been done dirty nowadays, with the release of Cyberpilot and especially Youngblood, we can atleast be thankful for the good that this game has brought upon the fps genre. 

The game is mostly about running around, killing nazis and solving puzzles to open up a door to the next levels. You do that basically in almost the entirety of the episodes in the game and after that you get to fight a big boss near the end and when you've defeated them, it's the end of the mission. It works in it's favor, even if it can be a tad bit repetative at times. Luckily the awesome gunplay and soundtrack make up for it!

Some parts do hold up pretty well, obviously when it comes to the pixel art of the characters in the game and gunplay, but man are the a few stuff that has aged kinda bad. The level design is a big maze with a ton of secrets and places to kill nazis, and it's not a bad idea at all! Many games have done this well, but in Wolfenstein 3D it's more of an annoyance than enjoyement. Sure, some secrets are fun to find but since it's a maze it can be a pain in the ass to navigate through. It also doesn't help when everything looks the same, meaning you'll have to spend a long time going around circles and such. Enemies can spawn out of nowhere and kill you, leaving you kind of flabbergasted and confused as to where the f*ck the came from in the first place. 

The earlier missions are pretty fun but later the levels kind of get progressively worse in terms of design. Instead of running around, killing nazis and stuff, you have to wait around in corner while theres an entire army of nazis trying to get to you. If you run out ammo or have low health when this happens, guess it's back to respawn! Seriously, f*ck those mutants. 
Wolfenstein Spear Of Destiny is a fine addition to the game although there's not a lot of interessting moments in the game that makes it less forgettable, aside from the final boss battle, it's the only memorable thing about the game. Though the level design in that game is even worse. 

Despite it's flaws without it, Doom and many other FPS games wouldn't have existed or been the way they are today. So yeah, that's really it, this game is both fun and annoying at the same time!

6.1/10 - Wolfenstein 3D 
5.6/10 Wolfenstein - Spear Of Destiny

For one, you have only 3 guns, all of which use the same ammo. You can't run sideways either, which is important if you are trying to shoot an enemy and dodge their attacks. The Great Grandfather of FPS Is a strange one. Its charming in simplicity with a hard set challenging difficulty that requires checking down every corner and often prefiring to not take even a hint of damage. The story is barebones in again, a charming way. You play as BJ Blazcowitz who escapes nazi prison to kill his way through the german army and eventually hitler. The Level design is just open spaces, tight corridors and mazes with bad guys around every turn. The mazes get increasinjgly frustrating the further you go.

Enemy nazis scream when they die, german swears and shouts, they alert other guards and often have you outnumbered, the players exceedingly fast speed at times is a unique gameplay counter to their large numbers. You have four weapons but you pretty much only use the submachine gun and the minigun. Fist are for style kills and pistol is just objectively worse than every other gun with no real need to be used. As they all share the same ammo, there is never a "damn i have only pistol ammo!" moment. 

After completing the main three campaigns there is some additional ones but they have much less polish and even more tedious mazes and labriynthys. The bosses are suprisingly fun to fight, the bullet sponges they are.  games had balls back then, let me kill hitler again in a shooter bruh.

I love the end dcreen messages calling the player a badass, i like the rythme of the combat, don't liike the mazes and tedious backtracking. Its an interesting playthrough, i would reccomend trying at least once if you love the FPS genre and its history.
6/10
