This isn't to say that we are diving into Tomb Raider levels of booby-traps, however it is that persistent issue from the expectations of a modern gamer looking into what was truly advanced and game-changing back in 1992, that idea of seeing things from the playable character's own eyes in real time sounds great and obviously left a strong impression for the time, yet it does have its obvious flaws over three decades later.

No doubt one of the biggest complaints when it comes down to Wolfenstein 3D is its labyrinth-like design as, although the first and third chapters are largely immune to this due to being based on a Third Reich castle and bunker respectively, the remaining four each take liberty with having less structured levels that often devolve into tight corridors with nothing noteworthy to aid the player in remembering where they are in the map and even can become intentional mazes seeking to confuse players, of which the 5th level of Chapter 4 'A Dark Secret' and the 7th level of Chapter 6 'Confrontation' are notoriously bad for this specific issue!

It should be noted though, however, that Wolfenstein 3D was developed during a time where many developers were brought up from the glorious arcade days of the 80s and those in id Software found themselves in this unique transitional period whereby the concept of games were starting to be seen as less of a digital 'game of skill' where the primary goal is to rack up as high of a score as possible and gradually shifting into new genres where new, different goals could be reached and through a whole host of methods distinctive to the video game medium as a whole, hence why I don't take this issue to heart and instead as a nitpick for anyone not used to how retro games used to operate in the past.

On the topic of scores, it is tracked mainly by three statistics as one can kill foes for points (guard = 100 points, SS = 500 points, mutant = 700 points etc) as well as collecting treasures that range from a simple gilded cross worth 100 points all the way to a regal crown granting 5000 points to the player when collected. Though it is the third statistic that gives Wolfenstein 3D somewhat of a notorious reputation as secrets is something that is present in every single level and often contains extra treasures to collect alongside food/medkits to heal yourself with and a surplus of ammo for your weapons, possibly even a secret exit that gives access to the elusive 'tenth' level that each chapter has on top of the standard 54!

I should preface this with another issue I had though, not that some secrets seem to lead to empty rooms (these often have another secret entrance that leads to the actual treasure) or even a select few that have Nazi troops hidden away, nicely tucked into a corner or pocket within the wall, as a shameless attempt to ambush the unsuspecting player who have their eyes on the goods! No, a sticking point is how these pushing blocks can be a little buggy at times, not pushing as far as they should and sometimes not revealing the hidden goods of a room which it still hides behind it, as the 7th level of Chapter 4 remains the only one of its kind where I had to look up a guide purely by virtue of a mandatory secret not working as it should which locked me out of a key needed to progress further into the level and the chapter as a whole.

Now based on the direction this review has been headed thus far, one might get the impression that I must have misclicked the recommendation for this game as it seems as though I am going hard on a DOS classic that has outdated gameplay and a 'relic of the past' as it were. Yet I understand all too well that such games as these have their place in gaming history, seeing as it differs from even its successors in the wake of the legendary Doom series whose DNA can be found in this very game as the setting being grounded in reality alongside a bigger focus on scores and a reduced sense of power fantasy lends itself quite nicely to you being one soldier taking on the impossible task of infiltrating and clearing out the fascist forces that lay within, deeply-entrenched in their fortified facilities.

After all, having played through all six chapters in increasing difficulty to get an idea of how the baby mode varies greatly from the stuff that makes boys into men, I can say with confidence that I get what makes Wolfenstein 3D such a joy to play despite all of the tedium and outdated game design from here in 2026. That thrill of carefully clearing out each room, not knowing what lays ahead, whilst tactfully conserving your ammo (especially at the start, where the knife's 'silent' attacks does wonders in preventing nearby enemies from being alarmed) is something that takes skill to learn the quick reflexes and precision needed to survive these stages.

Yet for what it's worth, I'd say it remains a game that is worth revisiting for veterans of the gaming scene as well as newcomers curious to see how the old retros felt like to play during the DOS days, especially with it often being on sale for as little as £0.59. And despite my issues with the overall confusing level design that was mimicking how levels looked back then with new ground-breaking technology of the era as well as specific bugs hampering the experience, you can't go wrong with cleaning out Third Reich scum in such vintage delights as seen with Wolfenstein 3D!

Played through the base game and Spear of Destiny on “Bring ’Em On!” difficulty.

[h2]Quick Verdict[/h2]

Wolfenstein 3D is engaging and historically important, but it overstays its welcome. Its charm lies in its simplicity, even if the maps are aggressively labyrinthine. I think it’s absolutely worth playing through the first episode, if only to appreciate where the FPS genre began.

[h2]Score: 2/5[/h2]

[hr]

[h1]The Review[/h1]

The only reason this game gets a recommendation at all is that I cannot bring myself to give the grandfather of the FPS genre a thumbs down. Compared to its contemporaries however, this game plays like a dream and is still very accessible to an average person. The game still scratches that itch for a very specific group of weirdos like me; people who enjoy the most stripped-down gameplay loop imaginable of:

> Shoot bad guys
> Find keys
> Find exit door
> Repeat

Wolfenstein 3D packs an impressive amount of charm into what little they had to work with.

Finding secrets (while obnoxiously accompanied by constant beeping as you sensually rub yourself against extended walls and mash your face into every fascist poster you see) is far more satisfying than you’d expect, especially when ammo and health are running dangerously low and any secrets you find can completely revitalize your dwindling supplies or give you a safe-room to come back to later to restock.

What really makes Wolfenstein engaging is resource management and enemy manipulation. You can’t just mow everything down with the chain gun, even though it fires absurdly fast, it chews through ammo at an alarming rate. Run out of bullets, and you’re left with only your knife, which feels like it was designed to cut the softest butter imaginable. In most situations, that’s a death sentence. Ammo quickly becomes your most valuable resource, often even more important than health.

Despite having pretty strong 3D spatial awareness, I found that getting lost became a regular part of starting new levels. I didn’t find this especially frustrating so much as tedious backtracking through cleared rooms, hunting for a missed key, and rechecking every corridor quickly became a chore.

At release, the game consisted of six episodic campaigns, later followed by 3 additional prequel mission packs. I found most of the added episodes fairly dull, and at their worst, outright frustrating. 

Spear of Destiny, a standalone prequel expansion, features noticeably better enemy placement that complements its maps more effectively. That said, I still spent a disproportionate amount of time wandering empty floors searching for keys long after the action was over. Where it really shines is in its bosses: excellent introductions, distinct personalities, clever arenas, and more interesting tactics. They’re a huge step up from the base game’s bosses and feel much better integrated into the overall experience.

I really enjoyed my time with Wolfenstein. I needed to take breaks about midway through every campaign to refresh my brain after keeping a detailed mind-map of the previous floor. It honestly can get exhausting. I just don't see myself playing through the whole game again, maybe the first 3 episodes.

Wolfenstein 3D is an enjoyable experience for the most part. The first 3 episodes are great. Watching Hitler melt in to a pile of blood was glorious. The last 3 are mostly frustrating. Some of the floors are too big and on the Bring 'em on difficulty there are just too many enemies. You die insanely quickly, often you wont even have time to react. There are mainly two reasons why the game can feel really unfair. When doors are opening enemies spot you before the door is even halfway open. Secondly enemies can shoot through each other and you can not. So if you are shooting down a tight corridor and there are multiple enemies lined up you will get shot. The reaction speed of enemies are insanely fast, coupled with how much damage you take at close range. During the last 3 episodes I was wondering if I had just gotten tired of the game because I was not having fun. When I started Spear of Destiny I realized why I felt that way. I wasn't tired of the game, I just did not enjoy how hard the game would get later on.

In conclusion: The first 3 episodes are great, the last 3 are mostly frustrating and Spear of Destiny is good up until maybe the last 5 floors or so.

The level design is a big maze with a ton of secrets and places to kill nazis, and it's not a bad idea at all! Sure, some secrets are fun to find but since it's a maze it can be a pain in the ass to navigate through. Enemies can spawn out of nowhere and kill you, leaving you kind of flabbergasted and confused as to where the f*ck the came from in the first place. 

The earlier missions are pretty fun but later the levels kind of get progressively worse in terms of design. Instead of running around, killing nazis and stuff, you have to wait around in corner while theres an entire army of nazis trying to get to you. If you run out ammo or have low health when this happens, guess it's back to respawn! Seriously, f*ck those mutants. 
Wolfenstein Spear Of Destiny is a fine addition to the game although there's not a lot of interessting moments in the game that makes it less forgettable, aside from the final boss battle, it's the only memorable thing about the game. Though the level design in that game is even worse. 

Despite it's flaws without it, Doom and many other FPS games wouldn't have existed or been the way they are today. So yeah, that's really it, this game is both fun and annoying at the same time!

Played through the base game and Spear of Destiny on “Bring ’Em On!” difficulty.

[h2]Quick Verdict[/h2]

Wolfenstein 3D is engaging and historically important, but it overstays its welcome. Its charm lies in its simplicity, even if the maps are aggressively labyrinthine. I think it’s absolutely worth playing through the first episode, if only to appreciate where the FPS genre began.

[h2]Score: 2/5[/h2]

[hr]

[h1]The Review[/h1]

The only reason this game gets a recommendation at all is that I cannot bring myself to give the grandfather of the FPS genre a thumbs down. Compared to its contemporaries however, this game plays like a dream and is still very accessible to an average person. The game still scratches that itch for a very specific group of weirdos like me; people who enjoy the most stripped-down gameplay loop imaginable of:

> Shoot bad guys
> Find keys
> Find exit door
> Repeat

Wolfenstein 3D packs an impressive amount of charm into what little they had to work with.

Finding secrets (while obnoxiously accompanied by constant beeping as you sensually rub yourself against extended walls and mash your face into every fascist poster you see) is far more satisfying than you’d expect, especially when ammo and health are running dangerously low and any secrets you find can completely revitalize your dwindling supplies or give you a safe-room to come back to later to restock.

What really makes Wolfenstein engaging is resource management and enemy manipulation. You can’t just mow everything down with the chain gun, even though it fires absurdly fast, it chews through ammo at an alarming rate. Run out of bullets, and you’re left with only your knife, which feels like it was designed to cut the softest butter imaginable. In most situations, that’s a death sentence. Ammo quickly becomes your most valuable resource, often even more important than health.

Despite having pretty strong 3D spatial awareness, I found that getting lost became a regular part of starting new levels. I didn’t find this especially frustrating so much as tedious backtracking through cleared rooms, hunting for a missed key, and rechecking every corridor quickly became a chore.

At release, the game consisted of six episodic campaigns, later followed by 3 additional prequel mission packs. I found most of the added episodes fairly dull, and at their worst, outright frustrating. 

Spear of Destiny, a standalone prequel expansion, features noticeably better enemy placement that complements its maps more effectively. That said, I still spent a disproportionate amount of time wandering empty floors searching for keys long after the action was over. Where it really shines is in its bosses: excellent introductions, distinct personalities, clever arenas, and more interesting tactics. They’re a huge step up from the base game’s bosses and feel much better integrated into the overall experience.

I really enjoyed my time with Wolfenstein. I needed to take breaks about midway through every campaign to refresh my brain after keeping a detailed mind-map of the previous floor. It honestly can get exhausting. I just don't see myself playing through the whole game again, maybe the first 3 episodes.

Wolfenstein 3D is an enjoyable experience for the most part. The first 3 episodes are great. Watching Hitler melt in to a pile of blood was glorious. The last 3 are mostly frustrating. Some of the floors are too big and on the Bring 'em on difficulty there are just too many enemies. You die insanely quickly, often you wont even have time to react. There are mainly two reasons why the game can feel really unfair. When doors are opening enemies spot you before the door is even halfway open. Secondly enemies can shoot through each other and you can not. So if you are shooting down a tight corridor and there are multiple enemies lined up you will get shot. The reaction speed of enemies are insanely fast, coupled with how much damage you take at close range. During the last 3 episodes I was wondering if I had just gotten tired of the game because I was not having fun. When I started Spear of Destiny I realized why I felt that way. I wasn't tired of the game, I just did not enjoy how hard the game would get later on.

In conclusion: The first 3 episodes are great, the last 3 are mostly frustrating and Spear of Destiny is good up until maybe the last 5 floors or so.

An excellent preservation of some of the most influential iconic games of all time.

Doom (1993) and Doom II (1994) were monumental entries in the FPS genre, building off the innovations of Wolfenstein 3D to create games that are timelessly entertaining and visceral. The music, the textures, and the sheer speed and size of everything happening on screen creates an experience only matched by the modern reboot trilogy of the same franchise (amazing games in their own right).

This package provides a way for modern systems to easily access the original two games as well as all of the user-created content that was released by the devs officially between the 90s and now. This gives you access to all of the original Doom engine content ever officially released while also providing mod support for you to play around with. 

In terms of the games themselves, I find that the original Doom shines the most in its level design, whereas Doom II sacrifices the quality of the level design for a new weapon and more enemy variety.

If you are a fan of the modern Doom games or older games, or simply want to experience the roots of an iconic series that is still fun to play to this day, I would seriously recommend this.

And the first contact was awful. I was running through Refuelling Base and the next level and just couldn't cope with the fact that you can't look up or down. This will likely be a shock for everybody who never played games from these days before.

I've been playing classic Doom basically my whole gaming life and I can comfortably say it's my favorite game. I have owned many console ports over the years and have played a wide variety of source ports/mods on the PC. This port of the game, while not being as fancy as something like ZDOOM (sorry kids, no brutal doom!), is easily the best user friendly/console compatible port yet.

This port makes it very easy to join and host multiplayer doom games that run custom WAD (mod) files. It's way more user friendly than the alternative of downloading Zandronum type clients/port forwarding.  It also works cross platform, which is great for me because now I can play Doom with one of my longtime Doom buddies who only has Xbox. If you join a game running a mod you don't have, the client downloads it for you. I can start a lobby on a custom map pack on coop mode playing casually by myself or with my friend, and other players come and go as they please. I've made some more doom buddies this way. This port adds a new modern cooperative mode too which only allows players to respawn if a surviving player is in a safe area which IMO is a great way to balance issues the classic cooperative mode had. There's local multiplayer as well including an 8 player splitscreen option which sounds absolutely nuts. I hope I can do that one day. 

If you are new to doom, this is an awesome way to start. I highly HIGHLY recommend exploring the brilliant community map packs that are out there; the things the community has conjured over the years blow the original levels out of the water by several orders of magnitude. Some of my favorites include Lost Civilization, Doom Zero, and Outposts of the Wastelands.

My recommendation is unclouded by nostalgia: When the classic Doom games were super popular, I was busy playing platformers and adventure titles like the Donkey Kong Country and Zelda games. So I went my entire childhood (and beyond) with zero interest in the FPS genre.

Damn, I really missed out! Doom 1&2 are still awesome over 30 years later. I'm really glad there's an accessible bundle like this that preserves them, while having updated keyboard and mouse controls. The lack of mouse look had me a little skeptical going in, but the games work perfectly without it and I adjusted to it almost instantly. 

Pretty much everyone (besides me, I guess, lol) has played OG Doom, so there's not much more else to say. You're zoomin and shootin and finding (some seriously hidden) secrets along the way. The weapons feel great and the demons are iconic. 

If I have anything to nitpick, it's that the new remixed soundtrack is awesome, but feels out of place. It's so full that it can easily drown out enemy noises, which are important to hear. So I like to take care of all the enemies in a mission with the original music on and then switch over to the remix while secret hunting to see how it compares. 

When it comes down to it, I'd recommend this bundle to pretty much everyone. Haven't played classic Doom yet? Pick this up and have fun doing your video game history homework! Alternatively, if you loved these back in the day, pick this up for a sweet nostalgia trip.

Released just a day before the release of the N64 in Japan Quake is very emblematic of that era of games when everybody was trying to get to be the first people to get true 3D out the door and more importantly get a HIT with true 3D gameplay out the door. There are certainly outliers that came before like the first System Shock for example but that was more niche and highly experimental in nature compared to Quake and didn't sell as well. And you can see that this is one of John Carmack's feathers in his hat. Like this is one of those games that exemplifies how his interest in tech worked together with the rest of ID to make technical leaps that would change the ecosystem around him. 

Indeed to truly understand the effect this had on the broader games industry you have to look at ID's contemporaries of the time. 3D Realms and Bungie, and how they worked around 3D rather than being truly 3D. A thing most people don't get is the Doom era of FPS was for the most part not real 3D, people often call these games 2.5D shooters for a reason. Doom the more you play it, lays this out really plainly that your more or less moving around on a flat plane and the APPEARENCE of 3D is actually a clever trick. Game logic wise its all just one plane. Because of this stuff like Room over Room for example was impossible to do. So when for example Heretic and Hexen had mouselook and projectiles moving through the space in a way that looks 3D that's actually just the visual sprites and the rendering doing a little trick. In the background game logic it's still moving at you on a flat plane. 

So to go back to the topic of ID's contemporaries the Build Engine with 3D realms used a clever trick called "portals" where the two space aren't actually connected at all, its essentially one big mirror trick using these portals to place the rooms on top of each other but in actuality your moving along flat planes. Bungies Alpeh One (It didn't have an official name before that source port came out as far as I know) engine was a bit more sophisticated they basically used 2.5D game logics and the architecture of the Macintosh itself and how it handles memory to make something that was basically as good as 3D and had an actual consistent 3D physics engine and everything but it still wasn't fully 3D even if the physics and stuff was there to some degree. 

Then along came Quake. This is Quake. This is the true 3D FPS. It's not 2.5D its 3D. Like actually 3D. And in a way that actually matters too. This is something I often disagree with people when they say Quake is just Doom since on the surface yes the game design is very similar to Doom's but if you actually get good enough at Doom and Quake you come to realize how different just having actual 3D makes things. True Projectiles, True jumping, the first game to have consistent and popularized Bhop. The fact that the engine was so influential and used as a base by so many people that things that people typically associate with Valve like Surfing for example actually has its origins in the Quake engine. True verticality meaning platforming and movement and level design is far more complex in terms of geometry and then navigating said geometry. This is actually one of the reasons why it has a lower monster count than doom. Its not just the level limits its the fact that the levels build upward more often than Doom does. Like that combined with the true projectiles of the Grenade Launcher and Rocket Launcher changed everything. It seems quaint now to think people had they're mouths agape at the grenade launcher in this game but that's real. I prefer to think of Quake as a evolutionary branch or Mutation of Doom and it's really good in its own right. 

But this is also kind of a bittersweet game. Why? Because it represents the end of the "Two Johns" era of ID Software and the beginning of the Carmack enlightened dictatorship era of ID. Indeed this is secondary but I feel like you can split ID's history into 3 era's. First you have the Classic ID era, John Romero and John Carmack working together at the head of the company which ends with Quake. Then you got the Carmack era which started with Quake 2 and ended with Rage and then you have the Modern ID era or maybe you could call it the Bethesda Zenimax era which started with Doom 2016. 

It's sad because this Game historically speaking in the context of ID's history kind of represents the "End" of the early and fun party atmosphere of ID's history. Yes you had earlier examples like Tom Hall leaving during Doom 1's development but that was one off compared to how fundamentally the company shifted when Romero left. It represents the band breaking up and Carmack's and Romero's friendship souring and falling apart. Its very garage band story esque. Two good friends falling apart as success puts on pressure. Indeed while Quake 1 you can definitely see Romeros influence it didn't turn out anything like what he personally wanted Quake to be. It represents that change to more of the philosophy of tech driving the game first and foremost that Carmack had in mind which while it wasn't without merit and they continued to put out good stuff like Quake 3 for example where that's an example of the Tech Driving the game was pulled off perfectly and everything aligned they also had stinkers like Rage. It also often resulted in the staff being overly deferential to Carmack and getting crunched a lot by him. Indeed in contrast to say Valves open Cabal design Carmack basically ruled as dictator and if you weren't on the same page and interested in the again very impressive tech advancements he was doing you kind of just got quietly resigned. 

I'm not anti Carmack and I understand his POV very well. He's genuinely driven and very autistic and lot of people can't keep up with that because to Carmack that IS his life! He's always straight no lifed and worked even himself 24/7. He's not genuinely a sociopath he just has a bad habit of driving his team too hard in pursuance to his goals but you notice that Money is never the goal. This is about his genuine Obsessiveness stemming from his psychology, not greed. He famously gave away his Ferrari in a Quake speedrun contest and later basically walked away from ID without making a fuss about the acquisition money, which says a lot. He's more interested in the next technical problem than accumulating anything. He genuinely wants to change everything with his tech and create great things he just gets confused when the people around him have more to their life than this because to him that's his normal. But ultimately I feel that the company was more well balanced when Romero was around and a lot of the upsets and stumbles like Rage could have been avoided if Romero was still there. And the friendship being ruined is a very sad thing to me. You hate to see that. You don't want to see the bros fighting like that. Its bad for your soul. Like as someone who also struggles with obsessive behavior that can put other people off sometimes reading Masters of Doom made me kind of bummed out to see them fall out like that.

Quake (1996) is legendary game for being the first fully-3D first-person shooter, and for establishing many things within the online gaming community that are now taken for granted, like competitive online play, more-extensive modding that included total conversions, and machinima - or movies made using recorded scenes in-game. People did have competitive play with Doom and made level packs for it, but the highly-customizable and fully-3D Quake sparked the kind of interest in modding games that would lead to games built in both Quake and Doom in time. As for the game itself, there is a lot of controversy to unpack, and people tend to actually prefer Quake II's campaign while preferring the competitive deathmatch play of the original.

This is mostly because Quake for all its innovation, had a very mixed art direction as it was originally intended to be a first-person action RPG with bigger, non-linear level design, based on works by H.P. Lovecraft. Unfortunately, so much time was wasted making, then re-making level architecture due to John Carmack really wrestling with making Quake fully 3D, that most of the team was burned out and vetoed John Romero's original plan, opting to use a lot of demo material and chop up the levels into smaller arenas and set pieces that a conventional first-person shooter could be built around, retaining much of the Lovecraftian theme, but splicing it with familiar sci-fi and Satanic themes borrowed from Doom. An episodic level design was once again used, but this time connected with a hub map.

Quake was a much faster game than Doom, and enemies required more evasive maneuvers to fight, something which compensated for there just being less of them since now they were fully 3D animated objects, and PCs of the day couldn't load up with 50 of them on-screen like they could with Doom's 2D sprite-based enemies.  Enemies have really punishing attacks and the levels are also filled with both platforming sections and booby traps, meaning you'll need that blistering run speed and insane reflexes to juke and jive yourself away from instant death most of the time. A fairly limited weapon selection with shotguns, nail guns, grenade launchers, rockets, and a lighting-style weapon called the scorcher keep things simple, and there is no room-clearing BFG-type weapon here like there was with Doom.

At the end of the day, the challenge can get so extreme with the mix of evasive combat, high-damage enemy attacks, and split-second reflexes that Quake has the player feeling like an acrobatic glass cannon, which may not be to everyone's liking compared to later entries in the series and just later FPS games in general. Unreal would definitely take a nod from the first Quake with its balancing of speed, fragility, and high-damage attacks, so in some ways that is the spiritual successor to this game. The moody ambient soundtrack from Nine Inch Nails is also unique to this game, and because it didn't receive as many console ports as Quake II, there were a lot of people without PCs in the 90's that actually went decades without playing it, myself included. 

Quake is a great game, and nowadays has a ton of mods for it, too. Back when episodic expansions were being officially released for it, they ended up being somewhat more influenced by the flavor of Quake II than the original Quake episodes, with heavy metal and industrial music themes, more humanoid enemies, and more "tech base" sci-fi elements than the original game, further showing just how at odds the Lovecraftian themes and vestiges of the originally-intended RPG found in the bones of the original Quake campaign were with the overall game itself. Still, a fun fast white-knuckle experience that holds up.

The unique blend of dark fantasy and science fiction gives the original Quake a one-of-a-kind feel and atmosphere that is complemented perfectly by it's soundtrack.

The gameplay is fun, addicting and still holds up after 30 years of the game's release. Ammo is quote frequent, so you can blow things up without worrying about running dry. The enemy variety is great and they all feel really good to blow into bloody chunks.

None of the game's issues made me give up on it, however, as the original Quake is still loads of fun, and truly a timeless classic.

If you are a fan of old fps games and somehow haven't played this one, I urge you to give it a try. You won't be disappointed!

[h1] doom-OSTAL LIFE 1996 [/h1]

Even almost thirty years later, Quake still feels like a middle ground between the bloody chaos of Doom and the maze-like corridors of the first Half-Life. You totally buy the more modest claims that it still inspires creators of retro shooters like Devil Daggers. Dark fantasy, a distinct vibe, and gloomy locations — all of this creates that exact aesthetic modern developers keep trying to replicate.

The main campaign lets you play through four big episodes, each ending with a boss fight. Gameplay-wise, it’s not insanely hard, but it’s also not just about mindlessly shooting enemies. Quake makes you not only pull the trigger but also hunt for buttons and keys, open hidden areas, and solve small puzzles to get ammo or health packs. The end-of-level stats, showing time taken, enemies killed, and secrets found, might not push you to explore every nook and cranny, but they do show that the level was cleverly designed.

The story isn’t Oscar-worthy: you’re basically collecting four magical runes to reach the finale. Still, even as a classic, the game has two noticeable downsides. First, it can get repetitive, with the scenery and objectives starting to feel stale. Second, enemies sometimes behave strangely or unpredictably—not just popping up suddenly behind you, but also with tricky traps mixed in.

The game feels pretty repetitive: even though we’re promised four different episodes with castles and 'dead' worlds, in reality it’s all patched together from the same assets. Dark fantasy, rusty blocks, and mold get mixed in almost randomly. The locations don’t flow smoothly, they just sort of snap from one to the next. Enemy behavior is sketchy too: some Shambler might just spin on the spot, unable to attack, while the 'centipedes' bombard you with purple orbs that are nearly impossible to dodge. Even on normal difficulty, regular zombies or knights soak up so much lead that you burn through almost a quarter of your magazine just to take them down (and in this game, zombies only really go down with rocket hits).

A little 'incident' happened to me personally near the end of the playthrough. No bugs occurred in the game, but... In theory, after collecting all four runes [spoiler] a passage to the floor for the final boss should open in the episode selection hub. [/spoiler] For me, though, the game just reopened paths to the old levels, and I had to manually tap through the last 2 levels (and another gripe with the game: there’s no proper layered system for continuing play). As for the arsenal, it’s not very impressive in terms of variety or creativity: a couple of nail guns, two types of shotguns, a rocket launcher with a grenade launcher, and one electric gun. The axe, in 95% of cases, is completely useless and just sits there as dead weight.

Nevertheless, Bethesda and Nightdive Studios did an excellent job with the re-release. There are some really cool quality-of-life features here: you can change the font to a more readable one so you don’t strain your eyes on the pixels, there’s proper Russian language support, and achievements. In this regard, the developers were far more thoughtful than Valve. Valve, it seems, has been so blinded by the success of Half-Life that in 25 years they still haven't bothered to add achievements or proper localization to the first game. In Quake, all of this has been done properly; the game has genuinely been updated for modern PCs without turning it into unplayable junk.

The final verdict is this: the first Quake is a niche game. It has noticeably aged and can seem a bit dull in places, but it’s still fully playable. It’s a great option for those who want to see where it all started and aren’t ready to listen to the Half-Life fanatics who elevate their grail to the absolute while forgetting about such important projects. The game is definitely worth giving a chance.

An absolutely outstanding FPS that has stood the test of time and inspired many others to follow in its footsteps.

The original Quake was a ground-breaking game when it first released, it absolutely changed the face of FPS gaming forever, and was the final nail in the coffin for many 2.5D shooters as it signalled a new dawn for the FPS genre. As Duke Nukem famously once said in Duke Nukem 3D "I ain't afraid of no Quake!" well...that didn't age so well! 

Quake II took what made the original spectacular and revamped everything, and this remastered Nightdive version of the game is SUPERB. Graphical upgrades look incredible, the support for modern hardware, and the easy online play plus added achievements are an absolute dream come true, not to mention the now improved enemy AI and QoL features such as the compass to help you navigate your way through the vast almost maze-like levels.

Also included are the levels from Quake 64, making this the ultimate Quake II package for older gamers wanting to revisit their childhood, or newcomers to the classic "boomer shooter" genre who want to see where it all started.

Quake II is a golden experience from start to finish!

The remaster has a shockingly large amount of content if you're looking to play every episode. Like 40-50 hours of gameplay total.

Call of the Machine is definitely the best out of all of them. My god, is it awesome both aesthetically and gameplay-wise. Sometimes it plays like a miniature Doom slaughtermap because PCs can handle many more enemies than in 1997.

Quake 2 is a fun romp through sci-fi bases. I can't really say much about it because it is just that. A very fun time. Would recommend. Although I guess that's obvious because it's the main campaign most people play.

Quake 2 64 is also a fun take on the original Quake 2 campaign. It's much, much shorter than Quake 2, and probably gets skipped over due to its name (Quake 1 64 was just a straight port, after all. So that sets poor expectations) but Quake 2 64 is actually its own campaign. It's pretty good.

The Reckoning is... meh. It's meh. It's that slow dip into Ground Zeroes where it's just bad. It's uninspired, bland. More of the same tech bases Quake 2 offered, but with worse level design and higher difficulty (but in the cheap shot way)

Ground Zeroes is definitely the worst. It was the worst then, and its the worst now. It has all of The Reckoning's faults multiplied by two. The does remaster try to fix some of its faults like adding visibility lasers to the turrets they LOVE to spam above doorways and in obscure spots, and it does really make it a better time than it was in 1997, it is still not good. If there was an episode to skip, I would skip Ground Zeroes, no hesitation.

[h1] RIBBIT !! [/h1]

Quake II feels at least one and a half times better than its predecessor. It's still the same corridor-shooter maze, but now it finally has a proper story. Instead of dry text summaries at the end of episodes, the game tries to deliver full cutscenes and clear objective descriptions. The story is simple: you're a marine, cut off from your squad, taking on the Stroggs — the local cybernetic aliens — on your own. Switching from the demons of the original to high-tech and computers really benefits the series: the arsenal has grown two and a half times, and you actually have to use each weapon smartly now.

Of course, for today’s audience (well, I actually turned 20 this year), the gameplay might feel a bit repetitive. The basics haven’t changed: run around, kill monsters, hunt for secrets, and collect bonuses. But the challenges have become visually more interesting: sometimes you have to figure out how to unlock a tricky door, sometimes interact with elevators, sometimes blow something up to progress. The real highlight is the compass on the Q button — seriously, NO JOKE, it’s a genius idea for these kinds of mazes — you can hit it anytime to see the way, so you don’t end up running in circles and screaming at your screen because you got lost.

Graphically, the game feels like a throwback to the late nineties, but thanks to Bethesda and Nightdive Studios, it all looks super clean. They didn’t just re-release the game—they improved the effects and added support for all modern features. Especially nice is that all expansions, The Reckoning and Ground Zero, are included. This really sets Bethesda’s approach apart from Valve, who with Half-Life were always overcomplicating things, selling Opposing Force and Blue Shift separately or putting out sketchy “remasters” like Half-Life: Source. Here, it’s done for the players: you just download and play the full version, no paying multiple times for the same game with different packaging.

Quake II is really a well-balanced and fair shooter. Don’t expect any mind-blowing revelations or crazy emotions that will change your life. But it’s a solid, classic game that’s still fun to play today, maybe while listening to a YouTube video on the side. The developers did a great job, and the game still feels alive nearly 30 years later.

This review is for the remaster rather than the game itself.

This might be Nightdive's best remaster so far, which I like to think was made with the attitude of "OH, YOU PEOPLE DON'T THINK QUAKE II WAS VERY GOOD, DO YOU? WELL TAKE THIS YOU MOTHERS." 

It plays and feels GREAT. More like a 2023 game than a 1997 one now, and I mean that in a good way.  

The new campaign is nifty and merges all the content from the base game, expansions and console ports together, something I wish Nightdive would've done for the Quake I remaster.

The slow weapon switching has been replaced with Quake III-esque weapon switching, making the game more fast-paced and solid in a way that may not necessarily be faithful to Quake II itself, but more to the Quake series as a whole, almost making Quake II fit between Quake I and Quake III Arena a lot easier now. I never had a problem with the weapon switching before but speeding it up was a good move I'd say.

Like with the Quake I remaster, deathmatch now has optional bot support for offline mode... And yes it's a blast. I do hope Nightdive keep adding bot support to their remasters in the future.

Whether this remaster completely obliterates vanilla Quake II on the Yamagi source port I can't really say since I still have a soft spot for vanilla Quake II. The Reckoning and Ground Zero expansions are easier/more balanced in this remaster which is something to keep in mind for those who are likely to get pissed off whilst playing them in their original forms. 

I can take or leave the Berserker's new leap attack. You can avoid taking damage by jumping at the right time, but it's an attack that really takes you aback (hey that rhymed) if you've played vanilla Quake II as much as I have.

So yeah, while vanilla Quake II is still a fine way to play the game, this a wonderfully high-quality package that Nightdive should be very proud of,

Quake II was a radical departure from the first Quake, released a year after it, and gave Id Software their first game that wasn't about alternate dimensions or demons of some kind since Wolfenstein 3-D. This was a big deal to the guys at Id, who had wanted to break away from occult themes after releasing the genre-defining Doom and Doom II in 1993 and 1994 respectively. Quake was meant to be something other than a first person shooter under the original direction of John Romero, who wanted a first-person action RPG with elements of Lovecraftian horror woven in. After a protracted development cycle mostly due to John Carmack wrestling with making a viable fully-3D game engine, Quake still ended up being a shooter.

This, combined with the tacking on of Sci-fi ideas last minute made Romero and co-designer Sandy Peterson furious, and they left Id Software afterward. The rest of the team didn't really sit well with the amalgamated themes they'd created with Quake either, and handed expansions off to other developers while they worked on a new game about hostile alien cyborgs threatening Humanity, using the Quake engine. After trying to think of a name, they ended up just calling it Quake II, and thus here we are. Game mechanics are not all that different from the original Quake other than the fact you are not zooming around like you're on roller skates, and the action is a bit more deliberate with cover playing a part. The weapons are almost entirely different save for the shotgun and super shotgun, and are more numerous.

You play as the soldier simply called Bitterman, with Humanity taking the war to the homeworld of their alien cyborg nemesis, the Strogg. Your drop pod malfunctions, and the result is crash landing deep within unknown territory, spending the rest of the game infiltrating and finding a way to lower the planetary defenses so a larger-scale invasion can begin. Like Quake, you can save anywhere, but unlike Quake, Quake II ditches the hub-based episodic format in favor of linear map design with a bit of branching and backtracking within each area, which consists of three to five interconnected maps you must navigate and explore to finish primary and secondary objectives. Each of these interconnected maps contain secret areas and sometimes entire secret levels too.

Quake II's more-cohesive story told in-game rather than through end-of-episode exposition, novel semi-open level design, and gritty sci-fi theme proved resonant with players and future game developers alike, and the campaign for Quake II is thus considered more memorable. Gameplay balance is also much better, with far less cheap deaths at the hands of enemies with overpowered splash and hit-scan attacks requiring lightning-fast twitch reflexes to avoid and kill, as opposed to the first Quake. which was full of moments that required you to save scum encounters on even easier difficulties due to the instant-death nature of combat. Lest we forget, Quake II also has an extremely impressive musical soundtrack by Sonic Mayhem featuring real guitars, blending heavy metal and industrial beats.

Quake II has always gotten more love from publishers than the original, receiving more ports onto consoles than Quake, and more re-issues and official expansions. The first Quake will always be the darling of the modding and deathmatch community, so much so that even today people are making content for it ranging from levels and episodes to total conversions. Quake II receives nowhere near this kind of community love, except for the people who really want to expand upon the Strogg lore and make fan content that continues the fight against them. There isn't much more I can say, this game is an influential masterpiece of first person shooter design that has aged like fine wine, and remains extremely replayable, especially with modern controller support and Nightdive Studios remastering.

[h1] Retro Review[/h1] 
After loving the first game, I jumped into Quake II expecting refinement and new gameplay aspects. Instead, I found a sequel that feels worse in almost every meaningful way.

[u] Gameplay[/u] 
The biggest issue is the weapon design.

Many weapons feel like downgraded versions of those from Quake 1. Some are essentially reskins with reduced effectiveness. In practice, only two to four weapons feel viable  and two of those share the same ammo pool, which limits variety even further. The shotgun, in particular, feels underpowered due to enemy design and engagement ranges.

Enemy design is where things really fall apart. Most enemies are hitscan, meaning their shots connect instantly. There’s little opportunity to dodge or outmaneuver attacks. In a fast-paced shooter, that removes a core layer of skill expression. Combat becomes less about movement and positioning and more about soaking damage and relying on quicksaves.

Instead of dynamic arena fights, encounters often feel like resource checks. If your main weapon runs dry, you switch to something clearly inferior and hope for the best. The result is repetitive combat with limited tactical depth.

Level design also shifts direction. Rather than standalone, tightly designed maps, the game opts for interconnected levels with overarching objectives which is closer in structure to Hexen than its predecessor. While that sounds ambitious, navigation quickly becomes frustrating. Without the enhanced port’s compass system, progression would feel unnecessarily confusing. This causes frequent pauses to check objectives or retrace steps.

[u]Story[/u] 
Unlike the original, this sequel attempts to present an actual narrative. Levels are tied together with ongoing objectives that form a loose plot. That said, calling it a fully realized story would be generous. It feels more like extended mission context than meaningful narrative development which is a step above reading the manual, but not by much.

[u]Aesthetics [/u] 
Technically, the graphics are improved. Enemy models react to damage, and visual effects are more advanced for the time. However, the strict industrial sci-fi setting makes many environments blend together. The original game wasn’t wildly varied either, but it had stronger atmosphere and identity.

The soundtrack is another downgrade. While not terrible, it lacks the distinct mood and memorability of the first game’s OST.

[u]Conclusion/TL;DR [/u] 
This feels like a sequel that misunderstood what made the original work.

Weapons are unbalanced, combat lacks tactical depth due to heavy hitscan enemy design, and level navigation often becomes tedious rather than engaging. While there are technical improvements, they don’t compensate for the loss of what made the first game compelling.

Not recommended, especially if you’re coming off the original expecting improvements instead of regression.

I've not quite finished all the campaigns within Quake II Remastered, but I have finished the main campaign and the new 'Call of the Machine', which were the two main draws for me.

I have a great love for Quake II after playing it in high school. Nowadays it seems to be a bit maligned as a game that's slower and less iconic than the first game, and not as polished or 'perfected' as the third entry (to the point where that game is more favourably renowned despite the singleplayer component being quite lacking). However, Quake II has a ton to offer and it does certain things better than either entry. The Remastered version brings some updates that only improve the situation in my eye.

First off, the atmosphere is still fantastic. It's not as iconic as the first Quake's Lovecraftian setting, but it's still dark, gory and claustrophobic, with great use of ambient sound. A couple of things to mention here - this is id Software's final pixel art game. The textures in this game throughout the levels look utterly gorgeous, provided you play the game [b]without texture filtering[/b]. Quake II also introduced atmospheric and moody coloured lighting. Now, here's where the Remastered version is a definite improvement - you could [b]not[/b] have unfiltered textures and coloured lighting on at the same time in the original release of Quake II, as enabling software rendering locked the textures into filtered mode. Granted the filtered mode probably looked great in the 90s, but it smears over the detail in the textures. Now with the remastered version, you can properly configure the graphics to make the most detailed and best visually dating iteration of Quake 2 yet.

There are also updates to weapon models and level geometry which makes things look a little more detailed. There are notable gameplay updates as well –  here some are starting with my favourites:

[list]
[*]Shots are actually centred on the crosshair now.
[*]Weapon switching is much, much faster now, closer to Quake 3's speed. The original release has quite ponderous animations between switching weapons which slows down the game a fair bit.
[*]There's a quick select wheel. I didn't think I would actually use it, but Quake II has such an expansive array of gadgets and weapons I ended up really loving it.
[*]There's an optional compass function that paints arrows on the floor leading to your next objective. I didn't use this once in the base game, which should attest to the quality of it's design.
[*]The shield mechanic is more fleshed out now, and even enemies can be shielded. Not only this, energy weapons are now vastly more effective against shields, which adds another layer onto weapon switching and target prioritisation. I'm a bit torn on this - there are not that many energy weapons and the Hyperblaster already has the highest DPS in the game. The default Blaster could have been made a [i]bit[/i] more effective against shields though, then it would see more use.
[/list]

When I played Quake II back in high school I was really impressed with how modern it felt. From what I've read it was a big stride forward in terms of FPS level design, in terms of moving away from levels being unconnected sequential hubs. Quake II moves away from the sensation of FPS games consisting of "Level 1.. level 2..." - it has levels that function as miniature hubs that branch off and require you to backtrack. However, any backtracking is integrated utterly perfectly, and the game might be a masterclass on how to effectively utilise and arrange physical space in levels. You'll double up on yourself without realising it and it'll be a surprise every time.

The weaponset in Quake II is absolutely my favourite, in terms of design and utility. The viewmodels of the other two Quake games might as well be tubes, and I was really disappointed going from Quake II to III in this regard. The animations and sound design are really imaginative and fun, especially for the time. Some of the weapons here are the VERY top of the top of the line for the FPS genre. THAT double-barrelled shotgun. THAT minigun. THE BFG10K. But my favourite might be the grenade launcher, which while nerfed compared to the other two games, has some of the most satisfying animation and sound design. The only reason I don't play through the whole game with it is because I run out of ammo. On top of weapons, Quake 2 is replete with gadgets to use as well, which greatly increase the amount of options you have for approaching situations. It just trounces the first game in this regard and I'm a big fan of this design ethos in games personally.

The enemies are fun. The low-level grunts have lots of things going on with animations and AI that give them a lot of character, and their random last-stand shooting animations on death can trip you up. The enemies do seem to get dumber the bigger they are but they're fine.

I've prattled on about details, but newcomers are probably think "Is the original campaign actually any good then?" In my opinion, it's really good, but like in the original release, [b]play it on Nightmare[/b]. Otherwise, it's a bit too boring without the stakes being higher. The game just doesn't really provide quite enough situations where you feel adequately challenged. The prospect of taking more damage forces you to play more carefully and smartly utilise your extensive array of weapons and fun gadgets. Story wise it's just a power fantasy of "bloke saves the day from nasty aliens", which is fine - not many revelations within this reverie. If there are moral themes in this game, it's in the horrors of hostile transhumanism and general cybernetic aesthetic in the levels and enemy design. Story-wise it's a bit mid and there are maybe not enough mind-blowing moments gameplay wise, but it's still very decent, just not quite on the level of something like Half Life (as for Unreal - I can't decide).

Having a spruced up version of the original campaign is great, but another great draw of the appeal in this package is the new [b]Call of the Machine[/b] campaign. This campaign has been very obviously been created by fans with a great reverence for the original game and intrinsic understanding of how Quake II's design.

I am going to be blunt. In terms of gameplay, it absolutely trounces the original Quake II campaign. And frankly, it might be one of the finest official Quake campaigns ever devised, if not the very best. It is STUPIDLY fun. The enemy count feels tripled from the original campaign, leading to a much faster paced, chaotic, and even more tactical experience, as target prioritisation and resource management take precedent. The campaigns are varied and packed full of little surprises and details and new novel tricks - it really helps that it's split into six parts designed by separate authors. It SLAPS Doom Eternal, and the comparative freedom you have with your extensive and generous weapon and gadget array really puts to death the idea that limiting resources and forcing players into illogical and unsatisfying combat loops is stifling and irritating. It's basically everything I wanted that could improve Quake II and it ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ROCKS!

This is the kind of remaster that is dreamt about. I've not even touched on the other campaigns or the multiplayer (which I always thought was underrated next to the other two games). The value in this package is insane and there's a lot here to bring you back compared to firing up a source port. Quake II fans should get it, Quake fans should get it, boomer shooter fans should get it.

As Duke Nukem famously once said in Duke Nukem 3D "I ain't afraid of no Quake!" well...that didn't age so well!

Also included are the levels from Quake 64, making this the ultimate Quake II package for older gamers wanting to revisit their childhood, or newcomers to the classic "boomer shooter" genre who want to see where it all started.

Quake II feels at least one and a half times better than its predecessor. It's still the same corridor-shooter maze, but now it finally has a proper story. Instead of dry text summaries at the end of episodes, the game tries to deliver full cutscenes and clear objective descriptions. The story is simple: you're a marine, cut off from your squad, taking on the Stroggs — the local cybernetic aliens — on your own. Switching from the demons of the original to high-tech and computers really benefits the series: the arsenal has grown two and a half times, and you actually have to use each weapon smartly now.

Of course, for today’s audience (well, I actually turned 20 this year), the gameplay might feel a bit repetitive. The basics haven’t changed: run around, kill monsters, hunt for secrets, and collect bonuses. But the challenges have become visually more interesting: sometimes you have to figure out how to unlock a tricky door, sometimes interact with elevators, sometimes blow something up to progress. The real highlight is the compass on the Q button — seriously, NO JOKE, it’s a genius idea for these kinds of mazes — you can hit it anytime to see the way, so you don’t end up running in circles and screaming at your screen because you got lost.

Graphically, the game feels like a throwback to the late nineties, but thanks to Bethesda and Nightdive Studios, it all looks super clean. They didn’t just re-release the game—they improved the effects and added support for all modern features. Especially nice is that all expansions, The Reckoning and Ground Zero, are included. This really sets Bethesda’s approach apart from Valve, who with Half-Life were always overcomplicating things, selling Opposing Force and Blue Shift separately or putting out sketchy “remasters” like Half-Life: Source. Here, it’s done for the players: you just download and play the full version, no paying multiple times for the same game with different packaging.

Quake II is really a well-balanced and fair shooter. Don’t expect any mind-blowing revelations or crazy emotions that will change your life. But it’s a solid, classic game that’s still fun to play today, maybe while listening to a YouTube video on the side. The developers did a great job, and the game still feels alive nearly 30 years later.

This review is for the remaster rather than the game itself.

This might be Nightdive's best remaster so far, which I like to think was made with the attitude of "OH, YOU PEOPLE DON'T THINK QUAKE II WAS VERY GOOD, DO YOU? WELL TAKE THIS YOU MOTHERS." 

It plays and feels GREAT. More like a 2023 game than a 1997 one now, and I mean that in a good way.  

The new campaign is nifty and merges all the content from the base game, expansions and console ports together, something I wish Nightdive would've done for the Quake I remaster.

The slow weapon switching has been replaced with Quake III-esque weapon switching, making the game more fast-paced and solid in a way that may not necessarily be faithful to Quake II itself, but more to the Quake series as a whole, almost making Quake II fit between Quake I and Quake III Arena a lot easier now. I never had a problem with the weapon switching before but speeding it up was a good move I'd say.

Like with the Quake I remaster, deathmatch now has optional bot support for offline mode... And yes it's a blast. I do hope Nightdive keep adding bot support to their remasters in the future.

Whether this remaster completely obliterates vanilla Quake II on the Yamagi source port I can't really say since I still have a soft spot for vanilla Quake II. The Reckoning and Ground Zero expansions are easier/more balanced in this remaster which is something to keep in mind for those who are likely to get pissed off whilst playing them in their original forms. 

I can take or leave the Berserker's new leap attack. You can avoid taking damage by jumping at the right time, but it's an attack that really takes you aback (hey that rhymed) if you've played vanilla Quake II as much as I have.

So yeah, while vanilla Quake II is still a fine way to play the game, this a wonderfully high-quality package that Nightdive should be very proud of,

Quake II was a radical departure from the first Quake, released a year after it, and gave Id Software their first game that wasn't about alternate dimensions or demons of some kind since Wolfenstein 3-D. This was a big deal to the guys at Id, who had wanted to break away from occult themes after releasing the genre-defining Doom and Doom II in 1993 and 1994 respectively. Quake was meant to be something other than a first person shooter under the original direction of John Romero, who wanted a first-person action RPG with elements of Lovecraftian horror woven in. After a protracted development cycle mostly due to John Carmack wrestling with making a viable fully-3D game engine, Quake still ended up being a shooter.

This, combined with the tacking on of Sci-fi ideas last minute made Romero and co-designer Sandy Peterson furious, and they left Id Software afterward. The rest of the team didn't really sit well with the amalgamated themes they'd created with Quake either, and handed expansions off to other developers while they worked on a new game about hostile alien cyborgs threatening Humanity, using the Quake engine. After trying to think of a name, they ended up just calling it Quake II, and thus here we are. Game mechanics are not all that different from the original Quake other than the fact you are not zooming around like you're on roller skates, and the action is a bit more deliberate with cover playing a part. The weapons are almost entirely different save for the shotgun and super shotgun, and are more numerous.

You play as the soldier simply called Bitterman, with Humanity taking the war to the homeworld of their alien cyborg nemesis, the Strogg. Your drop pod malfunctions, and the result is crash landing deep within unknown territory, spending the rest of the game infiltrating and finding a way to lower the planetary defenses so a larger-scale invasion can begin. Like Quake, you can save anywhere, but unlike Quake, Quake II ditches the hub-based episodic format in favor of linear map design with a bit of branching and backtracking within each area, which consists of three to five interconnected maps you must navigate and explore to finish primary and secondary objectives. Each of these interconnected maps contain secret areas and sometimes entire secret levels too.

Quake II's more-cohesive story told in-game rather than through end-of-episode exposition, novel semi-open level design, and gritty sci-fi theme proved resonant with players and future game developers alike, and the campaign for Quake II is thus considered more memorable. Gameplay balance is also much better, with far less cheap deaths at the hands of enemies with overpowered splash and hit-scan attacks requiring lightning-fast twitch reflexes to avoid and kill, as opposed to the first Quake. which was full of moments that required you to save scum encounters on even easier difficulties due to the instant-death nature of combat. Lest we forget, Quake II also has an extremely impressive musical soundtrack by Sonic Mayhem featuring real guitars, blending heavy metal and industrial beats.

Quake II has always gotten more love from publishers than the original, receiving more ports onto consoles than Quake, and more re-issues and official expansions. The first Quake will always be the darling of the modding and deathmatch community, so much so that even today people are making content for it ranging from levels and episodes to total conversions. Quake II receives nowhere near this kind of community love, except for the people who really want to expand upon the Strogg lore and make fan content that continues the fight against them. There isn't much more I can say, this game is an influential masterpiece of first person shooter design that has aged like fine wine, and remains extremely replayable, especially with modern controller support and Nightdive Studios remastering.

The Ship: Single Player, developed by Outerlight Ltd. and published by Blazing Griffin Ltd., is a stealth-driven first-person experience that places players inside a deadly social game set aboard a 1920s luxury ocean liner. Rather than focusing on traditional run-and-gun combat, it builds its identity around calculated assassination, social navigation, and constant tension. The premise is deceptively simple: you are trapped on a cruise ship under the control of the mysterious Mr. X, forced to eliminate assigned targets while avoiding becoming prey yourself.

The central mechanic revolves around a hunter-and-hunted structure. Each mission assigns you a specific passenger to track and kill, but at the same time, another character is pursuing you. This dynamic creates a layered tension that differentiates it from standard stealth shooters. You must observe routines, study patrol routes, and identify isolated moments to strike. Careless aggression results in suspicion, arrest by ship security, or swift retaliation from your unseen pursuer. Success depends on subtlety and patience rather than reflexes alone.

The ship itself functions as more than just a backdrop; it is an integral part of the gameplay. Its art deco interiors, lounges, cabins, kitchens, and corridors create a believable 1920s environment filled with NPC passengers going about their routines. Learning the layout becomes essential. Hidden corners, blind spots, and quiet rooms offer opportunities for discreet eliminations, while crowded areas increase the risk of detection. Environmental awareness becomes a survival skill as much as weapon proficiency.

Unlike many shooters of its era, The Ship incorporates survival-style meters that add complexity to decision-making. Hunger, sleep, and hygiene levels must be managed throughout missions. Ignoring these needs can impair performance or draw unwanted attention. This system reinforces the idea that you are not simply an assassin but a participant in an elaborate social experiment. Balancing self-preservation with hunting objectives deepens the strategic layer of the experience.

Combat itself is varied but intentionally restrained. Players can use melee weapons, firearms, and improvised tools, but brandishing them openly risks detection. Silent kills are often preferable, rewarding careful planning over brute force. The AI, while sometimes inconsistent by modern standards, provides enough unpredictability to keep missions tense. NPCs react to suspicious behavior, and security personnel patrol key areas, reinforcing the sense that every action carries consequence.

Visually, the game reflects its mid-2000s origins, with character models and animations that feel dated compared to modern titles. However, the period-inspired design gives it a distinct identity. The lighting and architecture contribute to the atmosphere, especially in quieter, dimly lit sections of the ship where tension peaks. The sound design complements this mood with ambient ship noises and subtle cues that heighten awareness during stalking sequences.

The pacing of the single-player campaign is episodic. Each mission presents a contained scenario with specific objectives, creating a rhythm of preparation, execution, and escape. While this structure keeps gameplay focused, it can sometimes feel repetitive once the core loop is understood. The scripted nature of certain encounters may limit replay value compared to the multiplayer mode that originally defined the broader franchise. Nonetheless, within the single-player format, there is satisfaction in executing a flawless, undetected elimination.

Ultimately, The Ship: Single Player stands as a unique experiment within the stealth and FPS genres. Its blend of social stealth, survival management, and assassination mechanics offers a distinct alternative to conventional shooters. Though its age shows in technical aspects, the core concept remains compelling. For players interested in calculated gameplay, atmospheric settings, and unconventional mission design, it delivers a memorable experience that emphasizes cunning over chaos.

Rating: 6/10

What seems to be a swiftly crafted `campaign` on The Ship universe, a game with main focus of hunting your target while being aware of you being someone else`s target, in a multiplayer environment. Since I don`t really care about MP games I tried to give this one a go, while it`s interesting it`s way too short of a campaign with a cliffhanger ending that just doesn`t lead anywhere. I`m all up for a good murder mystery but most objectives here are just you murdering others, there`s no mystery.
The tutorial is crashing out on Windows 11, (but not this...weird), and without it, the game has too many mechanics to understand easily.

The Ship: Single Player is a wild ride of murder, mystery, and old-timey manners. Trapped on a luxury liner with a group of strangers, your job is simple: kill your target before they kill you — all while maintaining your social etiquette. It’s a bizarre blend of stealth, strategy, and dark humor, with plenty of surprises around every corner. If you love quirky games with a unique twist, **The Ship** is a truly unforgettable experience. 🕵️‍♂️🔪🚢
